- [x] Wave 89 (2026-05-28): **0x003F PLANET_POSITIONAL_UPDATE byte-exact 48-byte hardening — sixteenth hardening-pattern wave, fifth space-sector-arm hardening.** Pins the byte-exact 48-byte payload shape of every 0x003F PLANET_POSITIONAL_UPDATE frame emitted during the space-sector login handshake stream. 0x003F is server-emitted by `Player::SendPlanetPositionalUpdate` at `server/src/PlayerConnection.cpp:1264-1283`: builds a stack-local `PlanetPositionalUpdate update`, memsets to zero, copies GameID/TimeStamp/Position[3]/OrbitID/OrbitDist/OrbitAngle/OrbitRate/RotateAngle/RotateRate/TiltAngle, and dispatches via `SendOpcode(ENB_OPCODE_003F_PLANET_POSITIONAL_UPDATE, (unsigned char *) &update, sizeof(update))`. Emit length is determined entirely by the packed struct definition in `common/include/net7/PacketStructures.h:883-895` — verified via standalone g++ -std=c++17 compile of the struct with ATTRIB_PACKED: int32_t GameID (4) + uint32_t TimeStamp (4) + float Position[3] (12) + int32_t OrbitID (4) + 6 × float (OrbitDist/OrbitAngle/OrbitRate/RotateAngle/RotateRate/TiltAngle = 24) = **48 bytes**. Cross-confirmed by the retail PacketStructures.h byte-position comments at lines 885-894 (this[12] GameID through this[56] TiltAngle, +4-byte EnbTcpHeader prefix = payload [0..48)) — these inline byte-offset comments are themselves primary-source corroboration. Call site chain: `SectorManager::SectorLogin2` at SectorManager.cpp:369 calls `m_ObjectMgr->SendAllNavs(player)`; SendAllNavs (ObjectManager.cpp:406-424) iterates m_StaticSectorList and dispatches per OT_PLANET via `obj->SendObject(player)`; base `Object::SendObject` (ObjectClass.cpp) calls SendCreateInfo / SendObjectEffects / SendRelationship / SendPosition / SendAuxDataPacket / SendNavigation / OnCreate; `Planet::SendPosition` override at PlanetClass.cpp:226-231 calls `player->SendPlanetPositionalUpdate(GameID(), PosInfo())` — same SPACE-arm dispatch as 0x0099 NAVIGATION (Wave 88) but routed through the SendPosition member of the Object base virtual chain instead of SendNavigation. The station-sector branch `StationLogin2` does NOT call SendAllNavs, so Wave 89 reuses Wave 53's 2-stage station→space login pattern: stage 1 EstablishAsync at Luna Station (10151) writes the avatar_level_info row; stage 2 ReestablishAsync at Luna space (1015) routes through SectorLogin2 and emits one 48-byte 0x003F frame per planet in the sector. - [x] Wave 86 (2026-05-28): **0x003E ADVANCED_POSITIONAL_UPDATE byte-exact 42-byte hardening — thirteenth hardening-pattern wave.** Pins the byte-exact 42-byte payload shape of every 0x003E ADVANCED_POSITIONAL_UPDATE frame emitted during the station-arm sector-login handshake stream (Bitmask=0 — no conditional-field bits set). 0x003E is server-emitted by `Player::SendAdvancedPositionalUpdate` at `server/src/PlayerConnection.cpp:1317-1393`: builds a stack-local `char packet[sizeof(AdvancedPositionalUpdate)]`, memset's it to zero, packs fields via dual pointer aliases `int32_t *pLong = (int32_t *) &packet[2]` and `float *pFloat = (float *) &packet[2]` — the `int32_t *pLong` stride is load-bearing per Wave 11's inline comment at PlayerConnection.cpp:1322-1325 ("wire slots are 4B each (length is computed as `2 + 4 * index` below). `long *` on Linux strides by 8B, which double-writes/overlaps every slot. int32_t locks the stride to 4B."). Emit size = `int length = 2 + 4 * index` at PlayerConnection.cpp:1384 where `index` counts populated 4-byte slots. Variable-length in principle (up to 9 additional conditional fields per Bitmask bits 0..8), but at handshake time the fresh player's `m_Position_info.Bitmask` is guaranteed to be 0 by `Moveable::Moveable`'s `memset(&m_Position_info, 0, sizeof(m_Position_info))` at Moveable.cpp:12 (no UpdateLocation tick has fired yet to OR in 0x03/0x04/0x07/0x29/0x01 movement-state bits). With Bitmask=0 the mandatory 10 always-written slots emit: 2 (bitmask) + 4 × (object_id + TimeStamp + Position[3] + Orientation[4] + MovementID = 10 slots) = **42 bytes**. Both handshake callers pass the fresh player's `&m_Position_info`: `SendLoginShipData` at PlayerClass.cpp:896 (self-emit) and `SendShipInfo` at PlayerClass.cpp:970 (observer fan-out) — both emit identical 42-byte bodies. Wave 86 is +0 because 0x003E is already counted by Wave 35's positive-presence assertion. **+0 ratchet (0x003E already counted by Wave 35); coverage stays at 104/207 (50.2%).**

With Wave 86 the only remaining Wave 35 fanout opcodes without byte-exact hardening are 0x001B AUX_DATA (variable per ship aux data), 0x0025 ITEM_BASE (variable per item template), 0x0052 LOUNGE_NPC (variable per station), 0x00B4 SUBPARTS (variable per ship subpart roster) — all genuinely variable-length without a load-bearing fresh-player invariant that would fix the size. Next-Phase-K targets after Wave 86: 0x001B AUX_DATA needs framing audit to determine if handshake-time emit has fixed size (likely no — varies per ship asset); 0x00B4 SUBPARTS similarly varies per ship; 0x0007 REMOVE +1 ratchet via object-removal stimulus; 0x0032 DEACTIVATE_RENDER_STATE +1 ratchet; proxy SendResponse sizeof(long) audit; upgrade existing 35 survival-probe assertions to byte-exact; potential follow-up wave to pin handshake-time captured-frame counts (e.g. Assert.Equal(2, count) for 0x0004, Assert.Equal(N, count) for 0x003E, etc.).

- [x] Wave 85 (2026-05-28): **0x0010 DECAL byte-exact 54-byte hardening — twelfth hardening-pattern wave.** Pins the byte-exact 54-byte payload shape of every 0x0010 DECAL frame emitted during the station-arm sector-login handshake stream. 0x0010 is server-emitted by `Player::SendDecal` at `server/src/PlayerConnection.cpp:1162-1185`: builds a stack-local `Decal` struct (PacketStructures.h:455-460), populates GameID + DecalCount, then fills `Item[0..decal_count-1]` with Index/decal_id/HSV/opacity. The emit size is computed as a trailing-array slice `((char *) &decal.Item[decal_count]) - ((char *) &decal)` then dispatched via `SendOpcode(ENB_OPCODE_0010_DECAL, &decal, size)`. Although variable-length in principle (capped at MAX_DECALS=6), every handshake caller passes `decal_count = 2`: `Player::SendLoginShipData` at PlayerClass.cpp:891 (self-emit) and `Player::SendShipInfo` at PlayerClass.cpp:951 (per-observer fan-out emit), both as `SendDecal(GameID(), m_Database.ship_data.decal, 2)`. For decal_count=2: wire size = offsetof(Decal, Item[0]) (= 4 GameID + 2 DecalCount = 6 ATTRIB_PACKED) + 2 × sizeof(DecalItem) (= 2 × (4+4+12+4) = 48) = **54 bytes** ATTRIB_PACKED. ATTRIB_PACKED on Decal is load-bearing: without it the natural-alignment hole between `short DecalCount` (offset 4-5) and `DecalItem.Index` int32 (would align to offset 8) inserts 2 padding bytes → 56 bytes total. The literal `2` at the two call sites is also load-bearing — a revert to MAX_DECALS (6) would emit 6 + 6 × 24 = 150 bytes. Wave 85 is +0 because 0x0010 is already counted by Wave 35's positive-presence assertion. **+0 ratchet (0x0010 already counted by Wave 35); coverage stays at 104/207 (50.2%).**

      Pattern — passive-observation byte-exact hardening (Wave 67/71/76/77/78/79/80/81/82/83/84 pattern). Single-stage station handshake into Luna Station (10151), filter HandshakeFrames for opcode 0x0010, assert at least one captured frame and every captured frame has payload length == 54. Multi-emit Assert.NotEmpty + Assert.All pattern (same as Wave 84). First wave to harden a variable-length-in-principle opcode that is fixed in practice by load-bearing literal call-site arguments — analogous to Wave 83's int32_t narrowing on 0x0005 START but on the caller-arg axis instead of the field-type axis.
      Regression coverage — 9 classes: (a) Decal/DecalItem struct layout drift — GameID int32→long (+4), DecalCount short→int (+2), DecalItem Index/decal_id int32→long (+4 per item × 2 items = +8), HSV/opacity float→double doubles them; (b) ATTRIB_PACKED removal on Decal at PacketStructures.h:460 (+2 padding bytes between DecalCount and Item[0].Index → 56); (c) decal_count literal-replacement at PlayerClass.cpp:891 / :951 (MAX_DECALS=6 → 150 bytes, decal_count=1 → 30 bytes, decal_count=0 → 6 bytes header-only); (d) SendDecal size-arithmetic refactor at PlayerConnection.cpp:1182 from trailing-slice to `sizeof(decal)` (would emit 150 bytes regardless of decal_count including all 6 unused Item slots — catastrophic on the wire); (e) MAX_DECALS clamp removal at PlayerConnection.cpp:1164-1167 (load-bearing guard against buffer over-read if future caller passes decal_count > MAX_DECALS); (f) SendDecal removal from SendLoginShipData chain (Assert.NotEmpty fires); (g) SendOpcode header-width revert; (h) proxy SendClientPacketSequence inner-opcode guard tightening; (i) drain-loop payload-length capture regression.
      Server-integrity POSITIVE. Pure passive-observation tightening of retail wire shape. No client stimulus, no server change. The 54-byte body is exactly what the retail Win32 client's DECAL decoder was compiled to receive for the standard decal_count=2 handshake emit.
      Touches: `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/Opcodes/SectorDecalHardeningTests.cs` (new file), TestAccounts.cs (cli_test80 9_000_080), seed.sql (cli_test80 INSERT), TestedOpcodes.cs (0x0010 amended in-place; MinTestedCount UNCHANGED at 104).
      Notes: Phase K coverage stays at **104/207 (50.2%)** — +0 ratchet, twelfth hardening-pattern wave (67/71/76/77/78/79/80/81/82/83/84/85). **Hardening pattern count now 12 waves**. Wave 85 introduces no server changes. Wave 85 is the FIRST hardening wave to pin a wire shape that depends on a load-bearing call-site literal (the `2` in SendDecal(..., 2) at PlayerClass.cpp:891 and :951) rather than purely on struct-field types — the regression class extends from struct-layout drift (Waves 67-84) to also include the caller-arg space. Next-Phase-K targets after Wave 85: 0x0025 ITEM_BASE (ItemBaseManager.cpp:114 — need to inspect emit-site struct layout); 0x003E ADVANCED_POSITIONAL_UPDATE (PlayerConnection.cpp:1383); 0x0052 LOUNGE_NPC (PlayerConnection.cpp:9721); 0x001B AUX_DATA byte-exact hardening (variable-length — needs framing audit); 0x0044 START_SUBPART (variable-length); 0x002B SET_BBOX; 0x0007 REMOVE +1 ratchet via object-removal stimulus; 0x0032 DEACTIVATE_RENDER_STATE +1 ratchet; proxy SendResponse sizeof(long) audit; upgrade existing 35 survival-probe assertions to byte-exact; potential follow-up Wave to pin the 0x0004 captured-frame count via Assert.Equal(2, count).

- [x] Wave 84 (2026-05-28): **0x0004 CREATE byte-exact 23-byte hardening — eleventh hardening-pattern wave.** Pins the byte-exact 23-byte payload shape of every 0x0004 CREATE frame emitted during the station-arm sector-login handshake stream. 0x0004 is server-emitted by `Player::SendCreate` at `server/src/PlayerConnection.cpp:1505-1529`: constructs a stack-local `Create` struct (PacketStructures.h:366-373), populates `GameID = game_id`, `Scale = scale`, `BaseAsset = asset`, `Type = (char) type`, `HSV[0..2]` from (h,s,v), emits via `SendOpcode(ENB_OPCODE_0004_CREATE, &create, sizeof(create))`. Wire size = `4 (int32_t GameID) + 4 (float Scale) + 2 (short BaseAsset) + 1 (char Type) + 12 (float HSV[3]) = 23` bytes ATTRIB_PACKED. The packed attribute is load-bearing: without it the natural-alignment hole between `Type` (offset 22) and `HSV[0]` (would align to offset 24) inserts 1 padding byte, growing `sizeof(Create)` to 24. 0x0004 is emitted at least twice per station-arm handshake: once for the player's own ship via the `SendLoginShipData` chain (PlayerClass.cpp:857-901), once for the manufacturing-lab pseudo-object via `StationLogin`'s `SendCreate(ManuID, ..., CREATE_MANU_LAB)` at SectorManager.cpp:478 — both ride the same `Player::SendCreate` serialiser and both emit the identical 23-byte body. Wave 84 is +0 because 0x0004 is already counted by Wave 35's positive-presence assertion; Wave 35 would still pass if Create struct layout drifted (GameID int32→long +4 bytes on LP64, BaseAsset short→int +2 bytes, Type char→int +3 bytes, HSV float[3]→double[3] +12 bytes, or ATTRIB_PACKED removal +1 byte). Wave 84 adds the byte-exact 23-byte payload-length assertion. **+0 ratchet (0x0004 already counted); coverage stays at 104/207 (50.2%).**
      Status: done — `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/Opcodes/SectorCreateHardeningTests.cs` new file with `Create_EmittedDuringStationSectorHandshake_HasExactly23BytePayload` (full xmldoc + 8 regression classes + `const int ExpectedCreatePayloadLength = 23` + `Assert.NotEmpty(createFrames)` + `Assert.All(createFrames, f => Assert.Equal(23, f.PayloadLength))`); cli_test79 account row added to TestAccounts.cs + seed.sql; TestedOpcodes.cs 0x0004 entry amended in-place; build green; test passes in 7s — empirically confirms the 23-byte invariant on every captured 0x0004 frame; CoverageRatchet stays at 104/207 (50.2%).
      Pattern — passive-observation byte-exact hardening (Wave 67/71/76/77/78/79/80/81/82/83 pattern). Single-stage station handshake into Luna Station (10151), filter HandshakeFrames for opcode 0x0004, assert at least one captured frame and every captured frame has payload length == 23. Multi-emit invariant pattern (Assert.NotEmpty + Assert.All) — Wave 84 is the FIRST hardening wave to use multi-emit `Assert.NotEmpty` plus `Assert.All`, contrasting with Wave 83's terminator-only `Assert.Single` (0x0005 START is unique terminator).
      Regression coverage — 8 classes: (a) Create struct layout drift — GameID int32→long (+4 bytes on LP64, 27 total), BaseAsset short→int (+2, 25), Type char→int (+3, 26), HSV float[3]→double[3] (+12, 35); (b) ATTRIB_PACKED removal at PacketStructures.h:373 (+1 padding byte between Type and HSV[0], 24 total); (c) sizeof(create) literal-replacement drift at PlayerConnection.cpp:1528; (d) SendCreate removal from SendLoginShipData chain (Assert.NotEmpty fires); (e) StationLogin SendCreate(ManuID, CREATE_MANU_LAB) removal at SectorManager.cpp:478 (count drops to 1 — documented as natural follow-up for a future +1 wave with Assert.Equal(2, count)); (f) SendOpcode header-width revert; (g) proxy SendClientPacketSequence inner-opcode guard tightening; (h) drain-loop payload-length capture regression.
      Server-integrity POSITIVE. Pure passive-observation tightening of retail wire shape. No client stimulus, no server change. The 23-byte body matches the retail Win32 client's CREATE decoder footprint (Win32 sizeof(long)=4, sizeof(short)=2, sizeof(char)=1, sizeof(float)=4 — every field width matches LP64 Linux for these specific types so the only LP64 divergence vector is a future int32_t→long revert on GameID).
      Touches: `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/Opcodes/SectorCreateHardeningTests.cs` (new file), TestAccounts.cs (cli_test79 9_000_079), seed.sql (cli_test79 INSERT), TestedOpcodes.cs (0x0004 amended in-place; MinTestedCount UNCHANGED at 104).
      Notes: Phase K coverage stays at **104/207 (50.2%)** — +0 ratchet, eleventh hardening-pattern wave (67/71/76/77/78/79/80/81/82/83/84). **Hardening pattern count now 11 waves**. Wave 84 introduces no server changes. Wave 84 is the FIRST hardening wave to use the multi-emit `Assert.NotEmpty + Assert.All` pattern (vs Wave 83's `Assert.Single` for the unique terminator, vs Waves 67/71/76/77/78/79/80/81/82's single-emit cases that all happened to admit `Assert.All` over typically 1-2 frames). Next-Phase-K targets after Wave 84: 0x001B AUX_DATA byte-exact hardening (variable-length — needs framing audit); 0x0044 START_SUBPART (variable-length); 0x002B SET_BBOX (4 float = 16 bytes); 0x0007 REMOVE +1 ratchet via object-removal stimulus; 0x0032 DEACTIVATE_RENDER_STATE +1 ratchet; proxy SendResponse sizeof(long) audit; upgrade existing 35 survival-probe assertions to byte-exact; potential follow-up Wave to pin the 0x0004 captured-frame count via Assert.Equal(2, count) (would catch the manu-lab SendCreate removal at SectorManager.cpp:478 specifically).

- [x] Wave 83 (2026-05-28): **0x0005 START byte-exact 4-byte hardening — tenth hardening-pattern wave.** Pins the byte-exact 4-byte payload shape of the single 0x0005 START frame emitted as the terminator of every sector-login handshake. 0x0005 is server-emitted by `Player::SendStart` at `server/src/PlayerConnection.cpp:1079-1089`: narrows the `long start_id` parameter to `int32_t start_id_wire = (int32_t) start_id` (load-bearing narrowing on LP64 Linux, documented inline at PlayerConnection.cpp:1083-1086 — passing `&start_id` with `sizeof(start_id)` directly would emit 8 bytes), then emits via `SendOpcode(ENB_OPCODE_0005_START, &start_id_wire, sizeof(start_id_wire))`. Wire size = `sizeof(int32_t) = 4` bytes — exactly what the retail Win32 client's START decoder was compiled to receive. Wave 83 is +0 because 0x0005 is already counted by Wave 7's StartAck test entry (the server-emitted 0x0005 is an implicit precondition of Wave 7's client-side 0x0006 START_ACK exercise). Wave 83 asserts `Assert.Single(startFrames)` (the terminator-only invariant) plus `Assert.Equal(4, startFrames[0].PayloadLength)`. **+0 ratchet (0x0005 already counted by Wave 7); coverage stays at 104/207 (50.2%).**

- [x] Wave 75 (2026-05-28): **0x0003 LOGOFF +1 ratchet via duplicate-login stimulus — crosses 50% Phase K coverage milestone.** First-time coverage of 0x0003 LOGOFF, distinct from 0x00BA LOGOFF_CONFIRMATION already covered by Wave 31. 0x0003 LOGOFF is server-originated via `Player::ForceLogout` at `server/src/PlayerConnection.cpp:7969-7998` which emits `SendOpcode(ENB_OPCODE_0003_LOGOFF, &GameIDD, sizeof(GameIDD))` where `GameIDD` is `int32_t` (Wave 69 server-tightening — was `long` pre-Wave 69, would have emitted 8 bytes on LP64 Linux). The duplicate-login stimulus opens a second concurrent global TCP, sends GlobalConnect with the same account credentials, the server's `UDP_Connection::HandleGlobalConnect` at `server/src/UDP_Global.cpp:136` calls `PlayerManager::CheckAccountInUse` at `server/src/PlayerManager.cpp:396-419`, which iterates `m_GlobalPlayerList`, matches by `strcasecmp(AccountUsername, username)`, sees `Active()==true` (Session A has settled into `StationLogin2:527 SetActive(true)`), calls `Dialog + ForceLogout + DropPlayerFromGalaxy`, and ForceLogout emits the 4-byte 0x0003 frame on Session A's per-Player UDP queue, calls `SendPacketCache` to flush synchronously, then sleeps 100ms before the player teardown. **+1 ratchet: coverage 103/207 → 104/207 = 50.2% — crosses 50% milestone.**
      Status: done — `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/Opcodes/SectorForceLogoutTests.cs` new file with `ForceLogout_TriggeredByDuplicateGlobalConnect_EmitsLogoffWithExactly4BytePayload` (full xmldoc + 8 regression classes + `Assert.Equal(4, reply.Payload.Length)`); cli_test70 account row added to TestAccounts.cs + seed.sql; TestedOpcodes.cs adds 0x0003 LOGOFF entry sorted between 0x0002 LOGIN and 0x0004 CREATE, bumps MinTestedCount 103→104; build green; test passes in 7s; CoverageRatchet tests confirm `[coverage] 104/207 opcodes have round-trip integration coverage (50.2%)`.
      Pattern — direct-stimulus +1 ratchet. The duplicate-login pattern is novel for Phase K — first wave to use a server-initiated emit triggered by an indirect client stimulus (a second concurrent connection rather than a direct opcode send). The stimulus path is exactly what the retail server did when the same account credentials connected a second time (kicks the first session with a 0x0003 LOGOFF); the retail Win32 client interprets it as "you-have-been-disconnected-by-another-login" and pops the disconnect dialog. The cleanup re-authenticates with a fresh ticket (the account is no longer "in use" by the time cleanup runs) and deletes the slot.
      Regression coverage — 8 classes documented inline in test xmldoc and TestedOpcodes commentary: (a) ForceLogout GameIDD parameter-type revert at PlayerConnection.cpp:7988 (long → 8 bytes on LP64 Linux re-introduces Wave 69 bug); (b) CheckAccountInUse Active() guard removal at PlayerManager.cpp:406 (race condition where ForceLogout fires mid-handshake before proxy routing is set up); (c) CheckAccountInUse strcasecmp → strcmp regression at PlayerManager.cpp:404 (silently breaks duplicate-login protection in production); (d) HandleGlobalConnect dispatch flip at UDP_Global.cpp:136 (removing CheckAccountInUse or moving it past the SendAvatarList emit at line 173 means Session A never receives 0x0003); (e) ForceLogout SendPacketCache removal at PlayerConnection.cpp:7991 (frame sits in queue past teardown — test times out); (f) ForceLogout usleep(100ms) removal at PlayerConnection.cpp:7992 (race where proxy may not complete forward before TCP teardown — flaky test); (g) proxy SendClientPacketSequence inner-opcode guard at proxy/UDPProxyToClient_linux.cpp:568 tightening (currently passes 0x0003 because 0x0003 < 0x0FFF; a regression to opcode >= 0x0004 would silently drop 0x0003); (h) SendOpcode header-width revert at PlayerConnection.cpp:127 (mis-decode opcodes in 0x2016 PACKET_SEQUENCE parser).

- [x] Wave 72 (2026-05-28): **PROXY TIGHTENING — bulk LP32/LP64 wire-shape fix via `proxy/PacketMethods.h` `AddData<long>` + `AddData<unsigned long>` template specializations.** Mirror of the server-side specialization landed in Phase K Wave 12 at `server/src/PacketMethods.h:38-49`. Single 22-line edit fixes the entire proxy-side LP64 emit-width divergence for the `long`/`unsigned long` specialisations of the generic `AddData<T>(packet, mydata, index)` template that emits exactly `sizeof(T)` wire bytes. Bulk approach explicitly requested by the user after observing roughly fifty separate find-then-convert `long → int32_t` waves and calling that "a massive waste of tokens". **+0 ratchet (existing handshake/sector tests cover the affected emit sites in aggregate); coverage stays at 103/207 (49.8%). First proxy-side mass-tightening wave in Phase K (Wave 69 was the server-side counterpart).**
      Status: done — `proxy/PacketMethods.h` edited (specializations added immediately after the primary `AddData<T>` template at line 11); `docker compose build proxy` clean; `docker compose up -d --wait` all containers Healthy; full xUnit suite (101 tests) re-run with all 100 still passing — the one failure (`SectorInventoryMoveTests.InventoryMove_UnrecognisedFromInv_ReceivesUnrecognisedErrorString`) is a known TCP-read timeout flake on the login/sector path, re-ran in isolation and passed in 7s.
      Bug-class citation. Wave 72 fixes a **real LP64 stack buffer overflow** at two proxy call sites, not a pure hygiene tightening. `proxy/UDPProxyToGlobal.cpp:208` in `SendMasterLogin` allocates `unsigned char data[8]` on the stack, writes `AddData(data, sector_id, index)` (with `long sector_id`) which on Linux LP64 advances `index` by `sizeof(long) = 8` consuming the entire buffer, then conditionally writes `AddData(data, (u8)1, index)` at `index == 8` — a one-byte write past the end of the stack buffer when `g_Packet_Opt_requested` is true. `proxy/UDPClient_linux.cpp:647` has the identical pattern with the same 8-byte buffer and the same conditional overflow byte. On Win32 (retail) `sizeof(long) == 4` so the sector_id consumes bytes 0..3 and the optional `(u8)1` fits at byte 4, exactly the wire shape the retail master server was compiled to parse. Pre-Wave-72, the proxy on Linux was emitting 12 effective bytes from an 8-byte buffer (sector_id 8 bytes + optional `1` byte stomping caller stack), divergent both ways: the master-side parser reads an 8-byte sector_id and 4 extra bytes of garbage where the optional byte should be (silent retail-fidelity bug); the proxy itself smashes its own stack on every `g_Packet_Opt_requested == true` path (silent crash hazard depending on what the caller pushed at `data[-1]`). Both issues vanish with the specializations: the `AddData<long>` specialisation forces a 4-byte emission via `*((int32_t *) &packet[index]) = (int32_t) mydata; index += 4;` matching Win32 retail wire shape exactly and leaving 4 free bytes for the optional `(u8)1`.
      Why a template specialisation and not edit each call site. The generic primary template `AddData<T>(unsigned char *packet, T mydata, int &index)` writes `sizeof(T)` bytes — correct for `short`, `int`, `int32_t`, `float`, `char` and every fixed-width type. Only `long` and `unsigned long` have the LP32/LP64 width-divergence problem; specialising those two cases preserves the primary template's intentional sizeof-equals-emit semantics for every other type and locks the Win32 4-byte wire shape for the two divergent types in one place. The server-side `PacketMethods.h` landed this exact pattern in Wave 12 (Phase K) for the same reason and has been load-bearing across every server-emit site since. The user's exact directive — "ive seen you separately find a bug then convert long to int32_t almost 50 times now, seems like a massive waste of tokens" — is satisfied by this approach: future `AddData(packet, long_var, index)` call sites in proxy code don't need to be individually rewritten to `int32_t`, the specialisation does the right thing on every platform.
      Affected proxy call sites (from `grep -nE 'AddData\(.*,\s*(long|unsigned long)\s+\w+' proxy/*.cpp` and broader grep of locals: `proxy/UDPProxyToGlobal.cpp:208` (SendMasterLogin sector_id — stack overflow path, primary bug); `proxy/UDPClient_linux.cpp:647` (parallel SendMasterLogin in the linux-only variant — same stack overflow path); `proxy/ClientToGlobalServer.cpp:236` (SendGlobalTicket level field — benign because `index` is hardcoded reset to 48 immediately afterwards, but architecturally wrong); and the remaining ~10 sites identified in the Wave 69 server-side audit grep (`proxy/ClientToSectorServer.cpp:255/702/707/716`, `proxy/UDPProxyToGlobal.cpp:388/453/472/505`) which all flow through `Connection::SendResponse` and now emit byte-exact retail wire shapes. The broader proxy `Connection::SendResponse` `sizeof(long)` framing-header issue Wave 69 deferred is a separate matter — that one is the **framing header** itself using `sizeof(long)`, not an `AddData<long>` call, so Wave 72 doesn't reach it. Framing-header remediation remains a follow-up wave.
      Server-integrity citation (per CLAUDE.md "Server integrity rules"). Per the rule, server-integrity-positive tightenings toward retail wire fidelity are explicitly welcomed; this is a proxy-side equivalent of that pattern. The retail Win32 proxy-and-client pair compiled `long` as 4 bytes; our Linux build was emitting 8 bytes; Wave 72 restores byte-exact agreement with the wire shape the retail master server was compiled to parse. Concretely: pre-Wave-72 the proxy `SendMasterLogin` emitted `<sector_id:8>` (and stack-overflowed on optional-byte write); post-Wave-72 it emits `<sector_id:4>` (and `<sector_id:4><1:1>` cleanly when `g_Packet_Opt_requested == true`). No relaxation, no widened input acceptance, no fabricated replies — pure wire-shape correction.
      Primary-source citation. Mirror of `server/src/PacketMethods.h:38-49` (Phase K Wave 12), which landed the identical pair of specialisations on the server side. The server-side pattern has been load-bearing since Wave 12 across every server `Send*` chain that calls `AddData(packet, long_var, index)` — including the entire StationLogin/SectorLogin handshake. Wave 72 ports the proven pattern to the proxy. The retail Win32 binary compiled the same `PacketMethods.h` template against LP32 longs and emitted 4 bytes per `AddData<long>` call; Wave 12 (server) and Wave 72 (proxy) together preserve that retail wire shape on LP64 Linux.
      Regression coverage — passive: every existing test that exercises a station→space handshake or a sector connection hits at least one of the `AddData<long>` chain sites; the Wave 68/Wave 71 `HandshakeFrames` payload-length capture path would surface any 4→8 byte-shift regression as an off-by-N payload-length assertion failure across multiple frames at once. A future dedicated hardening test could send the `g_Packet_Opt_requested == true` SendMasterLogin path and assert the exact 5-byte body via packet capture instrumentation; the 8-byte body that the pre-Wave-72 LP64 build emitted would also be caught by the parser-rejects-malformed-frame side of any master-server-side wire validation. Wave 72 leaves the regression net to existing coverage; the broad-sweep nature of the fix (specialisation in one place lights up every call site) makes adding per-site tests low-yield versus the +0 ratchet cost.
      Seam-discovery — Wave 72 is the **second mass-tightening wave in Phase K** after Wave 69's server-side `Send*(long)` audit, and the **first proxy-side mass-tightening wave**. Together Waves 12 (server, `PacketMethods.h`) + 69 (server, `Send*(long)` parameter declarations) + 72 (proxy, `PacketMethods.h`) close out the three principal categories of LP32/LP64 emit-width divergence in the codebase. The proxy `Connection::SendResponse` framing header at `proxy/Connection.cpp:606-635` is the **only remaining deferred** category — it uses `sizeof(long)` for the inter-process framing header offset, which is internally LP64-consistent (both endpoints are our code) but diverges from retail-client framing; remediation requires coordinated changes on both proxy and any clients that consume that framing.
      Touches: `proxy/PacketMethods.h` (added 22 lines: explanatory comment block + `AddData<long>` specialisation + `AddData<unsigned long>` specialisation, immediately after the primary `AddData<T>` template at line 11, mirroring the server-side specialisations at `server/src/PacketMethods.h:38-49`). Single-file edit.
      Notes: Phase K coverage stays at **103/207 (49.8%)** — Wave 72 is +0 ratchet, mass proxy-side tightening. Direct-reply/survival-probe pattern count unchanged at 35 waves; passive-observation pattern count unchanged at 5 waves; hardening pattern count unchanged at 3 waves; proxy-fidelity-fix pattern count now **2 waves** (Wave 70 — launcher-opcode silent-drop; Wave 72 — `AddData<long>` specialisation). Wave 72 is the **first proxy-side wave to mirror a known-good server-side pattern landed in an earlier wave** (Wave 12); the audit methodology applied to identify the gap was a simple grep `grep -nE 'AddData\(' proxy/PacketMethods.h server/src/PacketMethods.h | head` revealing the server file had the specialisations and the proxy file did not. Next-Phase-K targets after Wave 72: dedicated hardening tests for the remaining three Wave 69 server tightening sites (0x0007 REMOVE — object-removal stimulus; 0x0032 DEACTIVATE_RENDER_STATE — render-state toggle; 0x0003 LOGOFF — body-length extension of the existing LogoffRequest test); proxy `Connection::SendResponse` `sizeof(long)` framing-header audit (the last remaining LP64 category); 0x004F STARBASE_SET negative-assertion wave; further +1 ratchets from the remaining ~104/207 untested opcodes.

Pins the Wave 69 LP32/LP64 tightening invariant for `Player::SendClientType` at `server/src/PlayerConnection.cpp:1071-1075`. **Coverage stays at 103/207 (49.8%).**

      Server-integrity citation (per CLAUDE.md "Server integrity rules"). Identical rationale to Wave 67/68 — depth-coverage hardening of an existing Wave 69 tightening. 0x003C is server-emitted by `Player::SendClientType` at `PlayerConnection.cpp:1071-1075`. Before Wave 69 the parameter was declared `long`; on Win32 (LP32) `sizeof(long)` evaluated to 4 (the wire shape the retail client was compiled to receive); on Linux LP64 it silently inflated to 8. Wave 71's hardening test locks that invariant in place via a passive 4-byte payload-length assertion.
      Pattern — third hardening-test wave (after Wave 67's 0x0096 byte-exact echo and Wave 68's 0x007F passive 4B payload). Uses the Wave 68-added `SectorHandshake.Session.HandshakeFrames` capture path: filter HandshakeFrames for opcode 0x003C and assert `Assert.NotEmpty + Assert.All(payloadLength == 4)`. Reuses Wave 52's 2-stage station→space handshake pattern because 0x003C only emits in the **space** branch of HandleSectorLogin — stage 1 EstablishAsync at Luna Station 10151 creates the avatar via `ReInitializeSavedData`; explicit `0x00B9 LOGOFF_REQUEST` → `0x00BA LOGOFF_CONFIRMATION` round-trip forces synchronous DropPlayerFromGalaxy (avoids G_ERROR_ACCOUNT_IN_USE); stage 2 ReestablishAsync at sector 1015 (Luna space) takes the ReloadSavedData path which preserves the space sector_num and routes through `SectorLogin` at SectorManager.cpp:347 → `player->SendClientType(m_SectorData->sector_type)`.

Assertions: `Assert.NotEmpty(clientTypeFrames)` (frame must appear at least once) + `Assert.All(clientTypeFrames, f => Assert.Equal(4, f.PayloadLength))` (every captured frame must be exactly 4 bytes). Cleanup runs standard reconnect + `GlobalDeleteCharacter` in `finally` block via best-effort 30s CTS.

- [x] Wave 70 (2026-05-28): **0x0098 GALAXY_MAP_REQUEST direct-stimulus + 0x0044 REQUEST_TIME survival probe + co-landed proxy-side launcher-opcode stall fix.** First +1 ratchet after the Wave 67–69 tightening trilogy and the first wave in Phase K to combine a coverage +1 with a proxy-side correctness fix in the same commit. **+1 ratchet: 102/207 → 103/207 (49.8%).**
      Status: done — proxy image rebuilt (`docker compose build proxy`) with `HandleCustomOpcode` returning TRUE for the launcher-side opcode set (0x2011, 0x2012-0x2014, 0x2018-0x2019); .NET test rebuilt; Wave 70 test passes in 7s post-fix.
      Server-side analysis. 0x0098 GALAXY_MAP_REQUEST is dispatched at `server/src/PlayerConnection.cpp:567-569` (case arm calls `HandleGalaxyMapRequest()` parameter-less; the dispatcher reads `HandleClientOpcode(short opcode, short bytes, unsigned char *data)` but the case arm passes nothing — the handler signature has no payload pointer). The handler at `server/src/PlayerConnection.cpp:10715-10720` is literally one effective line: `SendOpcode(ENB_OPCODE_2011_GALAXY_MAP_CACHE, 0, 0)`. No state mutation, no fan-out, no DB write — the purest one-liner emit handler in the 0x0090-0x009F dispatch range. The dispatcher case arm sits between 0x008D INCAPACITANCE_REQUEST (line 563; Wave 29-abandoned target — global NPC template mutation crashes the server) and 0x009B WARP (line 571; heavy state mutation).
      First-attempt FAILURE diagnosed a real proxy stall. The Wave 70 test was originally written as "send 0x0098 → server emits 0x2011 → proxy silently drops 0x2011 → send 0x0044 REQUEST_TIME → server echoes 0x0034 CLIENT_SET_TIME → assert sentinel tick echoes verbatim." The first run timed out at 90s in `EncryptedTcpConnection.ReceiveAsync` — no frames came back at all. Root cause analysis: the proxy `SendClientPacketSequence` walker at `proxy/UDPProxyToClient_linux.cpp:531-585` evaluates each inner opcode in a server→client packet sequence: if `HandleCustomOpcode` returns FALSE, it falls through to a `0x0000 < opcode < 0x0FFF` guard that gates raw-forward. 0x2011 = 8209 fails that bound and hits the bottom-of-switch `else` arm which logs "bad opcode through to proxy" and sets `terminate = true`. The function returns `!terminate` = false. The caller at lines 399/412 marks the packet `PACKET_BLANK` (instead of `PACKET_DONE`), `m_CurrentPacketNum` never advances. Subsequent server replies (including the REQUEST_TIME echo) queue at higher sequence numbers behind the stuck slot — the proxy keeps requesting resends of the stuck slot, the server keeps sending the same 0x2011, the loop never breaks. The 0x0034 reply we needed is sitting in a packet that the proxy never gets around to forwarding because the prior packet is permanently `PACKET_BLANK`. Test cancellation token fires at 90s with no frames received.
      Proxy-side fix: server-integrity-NEUTRAL launcher-opcode silent-drop. `HandleCustomOpcode` at `proxy/UDPProxyToClient_linux.cpp:480-518` listed the launcher-side opcode set (0x2011 GALAXY_MAP_CACHE, 0x2012-0x2014 PROSPECT/TRACTOR/LOOT, 0x2018-0x2019 STATIC/RESOURCE_OBJECT_CREATE) but returned FALSE, with a comment that wrongly claimed the fall-through path would "forward the raw opcode for clients that handle it themselves" — the comment was a misread of how the < 0x0FFF guard interacts with terminate=true. Fix is a single-token swap: `return false` → `return true` for the launcher-opcode arm, plus a longer explanatory comment that pins the actual semantics (handled-and-dropped, walker advances). Per CLAUDE.md server-integrity rules this is server-integrity-NEUTRAL: the server emits the same exact 0x2011 bytes pre- and post-fix; what changes is whether the proxy stalls or silently drops on opcodes it always intended to drop. Primary-source citation for the silent-drop being preservation-faithful: the Win32 retail proxy at `proxy/UDPProxyToClient.cpp:370` consumes 0x2011 locally via `SendCachedGalaxyMap()` + `m_UDPClient->SetReceivedGalaxyMap()`; the Win32 launcher-side path at `proxy/UDPClient.cpp:263` similarly consumes 0x2011 via `SendCachedGalaxyMap()`. Neither retail path forwards 0x2011 over the game-protocol TCP channel to the game receiver — 0x2011 is a launcher-side opcode by design. Our Linux server-side proxy doesn't ship the launcher hookup; silent-drop matches retail's net effect on the game channel (game receiver never sees 0x2011 either way).
      Wave 70 test design. Survival-probe pattern (the 34th wave to use it). Stimulus: empty-body 0x0098 on the post-handshake sector connection. Survival assertion: send 0x0044 REQUEST_TIME with sentinel tick = `unchecked((int)(DateTime.UtcNow.Ticks & 0x7FFFFFFF))`, drain up to 400 frames tolerating positional-update interleavings, when 0x0034 CLIENT_SET_TIME appears assert `payload.Length == 12` and `BinaryPrimitives.ReadInt32LittleEndian(payload[..4]) == clientTick`. Pre-fix this test never received any frame after the 0x0098 stimulus because of the proxy stall described above. Post-fix the test passes in 7 seconds (handshake ~2s + 0x0098 stimulus + REQUEST_TIME round-trip is sub-second).
      Regression coverage — 5 classes documented inline in test xmldoc: (a) Dispatcher mis-route at `PlayerConnection.cpp:567-569` — a copy-paste swap with the adjacent 0x008D INCAPACITANCE_REQUEST arm on our empty payload would drive HandleIncapacitanceRequest's `long player = *data` read past byte 0 of the 0-byte buffer (UB); a swap with the 0x009B WARP arm would over-read for the Warp struct cast. Survival via REQUEST_TIME catches the crash boundary. (b) HandleGalaxyMapRequest one-liner regression at `PlayerConnection.cpp:10715-10720` — a refactor that swaps in a `SendDataFileToClient("GalaxyMap.dat")` call (hinted at by the commented-out line 10719) could attempt fopen() of a file absent from the test env, NULL-deref the fopen failure path, crash the connection. Survival catches. (c) SendOpcode header-width revert at `server/src/PlayerConnection.cpp:127` — would corrupt every inner opcode in the 0x2016 PACKET_SEQUENCE parser; REQUEST_TIME echo silents and the test times out. Same shape as Waves 11/27/29/30/36/37/38/39/40/41/44. (d) Proxy default-case ForwardClientOpcode dropping 0x0098 — 0x0098 has no entry in `proxy/ClientToServer_linux_stubs.cpp` and falls through to the bottom-of-switch default forward arm. A regression that drops the default arm would silently lose both 0x0098 and 0x0044 → REQUEST_TIME echo silents → test times out. (e) Proxy `SendClientPacketSequence` launcher-opcode regression — if a future edit reverts `HandleCustomOpcode` to return false for 0x2011 (or for any of 0x2012-0x2014, 0x2018-0x2019), the same packet-sequence stall returns and the survival probe times out exactly the way the first attempt did. This is now a load-bearing regression guard for the proxy fidelity fix Wave 70 itself landed.
      Pattern catalogue update. Wave 70 is the **first wave in Phase K to co-land a proxy-side correctness fix with a coverage +1**. Coverage-only waves modify server-integrity-neutral test scaffolding; tightening waves (67/68/69) modify server-emit wire shapes; Wave 70 introduces a third recipe — proxy stall classes that block opcode coverage can be diagnosed by the first-attempt timeout and unblocked by a server-integrity-neutral proxy edit in the same wave. Future candidates for the same recipe: any other proxy bottom-of-switch guard that drops legitimate server emits without forwarding (audit candidates: outbound `ProcessClientOpcode` default arm at `proxy/UDPClient.cpp:287-289`; inbound proxy → client framed dispatch at `proxy/UDPProxyToClient.cpp` HandleCustomOpcode default arm; proxy `Connection::SendResponse` LP64 framing header — the broader-scope Wave 69-deferred issue).
      Coverage citation refresh. `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/Coverage/TestedOpcodes.cs` `MinTestedCount` ratcheted **102 → 103**. New entry inserted between the existing 0x0097 GALAXY_MAP and 0x0099 NAVIGATION rows: `0x0098 GALAXY_MAP_REQUEST` cited to `SectorGalaxyMapRequestTests.GalaxyMapRequest_OnFreshSession_DoesNotBreakConnection_RequestTimeStillRoundTrips`. Pattern label "Survival probe via REQUEST_TIME echo, with co-landed proxy-side launcher-opcode stall fix."
      Touches: `proxy/UDPProxyToClient_linux.cpp` (HandleCustomOpcode launcher-opcode arm: `return false` → `return true` plus longer explanatory comment pinning the actual semantics and citing the Wave 70 first-attempt failure mode); `tools/cli-client/src/CliClient.Core/Opcodes/OpcodeId.cs` (+0x0098 GalaxyMapRequest between 0x0089 Relationship and 0x0099 Navigation in the wire-typed registry); NEW `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/Opcodes/SectorGalaxyMapRequestTests.cs` (full xmldoc with 5 regression classes + the proxy stall + fix narrative); `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/TestAccounts.cs` (`GalaxyMapRequest_OnFreshSession_DoesNotBreakConnection_RequestTimeStillRoundTrips` → cli_test66 9_000_066); `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/Fixtures/seed.sql` (cli_test66 INSERT row, status=100); `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/Coverage/TestedOpcodes.cs` (MinTestedCount 102 → **103**; new 0x0098 GALAXY_MAP_REQUEST sorted-position entry with full citation).
      Notes: Phase K coverage **102/207 → 103/207 (49.8%)** — first +1 ratchet after Wave 66 (Waves 67/68/69 were all +0 tightenings). Direct-reply/survival-probe pattern count now **35 waves**; passive-observation pattern count unchanged at 5 waves; hardening pattern count unchanged at 2 waves (Wave 67, Wave 68); proxy-fidelity-fix pattern count now **1 wave** (Wave 70). Next-Phase-K targets after Wave 70: dedicated 4-byte payload-length hardening tests for the four Wave 69 wire-shape tightening sites (0x003C, 0x0007, 0x0032, 0x0003); proxy `Connection::SendResponse` `sizeof(long)` framing-header audit (broader-scope LP64 fidelity issue, deferred from Wave 69); 0x004F STARBASE_SET negative-assertion wave; further +1 ratchets from the remaining ~104/207 untested opcodes, including the heavier-state opcodes that were previously blocked behind the same proxy stall class (0x2012-0x2014 PROSPECT/TRACTOR/LOOT now have the same survival-probe path unblocked by the Wave 70 proxy fix, though their server handlers have other state-mutation hazards that need triage before they're safe to direct-stimulus).

- [x] Wave 69 (2026-05-28): **SERVER TIGHTENING — MASS LP32/LP64 WIRE-SHAPE AUDIT** — bulk-tightening of seven `long`-typed server-emit parameters across `Player::Send*` methods that all share the Wave 67/68 LP64 sizeof(long) regression class. Triggered by user feedback that we'd been doing this fix one wave at a time across 50+ separate find-and-fix cycles. **+0 ratchet (all affected opcodes already covered by passive-observation tests); coverage stays at 102/207 (49.3%). Second mass tightening commit in Phase K.**
      Status: done — `server/src/PlayerClass.h` declarations + `server/src/PlayerConnection.cpp` definitions modified in lock-step for six entry points: `SendClientType(long → int32_t)` (opcode 0x003C — wire-shape body bug), `SendActivateRenderState(long, unsigned long → int32_t, uint32_t)` (opcode 0x0030 — parameter hygiene; struct fields already int32_t/uint32_t), `SendInitRenderState(long, unsigned long → int32_t, uint32_t)` (opcode 0x002F — hygiene), `SendActivateNextRenderState(long, unsigned long → int32_t, uint32_t)` (opcode 0x0031 — hygiene), `SendDeactivateRenderState(long → int32_t)` (opcode 0x0032 — wire-shape body bug), `RemoveObject(long → int32_t)` (opcode 0x0007 — wire-shape body bug). Plus one local-variable tightening: `long GameIDD = GameID()` → `int32_t GameIDD = GameID()` in the LOGOFF emit chain (opcode 0x0003 — wire-shape body bug). Total **4 wire-shape body bugs + 3 parameter-hygiene cleanups** in one wave. Server image rebuilt; broad regression sweep (19-test cohort spanning handshake fanouts, Wave 68 hardening, LogoffRequest, FullSectorLogin, Recustomize) re-run with all assertions clean; one transient flake re-run in isolation and passed.
      Server-integrity citation (per CLAUDE.md "Server integrity rules"). Identical rationale to Wave 67/68: the CLAUDE.md rule explicitly welcomes tightenings toward retail wire fidelity. Concretely: each of the four wire-shape bugs (0x003C, 0x0032, 0x0007, 0x0003) was a `void func(long X) { SendOpcode(opcode, &X, sizeof(X)); }` pattern where the local-variable width directly determines the payload width. On Win32 (retail, LP32) `sizeof(long)` = 4, matching the retail client's expected 4-byte body. On Linux LP64 our build was emitting 8 bytes — silent wire-shape divergence away from the format the real client was compiled to receive. Single-token swaps (`long` → `int32_t`) lock each payload to 4 bytes on every platform. No widened input acceptance, no loosened gating, no relaxed posture, no fabricated replies — just wire-shape corrections. The three hygiene tightenings (0x0030/0x002F/0x0031) don't change wire bytes (the struct fields were already int32_t/uint32_t) but tighten the API to match, eliminating silent implicit narrowing at call sites and preventing future re-introduction of the same bug class.
      Triage methodology — the audit grep that produced this list was: enumerate every `Send*\(...sizeof\(([a-zA-Z_]+)\)\)` call site in server-native code (server/src/, login-server/, proxy/, excluding third_party/), then for each one check whether the referenced local variable is declared `long` within the surrounding 40 lines. This caught all four Wave 69 wire-shape bugs in PlayerConnection.cpp plus parallel candidates in MasterServerToSectorServer.cpp (master→sector internal, both LP64 — consistent on the wire so not a retail-fidelity regression), login-server/Net7SSL/UDPClient.cpp (UDP framing, separate audit), and ~10 proxy sites (proxy/ClientToSectorServer.cpp:255/702/707/716, proxy/ClientToGlobalServer.cpp:120, proxy/UDPProxyToGlobal.cpp:388/453/472/505). The proxy candidates ride through `Connection::SendResponse` (proxy/Connection.cpp:606-635) which itself uses `sizeof(long)` for the inter-process **framing header** (offset bytes 4..7 vs 4..3 on LP32 → 4 garbage bytes between header and body on LP64). The proxy framing is internally LP64-consistent (both endpoints are our code) but diverges from retail-client framing; remediating that is a separate larger effort because it requires coordinated changes on both proxy and any clients that consume that framing — deferred to a follow-up phase. Server-side `Send*` body bugs are the localized portion that can be safely tightened in isolation.
      Regression coverage — covered by existing passive-presence tests: 0x003C is asserted to be present in the space-sector handshake by `HandshakeEmitsClientTypeAndGalaxyMapOnSpaceSectorLogin` (Wave 49). 0x0007/0x0030/0x002F/0x0031/0x0032 are not directly under passive-presence assertions today; their preservation-fidelity invariants are protected indirectly via the connection-survival assertions in any test that triggers `RemoveObject` or `SendDeactivateRenderState` (a regression that breaks the wire shape would propagate to an immediate frame-parse error in the test client). 0x0003 LOGOFF body shape is not asserted directly (the existing LogoffRequest test asserts the 0x00BA LOGOFF_CONFIRMATION body shape, a separate downstream opcode). Future +0 hardening waves can add byte-exact length assertions on each of these.
      Seam-discovery — Wave 69 is the **second server-tightening wave in Phase K** (after Wave 67) and the **first to bulk-update multiple wire-emit sites in one commit**. The audit pattern (grep `Send*\(...sizeof\(varname\)\)` cross-referenced with `long varname` declarations) is reusable for future LP32/LP64 wire-shape sweeps. Outstanding LP64 divergence categories the same audit identified for follow-up: (1) the proxy `Connection::SendResponse` `sizeof(long)` framing header (broader-scope retail-fidelity issue, deferred); (2) `long X = ntohl(...)` decode patterns where X flows into a wire emit (separate Wave 69 audit found ~33 of these across server/, proxy/, login-server/ — most are decode-locals that DON'T re-flow to a wire emit so harmless, but the ones that do need individual triage); (3) packed-struct `long` fields in PacketStructures.h (a previous audit migrated most of these — a follow-up grep confirms residuals are zero in common/include/net7/PacketStructures.h's exported wire structs).
      Touches: `server/src/PlayerClass.h` (6 decls: SendClientType, SendActivateRenderState, SendInitRenderState, SendActivateNextRenderState, SendDeactivateRenderState, RemoveObject — `long` → `int32_t` / `unsigned long` → `uint32_t`), `server/src/PlayerConnection.cpp` (6 defs at the matching SendClientType/SendActivateRenderState/SendInitRenderState/SendActivateNextRenderState/SendDeactivateRenderState/RemoveObject definitions + 1 local-var tightening `long GameIDD` → `int32_t GameIDD` in the LOGOFF emit chain). Total: 2 files modified.
      Notes: Phase K coverage stays at **102/207 (49.3%)** — Wave 69 is +0 ratchet because all six opcodes were already counted by passive-observation tests. Direct-reply/survival-probe pattern count unchanged at 34 waves; passive-observation pattern count unchanged at 5 waves; hardening pattern count now 2 waves (Wave 67 — 0x0096; Wave 68 — 0x007F). Wave 69 is a server change with no new tests — pure tightening sweep, validated by existing test coverage proving no regression. Next-Phase-K targets after Wave 69: dedicated 4-byte payload-length hardening tests for each of the 4 wire-shape tightening sites (0x003C, 0x0007, 0x0032, 0x0003); proxy `Connection::SendResponse` `sizeof(long)` framing-header audit (broader scope, coordinated remediation across proxy + test client); 0x004F STARBASE_SET negative-assertion wave; further +1 ratchets from the remaining ~105/207 untested opcodes.

- [x] Wave 68 (2026-05-28): **SERVER TIGHTENING** — `Player::SetManufactureID` at `server/src/PlayerConnection.cpp:10134-10138` (declared at `server/src/PlayerClass.h:1028`) used to emit 0x007F MANUFACTURE_SET_MANUFACTURE_ID with `sizeof(long)` bytes. Same LP64 sizeof(long) regression class as Wave 67's case-8 JOB_ACCEPT_REPLY (0x0096): on LP64 Linux x86_64, `long` is 8 bytes; on Win32 (retail), `long` is 4 bytes. Wave 68 tightens the Linux server to emit 4 bytes via an `int32_t mfg_id` parameter (was: `long mfg_id`) and pins the byte-exact 4-byte payload via a new hardening test. **+0 ratchet (0x007F already covered by Wave 35's passive-presence assertion in HandshakeEmitsFullSendLoginShipDataFanout); coverage stays at 102/207 (49.3%). Second server tightening in Phase K.**
      Status: done — `PlayerConnection.cpp:10134-10139` modified (`int32_t mfg_id` replaces `long mfg_id` plus an explanatory comment matching Wave 67's style); `PlayerClass.h:1028` declaration tightened to match (ODR consistency); server image rebuilt; `SectorManufactureSetManufactureIdHardeningTests.ManufactureSetManufactureId_EmittedDuringHandshake_HasExactly4BytePayload` written; build green; test passes (7s).
      Server-integrity citation (per CLAUDE.md "Server integrity rules"). Same rationale as Wave 67. The CLAUDE.md rule explicitly welcomes tightenings toward retail wire fidelity. Primary-source citation: `archive/kyp-snapshot/linux-port-legacy/PlayerConnection.cpp:6924-6928` is the unaltered Win32-derived source — `SetManufactureID(long mfg_id)` with `SendOpcode(..., &mfg_id, sizeof(mfg_id))`. On Win32 (LP32) that evaluated to 4 bytes — the wire shape the retail client was compiled to receive. The Linux build silently inflated to 8 bytes because LP64 widens `long`. Wave 68's single-token swap (`long` → `int32_t`) restores byte-exact agreement with the retail wire format.
      Pattern — harness extension wave: extended `SectorHandshake.Session` to expose `HandshakeFrames` as `IReadOnlyList<(ushort Opcode, int PayloadLength)>` (the same set of frames as `HandshakeOpcodes` but with each entry's wire-payload length attached). Wave 68 lit this up so byte-exact hardening tests can pin a specific emit's payload size from the captured handshake stream without re-driving the login flow. `DoSectorLoginUntilStartAsync` now returns `(EncryptedTcpConnection conn, int startId, IReadOnlyList<(ushort, int)> frames)`; `EstablishAsync`/`ReestablishAsync` populate both `HandshakeOpcodes` (preserved for backward compatibility with Wave 35/49/50/51/52/53/54 tests) and the new `HandshakeFrames`. Test runs at Luna Station (sector 10151) via StationLogin → SetManufactureID(ntohl(ManuID)) emit path; the test filters `HandshakeFrames` for opcode 0x007F and asserts every captured frame has `PayloadLength == 4` exactly.
      Test — new `ManufactureSetManufactureId_EmittedDuringHandshake_HasExactly4BytePayload` in `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/Opcodes/SectorManufactureSetManufactureIdHardeningTests.cs`. Character firstName="MfgPin68", shipName="MfgPin68Ship". Assertion is `Assert.NotEmpty(mfgFrames)` followed by `Assert.All(mfgFrames, f => Assert.Equal(4, f.PayloadLength))`. Cleanup runs standard 0x00B9 → 0x00BA + GlobalDeleteCharacter via finally block.
      Regression coverage — 6 classes documented inline in test xmldoc: (a) SetManufactureID parameter-type revert at `server/src/PlayerConnection.cpp:10134` (the whole point of Wave 68 — reverting `int32_t mfg_id` back to `long mfg_id` would re-inflate `sizeof(mfg_id)` to 8 bytes on Linux x86_64, ballooning every 0x007F frame to an 8-byte payload — caught immediately); (b) SetManufactureID header-declaration revert at `server/src/PlayerClass.h:1028` (declaration must match definition; a mismatch would either fail to compile or silently bind to a different overload — caught by C++ ODR plus the length assertion); (c) SendOpcode header-width revert at `server/src/PlayerConnection.cpp:127` (would corrupt every inner opcode in the 0x2016 PACKET_SEQUENCE parser; 0x007F wouldn't appear under its correct label so the Assert.NotEmpty filter fires); (d) Proxy SendClientPacketSequence inner-opcode guard tightening at `proxy/UDPProxyToClient_linux.cpp:568` (currently passes 0x007F < 0x0FFF; a tighter bound would silently drop 0x007F from the wire and Assert.NotEmpty fires); (e) SectorManager call-site signature drift at `server/src/SectorManager.cpp:345/475/538` (if a future refactor reintroduces a `long` overload or removes one of the three SetManufactureID call sites, fewer/zero 0x007F frames land — Assert.NotEmpty catches); (f) DoSectorLoginUntilStartAsync drain-loop payload-length capture regression (the new harness extension at `SectorHandshake.cs:396-428`; if a future refactor drops the length field or starts under-counting payload bytes, the test would observe wrong/zero lengths for every captured 0x007F frame — every subsequent payload-length hardening wave depends on this capture path working).
      Seam-discovery — Wave 68 is the **second server-tightening wave in Phase K** (Wave 67 was the first) and the **second "hardening test" pattern** in the suite. The same LP64 sizeof(long) hazard appears in any other server-side `void func(long X) { SendOpcode(..., &X, sizeof(X)); }` pattern — Wave 69 generalised this with a mass-audit (see Wave 69 entry). Wave 68 also added the `HandshakeFrames` capture path as harness infrastructure for future byte-exact payload-length hardening waves — any opcode that fires during the sector-login handshake can now be byte-exact length-asserted with a single LINQ filter against `session.HandshakeFrames`, no additional client stimulus needed.
      Touches: `server/src/PlayerClass.h` (line 1028 decl `long mfg_id` → `int32_t mfg_id`), `server/src/PlayerConnection.cpp` (lines 10134-10139 def `long mfg_id` → `int32_t mfg_id` with explanatory comment), `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/Opcodes/SectorHandshake.cs` (extended Session class with `HandshakeFrames` field; refactored DoSectorLoginUntilStartAsync to return tuples; EstablishAsync/ReestablishAsync populate both HandshakeOpcodes and HandshakeFrames), new `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/Opcodes/SectorManufactureSetManufactureIdHardeningTests.cs` (full xmldoc citing kyp linux-port-legacy primary source + 6 regression classes + 4-byte payload-length assertion), `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/TestAccounts.cs` (`ManufactureSetManufactureId_EmittedDuringHandshake_HasExactly4BytePayload` → cli_test65 9_000_065), `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/Fixtures/seed.sql` (cli_test65 INSERT row, status=100), `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/Coverage/TestedOpcodes.cs` (0x007F citation updated to credit Wave 68's hardening test alongside Wave 35's passive-presence assertion).

Direct-reply/survival-probe pattern count unchanged at 34 waves; passive-observation pattern count unchanged at 5 waves; hardening pattern count now 2 waves (Wave 67 — 0x0096; Wave 68 — 0x007F). **Second server change in Phase K** — server image was rebuilt via `docker compose build server`. - [x] Wave 67 (2026-05-28): **SERVER TIGHTENING** — case-8 of `Player::HandleStarbaseRequest` at PlayerConnection.cpp:10006-10020 used to emit 0x0096 JOB_ACCEPT_REPLY with `sizeof(long)` bytes. On LP64 Linux x86_64, `long` is 8 bytes; on Win32 (retail), `long` is 4 bytes. Wave 67 tightens the Linux server to emit 4 bytes via an `int32_t job_id` local in the case-8 arm (was: `long job_id`) and pins the byte-exact 4-byte echo via a new hardening test. **+0 ratchet (0x0096 already covered by Wave 57 case-9 arm); coverage stays at 102/207 (49.3%). First server change in many waves.**
      Status: done — `PlayerConnection.cpp:10009-10013` modified (`int32_t job_id` replaces `long job_id` plus explanatory comment); server image rebuilt; `SectorStarbaseAcceptJob8Tests.StarbaseAcceptJobAction8_OnSentinelJobId_ReceivesByteExact4ByteJobAcceptReply` written; build green; test passes (15s).
      Server-integrity citation (per CLAUDE.md "Server integrity rules"). The CLAUDE.md rule says: "It does NOT restrict changes that *tighten* the server toward greater fidelity (e.g. rejecting an input the real server rejected but we currently accept) — those are always welcome." Wave 67 is *exactly* that — bringing our Linux server's case-8 wire shape into byte-exact agreement with the retail Win32 wire format the real client expects. Concretely: the retail Win32 server's `sizeof(long)` evaluated to 4 (LP32 Win32 long); our Linux server's `sizeof(long)` evaluates to 8 (LP64 Linux long); the fix is a single token swap (`long` → `int32_t`) that locks `sizeof(job_id) == 4` on both platforms. No widened input acceptance, no loosened gating, no relaxed posture, no fabricated replies — just a wire-shape correction. The retail client expects 4 bytes; an 8-byte emit drifts the inner-opcode framing of the 0x2016 PACKET_SEQUENCE the case-8 reply rides in, risking mis-frame of any subsequent inner opcode the proxy bundles into the same sequence. Tightening per CLAUDE.md welcomed.
      Pattern — third wave to use the two-stage handshake into Net-7 SOL (sector 10711, the unique JT-equipped starbase). Stimulus is the 9B 0x004E payload with `StarbaseID` repurposed as the `job_id` selector (Action=8 Accept-Job; sentinel job_id 0x12345678 provably absent from m_JobList since RefreshJobs assigns IDs monotonically from 0 via m_JobListID++ at SectorManager.cpp:1476-1491). Server case-8 arm now runs: `int32_t job_id = pkt->StarbaseID; SendOpcode(0x0096, (u8*)&job_id, sizeof(job_id)); if (sm && !sm->AwardJob(this, job_id)) SendVaMessageC(17, "Job unavailable.");` — `sizeof(job_id)` is now provably 4. AwardJob returns false for any job_id not present in m_JobList, so the trailing 0x001D MESSAGE_STRING "Job unavailable." also fires; we don't assert on the secondary frame (already covered by existing MESSAGE_STRING tests); the load-bearing assertion is the byte-exact 4-byte 0x0096 shape.
      Test — new `StarbaseAcceptJobAction8_OnSentinelJobId_ReceivesByteExact4ByteJobAcceptReply` in `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/Opcodes/SectorStarbaseAcceptJob8Tests.cs`. Character firstName="Acceptr", shipName="AcceptrShip". Stimulus 9B 0x004E with Action=8 and StarbaseID=0x12345678. 400-frame drain looking for 0x0096. Assertions: `payload.Length == 4` EXACTLY (load-bearing — pins the tightening); int32 LE at [0..4] == 0x12345678 (sentinel echo verbatim). Cleanup runs standard 0x00B9 → 0x00BA + GlobalDeleteCharacter.
      Regression coverage — 9 classes documented inline in test xmldoc: (a) `sizeof(long)` revert at PlayerConnection.cpp:10009 (the whole point of Wave 67 — reverting `int32_t job_id` back to `long job_id` would inflate sizeof(job_id) to 8 on Linux x86_64, ballooning 0x0096 payload to 8 bytes); (b) SendOpcode case-8 emit removal at PlayerConnection.cpp:10010 (drain times out — case-9 covered by Wave 57 is a separate code path); (c) Echo regression — if `(u8*)&job_id` is changed to a different source (hardcoded literal, stale buffer pointer), the byte-equal sentinel assertion catches; (d) StarbaseRequest packed-struct layout regression at PacketStructures.h:812-817 (9B canonical with ATTRIB_PACKED); (e) StarbaseID-field-width regression — int32→int64 widening shifts Action past payload end, server reads garbage; (f) STARBASE_REQUEST dispatch-table entry deletion at PlayerConnection.cpp:487-489 (server silently swallows request, drain times out); (g) SendOpcode header-width revert at PlayerConnection.cpp:127 (corrupts every inner opcode in 0x2016 PACKET_SEQUENCE); (h) proxy SendClientPacketSequence guard at UDPProxyToClient_linux.cpp:568 (currently passes 0x0096<0x0FFF; tightening drops the reply); (i) m_TradeWindow side-effect regression at PlayerConnection.cpp:9875 (handler unconditionally resets m_TradeWindow=false before the switch).
      Seam-discovery — Wave 67 is the **first server change in many waves**, and the first wave to use a server *tightening* (not just a passive observation) to validate a preservation-fidelity regression class on Linux. The same `sizeof(long)`-on-LP64 hazard pattern appears in any other server-side AddData<long>-with-sizeof-arg site that hits the wire — there's an opportunity for a follow-up grep audit over `sizeof(long)` and `sizeof(.*long.*)` in `server/src/` to identify other LP64-divergence candidates, gated by primary-source confirmation that the retail wire wanted 4 bytes (otherwise the change would *introduce* divergence). Also notable: Wave 67's test is the first **hardening test** in Phase K — a test that exists to pin an invariant that was *intentionally tightened by us*, not to characterise existing behaviour. The +0 ratchet reflects that we already had 0x0096 covered by Wave 57 (case-9 arm); this is depth coverage of a different case-arm with a load-bearing wire-shape assertion that case-9 does not have.
      Touches: `server/src/PlayerConnection.cpp` (case-8 of HandleStarbaseRequest at lines 10009-10013: `long job_id` → `int32_t job_id` + 3-line explanatory comment); `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/Opcodes/SectorStarbaseAcceptJob8Tests.cs` (NEW file with full xmldoc and 9 regression classes); `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/TestAccounts.cs` (`StarbaseAcceptJobAction8_OnSentinelJobId_ReceivesByteExact4ByteJobAcceptReply` → cli_test64 9_000_064); `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/Fixtures/seed.sql` (cli_test64 INSERT row, status=100). NOT touched: `tools/cli-client/src/CliClient.Core/Opcodes/OpcodeId.cs` (0x0096 already defined); `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/Coverage/TestedOpcodes.cs` (MinTestedCount stays at **102** since 0x0096 already counted by Wave 57; the new test is hardening depth coverage of a different case-arm).
      Notes: Phase K coverage stays at **102/207 (49.3%)** — Wave 67 is +0 ratchet because 0x0096 already counted. Direct-reply/survival-probe pattern count now 34 waves; passive-observation pattern count unchanged at 5 waves; indirect-stimulus pattern count unchanged at 3 waves. **First server change in many waves** — server image was rebuilt via `docker compose build server`.       Pattern — second wave to use Wave 65's two-stage handshake into Net-7 SOL (sector 10711, the unique JT-equipped starbase in the seed). Stimulus is the 9B 0x004E payload with `StarbaseID` repurposed as the `job_id` selector. Test uses **sentinel job_id 0x7FFFFFFF (INT32_MAX)** that is provably absent from m_JobList — RefreshJobs assigns real IDs monotonically from 0 via m_JobListID++ at SectorManager.cpp:1476-1491, so no test run produces enough cycles to reach INT32_MAX. Server case-7 arm calls GetJobDescription which: (1) early-returns 0 when m_JobListCount==0 (non-JT — doesn't fire here); (2) writes the 4B job_id LE placeholder header via AddData<long> (index → 4); (3) scans m_JobList for jn->ID==job_id (no match); (4) the dead-code `if (index == 0)` fallback at line 1570 doesn't fire (index already 4); (5) returns 4. SendOpcode emits 0x0094 with exactly 4 bytes — the echoed job_id LE.

      Pattern — first Phase K wave to use the two-stage `EstablishAsync(10151)` + `ReestablishAsync(10711)` handshake to land in a JT-equipped starbase OTHER than the Terran-Warrior start sector. Sector 10711 (Net-7 SOL) is the unique JT-equipped starbase in the integration-test seed (`starbase_terminals` row id=61, starbase_id=43, type=3, terminal_level=50; StationLoader.cpp:314-316 assigns JTLevel when type==3 && terminal_level>0). The first-login at 10151 (Luna Station, non-JT) creates the avatar via `ReInitializeSavedData`; explicit 0x00B9 → 0x00BA round-trip + `DropPlayerFromGalaxy` then the second-login at 10711 takes the `ReloadSavedData` branch preserving the LOGIN packet's `ToSectorID`. Client sends a 9B 0x004E payload {int32 PlayerID=0; int32 StarbaseID=0; byte Action=6}. Server emits 0x0093 with a variable-length payload: 4B count header (overwritten with the actual emitted-job count) + N tuples of {int32 id, int32 category, int32 zero, int32 level, NUL-terminated title, NUL-terminated sponsor, NUL-terminated reward}. Test parses the structure (walks count header → N×16B fixed prefix + 3 NUL-strings each → asserts cursor exactly consumes payload).

Assertions: payload.Length>=4; jobsCount=Read32LE(payload[0..4]) in [0,9]; each tuple's level in [1,200]; each of the 3 strings is NUL-terminated; cursor==payload.Length at end of walk. Honesty correction: the wire-walking structural assertion is strictly stronger than the byte-exact 4B-zero assertion would have been — it pins the entire {count, N tuples of {prefix, 3 strings}} layout including the AddData<long> 4B-on-x86_64 specialisation as a load-bearing per-field wire invariant.

The seed has exactly one JT-equipped starbase (Net-7 SOL); future +1 waves needing JT-handler coverage would have to either: (i) seed an additional starbase_terminals row with type=3+terminal_level>0 (low-risk DB-only change since StationLoader doesn't filter on is_active); or (ii) accept Net-7 SOL as the canonical JT venue and pile additional STARBASE_REQUEST sub-action waves on top. Sub-action 7 (JOB_DESCRIPTION) and the case-8 commit arm both exist as remaining direct-reply candidates from the same 0x004E entry point. Also notable: the test's wire-walking approach (parse structure rather than asserting fixed bytes) is the right pattern for any future Phase K wave where the server emits non-deterministic-per-run payloads — sets a template for variable-length-emit assertions.

      Pattern — closes 0x00A3 CLIENT_CHAT_REQUEST as a **complete triple-fan-out test surface**: type=24 → 0x00A4 (Wave 63), type=28 → 0x00A5 (Wave 32), type=1 + bogus recipient → 0x00A6 (Wave 64). Client sends a 29-byte 0x00A3 payload with type=1 (CCE_SPEAK_LOCALLY), string1="Bzqxqxz64", string2="", string3="hi", data_size=0. Server walks variable-length strings; type=1 has no matching switch case (default falls through) and reaches the post-switch if-chain at PlayerConnection.cpp:1731 where `CCE_SPEAK_LOCALLY + string3[0] != '/'` routes to `ChatSendPrivate`. The lookup loop walks m_GlobalPlayerList via strcasecmp against the trimmed nick; "Bzqxqxz64" cannot be a real character name (no vowels — character creation rejects via G_ERROR_ONE_VOWEL), so FoundPlayer stays false and the post-loop branch fires `SendClientChatError(CHAT_ERROR_INVALID_PERSON=4, CCE_SPEAK_LOCALLY=1, Nick)`. SendClientChatError builds a 23-byte payload via AddData(reason) + AddData(type) + AddDataLS(player) + AddDataLS(channel="") + AddDataLS(other="") and emits 0x00A6 via SendOpcode.
      Test — new `ClientChatRequest_SpeakLocallyNonexistentRecipient_ReceivesClientChatError` in `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/Opcodes/SectorClientChatErrorTests.cs`. Character firstName="Erroria", shipName="ErroriaShip" (vowel-required compliant). Stimulus 29B 0x00A3 with type=1, nick="Bzqxqxz64" (9 chars, no vowel — guaranteed not a real character), msg="hi" (2 chars, non-slash). 400-frame drain looking for 0x00A6. Assertions: payload.Length==23; bytes [0..4] reason==4 (CHAT_ERROR_INVALID_PERSON); [4..8] type==1 (CCE_SPEAK_LOCALLY); [8..10] player_len==9; [10..19] ASCII player=="Bzqxqxz64" verbatim; [19..21] channel_len==0; [21..23] other_len==0. Cleanup runs standard 0x00B9 → 0x00BA + GlobalDeleteCharacter.
      Legitimacy — fourth direct-reply opcode off the 0x00A3 dispatcher (Waves 32/63/64 cover three switch arms each emitting a different reply). HandleClientChatRequest, the post-switch CCE_SPEAK_LOCALLY branch, ChatSendPrivate's !FoundPlayer arm, and SendClientChatError all exist in the upstream server unchanged; Wave 64 pins existing behaviour with zero server change, zero widened input acceptance, zero loosened gating, zero debug-only opcode. Per CLAUDE.md server-integrity rule, exactly the "tightening" the rule welcomes. The no-vowel recipient name choice is **load-bearing for test determinism** — character creation enforces `G_ERROR_ONE_VOWEL` at the global-plane CreateCharacter handler, so any name without a vowel is provably absent from the global player list regardless of which parallel tests are running. This Phase K test-authoring technique is reusable for any future test that needs a "guaranteed not in the player list" recipient string.
      Regression coverage — 13 classes documented inline in test xmldoc and TestedOpcodes commentary: (a) 0x00A6 SendOpcode removal at PlayerConnection.cpp:4677 (drain times out); (b) SendClientChatError wire-shape regression — adding fields, reordering, changing default-args for channel/other surfaces via length and byte-exact assertions; (c) AddData<long> specialisation revert at PacketMethods.h:37-42 (reason+type are both `long` in C++ signature; generic template would emit 8B each, ballooning payload from 23B to 31B); (d) ChatSendPrivate !FoundPlayer branch removal at PlayerManager.cpp:1008-1011 (silent no-op, drain times out); (e) ChatSendPrivate Nick&&Message guard regression at PlayerManager.cpp:973 (widened guard silently no-ops 9B nick); (f) post-switch CCE_SPEAK_LOCALLY branch regression at PlayerConnection.cpp:1731 (deletion or AdminLevel gate stops ChatSendPrivate); (g) slash-dispatch over-trigger regression at PlayerConnection.cpp:1735 (broadening `string3[0] == '/'` would route "hi" through HandleSlashCommands); (h) variable-length string walker regression at PlayerConnection.cpp:1659-1692; (i) ClientChatRequest layout regression in PacketStructures.h:669-681 (long→int32 revert shifts type to offset 8, reads type==0 CCE_SPEAK_ON, falls into SPEAK_ON branch); (j) dispatcher mis-route at PlayerConnection.cpp:587 (swap with HandleTriggerEmote routes 29B through SendNotifyEmote); (k) proxy default-case ForwardClientOpcode dropping 0x00A3; (l) proxy SendClientPacketSequence inner-opcode guard at UDPProxyToClient_linux.cpp:568 tightening (0x00A6 < 0x0FFF passes currently); (m) Player::Name() postfix regression at GetPostFix path — documented as out-of-scope because the echoed Player field in 0x00A6 is the Nick we sent verbatim, not Name(), so this regression doesn't break Wave 64's assertions.
      Seam-discovery — Wave 64 establishes **systematic dispatcher-entry-point enumeration** as a Phase K technique: pick a high-fan-out handler (0x00A3 has 8+ switch cases plus 5+ post-switch branches each gated on `request->type`), enumerate uncovered emit sites, write per-arm test stimuli — yields multiple +1 ratchets from a single dispatcher entry with minimal per-wave authoring overhead (Waves 32 + 63 + 64 covered 0x00A4, 0x00A5, 0x00A6 from the same 0x00A3 stimulus opcode). Other high-fan-out dispatchers worth auditing for the same technique: 0x002C/0x002D ACTION/ACTION2, 0x0099 NAVIGATION (already covered), 0x004E/0x004F STARBASE_REQUEST/STARBASE_SET (one covered, one open as negative-assertion candidate), 0x0086/0x0087 MISSION_FORFEIT/MISSION_DISMISSAL (both still untested in Phase K). The 0x00A3 triple-fan-out closure also unlocks a future negative-assertion wave on the OTHER post-switch branches (CCE_SPEAK_ON, CCE_ENTER_CHANNEL, CCE_EXIT_CHANNEL, CCE_INSERT_CHANNEL, CCR_LIST_CHANNELS, CCR_LIST_ALL_CHANNELS, CCR_SECTOR_LOGIN) which mostly route to single-emit helpers but a couple may fan out into multi-opcode chains worth pinning.

      Status: done — `SectorClientChatListTests.ClientChatRequest_ListFriendsEmptyFriendList_ReceivesClientChatList` written; build green; test passes (8s); zero server change.
      Pattern — client establishes a station-sector handshake at Luna Station (sector 10151) and sends an 18-byte 0x00A3 CLIENT_CHAT_REQUEST {int32 PlayerID=0; int32 type=24 (CCR_LIST_FRIENDS, PacketStructures.h:659); short string_length1=0; short string_length2=0; short string_length3=0; int32 data_size=0} on the sector connection. Server `Player::HandleClientChatRequest` (PlayerConnection.cpp:1648) walks the variable-length string layout (three back-to-back short-prefixed strings followed by a 4B data_size and an optional trailing data block) which fast-paths through with all three lengths zero. The switch on `request->type` at line 1695 hits `case CCR_LIST_FRIENDS` at line 1720 which calls `ListFriends()` and **uses `return;` not `break;`** — the post-switch if-chain at line 1731 is skipped entirely, so 0x00A4 CLIENT_CHAT_LIST is the ONLY emit reachable from CCR_LIST_FRIENDS. `ListFriends` iterates `m_NumFriends`; for a fresh starbase character `m_NumFriends == 0` (initialised in `Player::FinishInit`), the for-loop is skipped, count stays 0, and `SendClientChatList(CHAT_LIST_FRIENDS=0, name, sector, 0, 0)` runs with the two iteration counts both zero. `SendClientChatList` builds a deterministic 14-byte payload via `AddData(listtype=0)` + `AddDataLS(channel="")` + `AddData(number1=0)` + `AddData(number2=0)` — the channel parameter defaults to `""` (declaration default at PlayerClass.h:850) so AddDataLS writes only the 2-byte length-prefix (zero) and zero string bytes; the per-entry name/sector inner loops are skipped because number1==0 and number2==0. Final wire payload: 4 + 2 + 4 + 4 = **14 bytes, all zero**.

(b) wire-shape regression in `SendClientChatList` — adding fields, reordering, changing the channel-string default, widening listtype to 8B (the AddData<long> specialisation class) all surface via 14B length assert and all-zero content assert; (c) AddData<long> specialisation revert at PacketMethods.h:37-42 — generic template would emit 8B for the listtype/number1/number2 long fields on Linux x86_64 ballooning payload to 4+4+2+8+8=26 bytes; (d) ListFriends body regression at PlayerMisc.cpp:1442 — non-zero iteration counts or non-empty channel string inflate the payload beyond 14B; (e) switch-case selector regression at PlayerConnection.cpp:1720 — literal CCR_LIST_FRIENDS must match PacketStructures.h:659 (=24); drift leaves no case matching, default falls through, no reply; (f) return→break regression at PlayerConnection.cpp:1722 — flips the invariant that ListFriends is the ONLY emit site reachable from CCR_LIST_FRIENDS (visible effect is nil for type=24 since none of the post-switch if-chain branches match, but the invariant is documented); (g) m_NumFriends initialisation regression in `Player::FinishInit` — stale entries from uninitialised heap memory would cause ListFriends to iterate them and emit a non-empty payload, surfaced via all-zero content assertion; (h) variable-length string walker regression at PlayerConnection.cpp:1659-1692 (short→int32 revert on string_length fields desyncs walker); (i) ClientChatRequest layout regression in PacketStructures.h:669-681 (long→int32 revert on PlayerID or type shifts type to offset 8, switch reads type==0 CCE_SPEAK_ON, falls into post-switch if-chain with no emit, drain times out); (j) dispatcher mis-route at PlayerConnection.cpp:587 — case label between 0x00A1 TRIGGER_EMOTE and 0x00B9 LOGOFF_REQUEST; a swap with HandleTriggerEmote routes 18B through SendNotifyEmote which fans out 0x00A2 to range-list peers and never replies (test times out); a swap with HandleLogoffRequest tears down the sector connection before 0x00A4 ever emits; (k) proxy default-case ForwardClientOpcode dropping 0x00A3 (relies on bottom-of-switch default forward in proxy/ClientToServer_linux_stubs.cpp); (l) proxy SendClientPacketSequence guard at UDPProxyToClient_linux.cpp:568 tightening — currently passes 0x00A4 (< 0x0FFF).
      Seam-discovery — Wave 63 confirms 0x00A3 CLIENT_CHAT_REQUEST as the **single highest-leverage dispatcher entry point in Phase K so far**: three previously-untested switch arms (CCR_LIST_FRIENDS=24, CCR_FRIEND_STATUS_ONLY=28, and the still-open CCE_SPEAK_LOCALLY=1 path) each emit a different server-side opcode (0x00A4, 0x00A5, 0x00A6 respectively). The CCE_SPEAK_LOCALLY path drives `Player::ChatSendPrivate` (PlayerManager.cpp ~1010) which emits `SendClientChatError(CHAT_ERROR_INVALID_PERSON=4, CCE_SPEAK_LOCALLY=1, Nick)` when string1 names a recipient that doesn't exist — a guaranteed-failure path for any fresh character sending a tell to a fabricated nick. That's Wave 64's strongest candidate. Also confirmed by direct test execution: the return; at line 1722 means there is exactly ONE 0x00A4 frame in the drain (8s test duration, no extra frames observed) — extremely clean diagnostic shape compared to the multi-emit fan-out helpers (Wave 62's /fgps emits 2 frames; passive-observation handshake waves emit dozens).
