- [x] Wave 62 (2026-05-27): 0x00BE CONFIRMED_ACTION_OFFER + 0x006A CLIENT_SOUND (`/fgps` CLIENT_CHAT slash command at PlayerConnection.cpp:6377-6382 driving `Player::SendConfirmedActionOffer` at PlayerConnection.cpp:861-873) **third indirect-stimulus +2 ratchet** — thirty-second direct/indirect-reply wave. +2 ratchet 96/207 → **98/207 (47.3%)**.
      Status: done — `SectorFgpsTests.FgpsSlashCommand_OnSlashFgps_ReceivesConfirmedActionOfferAndClientSound` written; build green; test passes (7s); zero server change.
      Pattern — client establishes a station-sector handshake at Luna Station (sector 10151) and sends a 0x0033 CLIENT_CHAT carrying Message="/fgps", Type=Group. `Player::HandleClientChat` (PlayerConnection.cpp:4614) forwards to `HandleSlashCommands` single-slash block at PlayerConnection.cpp:5447. The case 'f' arm at PlayerConnection.cpp:6377-6382 (sitting at the top level of case 'f', OUTSIDE both `AdminLevel() >= GM` inner blocks — confirmed by re-reading the surrounding block structure) matches `strcmp(pch, "fgps") == 0` (note: exact strcmp, not MatchOptWithParam, so no args are taken). On match the handler calls `SendConfirmedActionOffer()` and sets `success = true; msg_sent = true`. `Player::SendConfirmedActionOffer` (PlayerConnection.cpp:861-873) emits TWO opcodes in fixed order: first 0x00BE CONFIRMED_ACTION_OFFER with a 17-byte hard-coded literal `{0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x01, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x65, 0x07, 0x00, 0x4d, 0x65, 0x73, 0x73, 0x61, 0x67, 0x65}` (header `00 00 00 01 00 00 00 65`, length-prefix `07 00` little-endian, ASCII string "Message"); then `SendClientSound("push_mission_alert_sound", 2, 0)` which constructs a 34B payload: int32 LE length=25 (24 char name + 1 NUL), 24B ASCII "push_mission_alert_sound", 1B NUL terminator, int32 LE channel=2, 1B queue=0.
      Test — new `FgpsSlashCommand_OnSlashFgps_ReceivesConfirmedActionOfferAndClientSound` in `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/Opcodes/SectorFgpsTests.cs`. Single-stage `EstablishAsync` at sectorId=10151 (Luna Station); character firstName="Fugpsi62", shipName="Fugpsi62Ship" (the initial "Fgps62" choice was rejected by the server's vowel-required character-name validation with `G_ERROR_ONE_VOWEL` (code 4) — a useful piece of seam-discovery for future Phase K wave authors). Stimulus 0x0033 CLIENT_CHAT with Message="/fgps", Type=Group followed by 400-frame drain that captures BOTH 0x00BE and 0x006A without assuming ordering (the two emits arrive back-to-back from the same SendConfirmedActionOffer call but the drain orders by network arrival, so the test grabs both and asserts independently). 0x00BE assertion: byte-for-byte equality on the full 17-byte literal `{00 00 00 01 00 00 00 65 07 00 4d 65 73 73 61 67 65}` — any byte flip in `SendConfirmedActionOffer` surfaces. 0x006A assertion: payload.Length==34, int32 LE length at bytes[0..4]==25, ASCII "push_mission_alert_sound" at bytes[4..28], NUL at offset 28, int32 LE channel at bytes[29..33]==2, byte queue at offset 33==0. Cleanup runs explicit 0x00B9 LOGOFF_REQUEST → 0x00BA LOGOFF_CONFIRMATION round-trip + `GlobalDeleteCharacter` (identical pattern to Waves 55-61).
      Legitimacy — third indirect-stimulus wave (Waves 60+61 were first/second). Slash-command dispatcher, /fgps arm, and SendConfirmedActionOffer + SendClientSound emit sites all exist in the upstream server unchanged; Wave 62 pins existing behaviour with zero server change, zero widened input acceptance, zero loosened gating, zero debug-only opcode. Per CLAUDE.md server-integrity rule, exactly the "tightening" the rule welcomes. Notable: this is the FIRST Phase K wave to land a +2 ratchet from a single stimulus (Wave 54's +2 was passive-observation off two unrelated handshake fan-outs; Wave 13's +2 was a request/reply pair). The /fgps arm is the cleanest "fixed-literal" emit site found so far in the slash-command dispatcher — both payloads are byte-for-byte deterministic (no atoi() echoing, no name-mangling, no per-character variability), making the byte-exact assertions wire-protocol-level guarantees rather than statistical observations.
      Regression coverage — 12 classes documented inline in test xmldoc and TestedOpcodes commentary: (a) 0x00BE emit removal at PlayerConnection.cpp:869 — drain times out for 0x00BE; (b) 0x00BE literal-byte regression — any flip in the 17B `action_data` initialiser at PlayerConnection.cpp:864-868 surfaces via byte-for-byte equality assert; (c) 0x006A emit removal at PlayerConnection.cpp:872 — drain times out for 0x006A; (d) SendClientSound length-prefix regression at PlayerConnection.cpp:949-968 — AddData<int32_t>(packet, strlen+1, index) precedes the string; flipping to strlen (excluding NUL) shrinks length to 24 and trips the length==25 assertion; (e) SendClientSound string-payload regression — name "push_mission_alert_sound" hard-coded at call site; refactor flipping to a different sound name surfaces via 24-byte ASCII compare; (f) SendClientSound NUL-terminator regression — explicit AddData<char>(packet, 0, index) at offset 28 after the 24-char string; if dropped the channel int32 slides forward 1B and the offset-28 NUL check trips; (g) SendClientSound channel/queue ordering regression — channel goes BEFORE queue (channel=2 int32 LE at bytes[29..33], queue=0 byte at offset 33); flipping order trips bytes[29..33]==2 OR offset-33==0; (h) AddData<long> template specialisation regression at PacketMethods.h:37-42 — currently forces int32_t 4B writes on Linux x86_64; if specialisation removed the default long template would write 8B and channel field grows to 8B making payload.Length==38 instead of 34 — Wave 62 pins this as a **load-bearing wire-protocol-level guarantee** because the channel parameter in C++ is `long` (PlayerConnection.cpp:949) and the AddData call resolves to AddData<long>; (i) single-slash gate regression at PlayerConnection.cpp:5447; (j) case 'f' dispatch regression at PlayerConnection.cpp:6377-6382 — the strcmp("fgps") sits at the top of case 'f' OUTSIDE the AdminLevel >= GM inner blocks, so removing the gate-removal protection (i.e. moving the arm INTO the GM block) would break this test on non-admin accounts but cli_test* accounts have status=100=ADMIN so they wouldn't surface a GM-only regression — Wave 62's regression coverage explicitly does NOT claim to catch that class; (k) HandleClientChat slash dispatch regression at PlayerConnection.cpp:4614-4617; (l) proxy SendClientPacketSequence inner-opcode guard at UDPProxyToClient_linux.cpp:568 (both 0x00BE and 0x006A are < 0x0FFF and pass current guard).
      Seam-discovery — Wave 62 establishes the indirect-stimulus pattern as capable of yielding **multi-opcode +N ratchets** when the slash arm fans out into a multi-emit helper function. `SendConfirmedActionOffer` is one such helper; others worth auditing for similar fan-out potential: `SendVaMessage` (used by many slash arms but emits a single 0x001D MESSAGE_STRING already covered), `SendNavigation` (already covered), `Player::OpenInterface` (single emit, Wave 60), `SendConfirmAction` family. Wave 62 also surfaced the **character-name vowel-required validation** as a Phase K test-authoring footgun: any future indirect-stimulus wave that needs a custom character name must include at least one of a/e/i/o/u/y or the CreateCharacter call returns GlobalError code 4 (G_ERROR_ONE_VOWEL) during the seeding stage and the test fails before reaching the actual assertion. The AddData<long> specialisation finding is also significant — it's the first Phase K wave to explicitly pin a template-specialisation as a load-bearing wire-protocol invariant, which means any future C++14/17/20 migration audit that touches PacketMethods.h:37-42 has at minimum Wave 62 as a regression backstop. Future indirect-stimulus +2-or-better candidates: /yell, /shout, /scan, /dock (most of these gate on AdminLevel or non-fresh-character state but a couple may pass — needs server-side audit).
      Touches: `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/Opcodes/SectorFgpsTests.cs` (NEW file with full xmldoc and 12 regression classes); `tools/cli-client/src/CliClient.Core/Opcodes/OpcodeId.cs` (+0x006A ClientSound between OpenInterface 0x0066 and the global section; +0x00BE ConfirmedActionOffer between CtaResponse 0x00BD and ConfirmedActionResponse 0x00C0); `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/TestAccounts.cs` (`FgpsSlashCommand_OnSlashFgps_ReceivesConfirmedActionOfferAndClientSound` → cli_test59 9_000_059); `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/Fixtures/seed.sql` (cli_test59 INSERT row, status=100); `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/Coverage/TestedOpcodes.cs` (MinTestedCount 96→**98**; +2 sorted-position entries — 0x006A CLIENT_SOUND between 0x0066 OPEN_INTERFACE and 0x006D GLOBAL_CONNECT and 0x00BE CONFIRMED_ACTION_OFFER between 0x00BD CTA_RESPONSE and 0x00C0 CONFIRMED_ACTION_RESPONSE — each with full regression-class commentary).

- [x] Wave 61 (2026-05-27): 0x0065 UI_TRIGGER (`/uitrigger 0,0` CLIENT_CHAT slash command at PlayerConnection.cpp:7575-7592) **second indirect-stimulus +1 ratchet** — thirty-first direct/indirect-reply wave. +1 ratchet 95/207 → **96/207 (46.4%)**.
      Status: done — `SectorUiTriggerTests.UiTriggerSlashCommand_OnSlashUitrigger_ReceivesUiTriggerEmit` written; build green; test passes (8s); zero server change.
      Pattern — client establishes a station-sector handshake at Luna Station (sector 10151) and sends a 0x0033 CLIENT_CHAT carrying Message="/uitrigger 0,0", Type=Group. `Player::HandleClientChat` (server/src/PlayerConnection.cpp:4614) forwards to `HandleSlashCommands`'s single-slash block at PlayerConnection.cpp:5447. The case 'u' arm at PlayerConnection.cpp:7575-7592 matches `MatchOptWithParam("uitrigger", pch, param, msg_sent)` (the first arm in case 'u'; `else if (MatchOptWithParam("upgrade", ...))` follows but only triggers on string "upgrade" which differs from "uitrigger" at position 1). `strtok_s` pulls a="0" and b="0"; on `if (b)` true the handler runs `int Index=0; unsigned char Data[75]; *((long*) &Data[Index]) = atoi(a); Index+=4; *((long*) &Data[Index]) = atoi(b); Index+=4; SendOpcode(ENB_OPCODE_0065_UI_TRIGGER, &Data, Index=8)`. Wire payload Index=8 bytes: {int32 a; int32 b} little-endian. For a=0, b=0 the payload is 8 zero bytes.
      Test — new `UiTriggerSlashCommand_OnSlashUitrigger_ReceivesUiTriggerEmit` in `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/Opcodes/SectorUiTriggerTests.cs`. Single-stage `EstablishAsync` at sectorId=10151; stimulus 0x0033 CLIENT_CHAT with Message="/uitrigger 0,0", Type=Group followed by 400-frame drain for 0x0065 asserts payload.Length EXACTLY 8, bytes[0..4]==0 (a echo), bytes[4..8]==0 (b echo). Cleanup runs explicit 0x00B9 → 0x00BA logoff + `GlobalDeleteCharacter` (identical pattern to Waves 55-60).
      Legitimacy — second indirect-stimulus wave in Phase K (Wave 60 was the first via /openif). Slash-command dispatcher and the /uitrigger SendOpcode site exist in the upstream server; Wave 61 pins existing behaviour with zero server change, zero widened input acceptance, zero loosened gating, zero debug-only opcode. Per CLAUDE.md server-integrity rule, exactly the "tightening" the rule welcomes. **The sizeof(long) WRITE-path bug at PlayerConnection.cpp:7585/7587 is wire-invisible here, unlike Wave 59's HandleCTARequest**. On Linux x86_64 the first 8B write to Data[0..8] stores low 4B=atoi(a) and high 4B=0; the second 8B write to Data[4..12] overwrites Data[4..8] with low 4B=atoi(b) and stores high 4B=0 at Data[8..12]; the final SendOpcode(... Index=8) only emits Data[0..8] = {a, b} which is identical to clean int32_t* writes, and the 75B buffer prevents stack overflow. This is the **third leg of the Phase K sizeof(long) bug taxonomy**: (1) READ over-read (Waves 7/11/12); (2) WRITE buffer overflow clobbering adjacent fields (Wave 59 — HandleCTARequest with 9B stack array); (3) WRITE wire-invisible-but-fragile (Wave 61 — fortunate-accident wire shape pinned so any future surrounding-code change surfaces).
      Regression coverage — 10 classes documented inline in test xmldoc and TestedOpcodes commentary: (a) 0x0065 emit removal at PlayerConnection.cpp:7589; (b) sizeof(long) WRITE-path regression flipping wire-visibility — widening emit slice to Index=16, reordering b-then-a so the high-half clobber goes the wrong direction, or dropping Index+=4 increments so both writes land at Data[0] — all surface via 8B length assertion or {a,b} value assertions; (c) single-slash gate regression at PlayerConnection.cpp:5447; (d) case 'u' dispatch regression at PlayerConnection.cpp:7575; (e) MatchOptWithParam parsing regression at PlayerConnection.cpp:4526-4554; (f) strtok_s second-call regression at PlayerConnection.cpp:7578-7579 — if b comes back NULL the SendOpcode is skipped and drain times out; (g) HandleClientChat slash dispatch regression at PlayerConnection.cpp:4614-4617; (h) ClientChatCodec wire-format regression; (i) proxy SendClientPacketSequence inner-opcode guard at UDPProxyToClient_linux.cpp:568 tightening (0x0065 < 0x0FFF currently passes); (j) SendOpcode header-width revert at PlayerConnection.cpp:127.
      Seam-discovery — Wave 61 establishes the indirect-stimulus pattern as a **repeatable** category, not a one-off. The /uitrigger arm has the same shape as the /openif arm except the SendOpcode wraps the args directly (no helper-function intermediary like Player::OpenInterface) — confirming the pattern generalises across single-slash arms. Wave 61 also **identifies the third leg of the Phase K sizeof(long) bug taxonomy** — wire-invisible-but-fragile WRITE paths where the source-level bug exists but the wire shape happens to be correct by a combination of buffer width and trailing-overwrite arrangement. This is more dangerous than the Wave-59 class because it's invisible until someone refactors the surrounding code — a pure preservation hazard that Wave 61's test now pins. A grep audit of `\*((long\*) &.*\[Index\]\) = atoi` patterns in server/src/ would enumerate other Wave-61-class sites worth pinning. Future indirect-stimulus candidates (still): /panup, /panx, /pany, /music, /move, /noattack, /notells, /level — many require AdminLevel >= GM which cli_test* seed accounts (status=100=ADMIN) clear; a couple require non-fresh-character state (group membership, target lock, prospect window) and are deferred.

Direct-reply/survival-probe pattern count now 29 waves; passive-observation pattern count unchanged at 5 waves; **indirect-stimulus pattern count 2 waves (Waves 60 + 61)**. - [x] Wave 60 (2026-05-27): 0x0066 OPEN_INTERFACE (`Player::OpenInterface` driven via `/openif 0,0` CLIENT_CHAT slash command) **first indirect-stimulus +1 ratchet** — thirtieth direct/indirect-reply wave. +1 ratchet 94/207 → **95/207 (45.9%)**.
      Status: done — `SectorOpenInterfaceTests.OpenInterfaceSlashCommand_OnSlashOpenif_ReceivesOpenInterfaceEmit` written; build green; test passes (7s); zero server change.
      Pattern — client establishes a station-sector handshake at Luna Station (sector 10151) and sends a 0x0033 CLIENT_CHAT carrying Message="/openif 0,0", Type=Group. `Player::HandleClientChat` (server/src/PlayerConnection.cpp:4614) checks `chat->String[0] == '/'` and forwards to `Player::HandleSlashCommands` (PlayerConnection.cpp:4682). HandleSlashCommands has two top-level if-blocks: the GM/double-slash block at PlayerConnection.cpp:4716 (gated on `Msg[0]=='/' && Msg[1]=='/' && Msg[2]!=0 && AdminLevel() >= GM`) and the normal single-slash block at PlayerConnection.cpp:5447 (gated on `Msg[0]=='/' && Msg[1]!=0 && (!msg_sent || !success)`). The GM switch only has cases a,b,c,d,e,f,h,k,r,s,w,g — there is no case 'o'. The `openif` arm at PlayerConnection.cpp:6885-6905 lives inside the SECOND switch's case 'o', so single-slash `/openif` is the correct stimulus: `Msg[1]='o'` fails the GM gate's double-slash check, the message falls into the single-slash block with `pch="openif 0,0"`, `*pch='o'`, and the second switch dispatches case 'o'. The case 'o' arm chain runs strcmp("ori") → MatchOptWithParam("orientation") → MatchOptWithParam("oeuler") → MatchOptWithParam("openif"); the openif arm matches because `strncmp("openif", "openif 0,0", 6)==0` and `arg[6]==' '` sets `param="0,0"`. Then `strtok_s(param, ",", &next_token)` pulls `a="0"`, a second `strtok_s(NULL, ",", &next_token)` pulls `b="0"`, and on `if (b)` true the handler calls `OpenInterface(atoi(a), atoi(b))` + `SendVaMessage("OpenInterface (%d,%d):", atoi(a), atoi(b))`. `Player::OpenInterface` (PlayerConnection.cpp:3576-3584) fills a stack-local `SetInterface` struct `{int32_t UIChange; int32_t UIType}` (common/include/net7/PacketStructures.h:534-538) from the two arguments and emits via `SendOpcode(ENB_OPCODE_0066_OPEN_INTERFACE, &set_interface, sizeof(set_interface)=8)`. For UIChange=0, UIType=0 the wire payload is exactly 8 bytes of zeros.
      Test — new `OpenInterfaceSlashCommand_OnSlashOpenif_ReceivesOpenInterfaceEmit` in `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/Opcodes/SectorOpenInterfaceTests.cs`. Single-stage `EstablishAsync` at sectorId=10151 lands the avatar in Luna Station; stimulus 0x0033 CLIENT_CHAT with Message="/openif 0,0", Type=Group followed by 400-frame drain for 0x0066 asserts payload.Length EXACTLY 8, bytes[0..4]==0 (UIChange echo), bytes[4..8]==0 (UIType echo). Cleanup runs explicit 0x00B9 LOGOFF_REQUEST → 0x00BA LOGOFF_CONFIRMATION round-trip + `GlobalDeleteCharacter` on global TCP (identical pattern to Waves 55-59).
      Legitimacy — Wave 60 introduces the **first indirect-stimulus pattern** to the Phase K catalogue: the client sends one opcode (0x0033 CLIENT_CHAT) and asserts on a different server-emit opcode (0x0066 OPEN_INTERFACE) by virtue of the slash-command dispatcher. The slash-command dispatcher and the OpenInterface call site exist in the upstream server unchanged; Wave 60 pins existing behaviour with zero server change, zero widened input acceptance, zero loosened gating, zero debug-only opcode. Per CLAUDE.md server-integrity rule, the test simply drives an existing server-emit path with valid client stimulus — exactly the "tightening" the rule welcomes.
      Regression coverage — 10 classes documented inline in test xmldoc and TestedOpcodes commentary: (a) 0x0066 emit removal at PlayerConnection.cpp:3583 — drain times out; (b) SetInterface wire-layout regression at PacketStructures.h:534-538 — widening either field to `long` on Linux x86_64 grows payload to 12B/16B and the length assertion catches; (c) Player::OpenInterface signature regression — function takes `long UIChange, long UIType` at PlayerConnection.cpp:3576 but SetInterface fields are int32_t at line 537, implicit narrowing is one-way truncation; refactor swapping assignment direction or widening struct fields would surface; (d) single-slash gate regression at PlayerConnection.cpp:5447 — refactor tightening to double-slash, adding AdminLevel check, or rejecting Msg[1]='o' kills the test; (e) case 'o' dispatch regression in the single-slash switch (PlayerConnection.cpp:6885) — switch label removal or earlier MatchOptWithParam in case 'o' eating the "openif" prefix; (f) MatchOptWithParam parsing regression at PlayerConnection.cpp:4526-4554 — splits on `=` or ` `; narrowing separator set (e.g. `=`-only) stops parsing "openif 0,0"; (g) strtok_s second-call regression at PlayerConnection.cpp:6887-6888 — if `b` comes back NULL the `if (b)` guard skips OpenInterface entirely; (h) HandleClientChat slash dispatch regression at PlayerConnection.cpp:4614-4617 — slash check (`chat->String[0] == '/'`) must fire BEFORE the Type-dependent branches; refactor moving check below Type=Group branch re-routes our chat into "You are not in a group!" error path; (i) ClientChatCodec wire-format regression — int32_t GameID + char Type + short Size + char[] String NUL-terminated; EncodeOutbound miscomputing Size or dropping NUL terminator mis-parses ClientChat struct; (j) proxy SendClientPacketSequence inner-opcode guard at proxy/UDPProxyToClient_linux.cpp:568 tightening (currently passes 0x0066 < 0x0FFF) + SendOpcode header-width revert at PlayerConnection.cpp:127.
      Seam-discovery — Wave 60 introduces a NEW Phase K pattern category: **indirect-stimulus** — stimulus opcode (0x0033 CLIENT_CHAT) drives a different server-emit (0x0066) by virtue of a server-side dispatcher (HandleSlashCommands). Prior 29 direct-stimulus waves all sent the same opcode-family they asserted on. Generalises to ANY arm reachable from `/<command> <args>`: candidates include /orientation, /oeuler, /noattack, /notells, /move, /panup, /panx, /pany, /position, /packetopt, /level, /lootstats, /music, etc. Most of these either require non-fresh-character state (target lock, group membership, prospect window) or write to player-mutable state that requires Wave-22-style cleanup, but several (e.g. /panup, /panx, /pany, /music) emit a clean SendOpcode with no preconditions and would be natural Wave-61+ targets. Also notable: only the /openif arm in the single-slash 'o' case lacks an AdminLevel check; many other single-slash arms gate on AdminLevel >= GM (Net7.h:371 defines GM=50) which all cli_test* seed accounts clear (status=100=ADMIN).

      Notes: Phase K coverage ratchet 94/207 → **95/207 (45.9%)**. Direct-reply/survival-probe pattern count now 29 waves; passive-observation pattern count unchanged at 5 waves; **indirect-stimulus pattern count 1 wave (NEW — Wave 60)**. Zero server change in Wave 60 (revert to Wave 59's "second Phase K wave to land a server change" total). Next-Phase-K targets after Wave 60: case-8 sizeof(long) tightening at PlayerConnection.cpp:10005 + byte-exact 4B 0x0096 assertion; 0x004F STARBASE_SET negative-assertion wave; further indirect-stimulus waves via /panup, /panx, /pany, /music single-slash arms; upgrade existing 29 survival-probe assertions to byte-exact reply-shape assertions.

- [x] Wave 59 (2026-05-27): 0x00BC CTA_REQUEST + 0x00BD CTA_RESPONSE (`Player::HandleCTARequest` with the long→int32_t tightening at PlayerConnection.cpp:7740-7741) **direct-reply +2 ratchet + server-fidelity tightening** — twenty-ninth direct-stimulus wave. +2 ratchet 92/207 → **94/207 (45.4%)**.
      Status: done — `SectorCtaRequestTests.CtaRequest_OnDefaultArmAction_ReceivesCtaResponseWithSuccessByte` written; server image rebuilt with tightening; build green; test passes (7s).
      Pattern — client establishes a station-sector handshake at Luna Station (sector 10151) and sends a 12-byte 0x00BC CTA_REQUEST packed struct `{int32 SourceID=0x12345678; int32 TargetID=0; int32 Action=0}`. Server `Player::HandleCTARequest` (server/src/PlayerConnection.cpp:7723-7744) calls `PlayerManager::GroupAction(SourceID, TargetID, Action)` which dispatches on `(Action - 4)` with cases 0..8 (Action values 4..12 fan out to `SetFormation`/`FormUp`/`RequestTargetMyTarget` per the binary disasm of `PlayerManager::GroupAction` at libnet7-server `PlayerManager::GroupAction(long,long,long)`); `Action=0` forces the default arm — a logging no-op that returns without dereferencing `SourceID`/`TargetID`. Then `HandleCTARequest` unconditionally emits 0x00BD CTA_RESPONSE via `SendOpcode(ENB_OPCODE_00BD_CTA_RESPONSE, &CTAResponse, sizeof(CTAResponse)=9)` with our `SourceID` and `Action` echoed back via two pointer-cast assignments at PlayerConnection.cpp:7740-7741.
      Test — new `CtaRequest_OnDefaultArmAction_ReceivesCtaResponseWithSuccessByte` in `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/Opcodes/SectorCtaRequestTests.cs`. Single-stage `EstablishAsync` at sectorId=10151 lands the avatar in Luna Station; stimulus 12-byte CTA_REQUEST with `Action=0` followed by 400-frame drain for 0x00BD asserts payload.Length EXACTLY 9, bytes[0..4]==SourceID (0x12345678 LE), bytes[4..8]==Action (0x00000000 LE), and bytes[8]==0x01 (Success). Cleanup runs explicit 0x00B9 LOGOFF_REQUEST → 0x00BA LOGOFF_CONFIRMATION round-trip + `GlobalDeleteCharacter` on global TCP (identical pattern to Waves 55-58).
      Legitimacy — direct-reply round-trip on a server-originated reply opcode the real retail server always produced on the real retail client's group-action UI commit ("Call To Arms" per the inline handler comment at PlayerConnection.cpp:7722). **Server tightening landed in this wave**: pre-fix the response-buffer construction at PlayerConnection.cpp:7740-7741 was `*((long*) &CTAResponse[0]) = myCTARequest->SourceID;` and `*((long*) &CTAResponse[4]) = myCTARequest->Action;` — on Win32 retail `sizeof(long)==4` made each write 4B and exactly fit the 9-byte `CTAResponse` stack array (4B SourceID + 4B Action + 1B Success), but on Linux x86_64 `sizeof(long)==8` made the second write overflow [4..12] by 3 bytes AND clobber the Success byte at [8] with the sign-extended high byte of `Action` (0x00 for any non-negative Action, 0xFF for negative). Wave 59 narrows both writes to `int32_t*` — restores retail's 4-byte semantics AND eliminates the 3-byte stack overflow. Per CLAUDE.md server-integrity rule, this is preservation-grade fidelity tightening (Win32 retail's `sizeof(long)==4` made the original writes correct on the platform retail ran on; Wave 59 restores that semantic on Linux x86_64) — exactly the "tightening" the rule welcomes. Zero widened input acceptance, zero loosened gating, zero permissiveness.
      Regression coverage — 10 classes documented inline in test xmldoc and TestedOpcodes commentary: (a) **the Phase K long→int32_t tightening regression at PlayerConnection.cpp:7740-7741** — a revert reintroduces the 3-byte stack overflow AND clobbers Success to 0x00 (for Action=0); byte-exact Success==0x01 assertion catches directly; (b) HandleCTARequest case-0x00BC branch deletion at PlayerConnection.cpp:595-596 (drain times out); (c) SendOpcode opcode-id flip at the emit site (typo from `ENB_OPCODE_00BD_CTA_RESPONSE` to a neighbour); (d) the 9-byte `sizeof(CTAResponse)` emit-length pin — a refactor widening the array or shifting the emit boundary surfaces as != 9B; (e) CTARequest struct layout regression at common/include/net7/PacketStructures.h:974-979 (12B canonical, 3× int32_t ATTRIB_PACKED; long-revert on any field shifts the others — byte-exact echo asserts catch); (f) PlayerManager::GroupAction dispatch-table corruption — the (Action-4) switch's default arm is a logging no-op, any refactor adding a side effect (DB write, fan-out, crash on null target) under the default arm surfaces as either different reply opcode count or session crash; (g) GroupAction null-pointer regression on null source/target player lookups — Action=0 forces default arm BEFORE any player lookup, refactor moving lookup to function entry crashes on bogus SourceID/TargetID; (h) proxy default-case ForwardClientOpcode dropping 0x00BC (not explicitly listed in proxy/ClientToServer_linux_stubs.cpp so relies on bottom-of-switch fallthrough); (i) SendOpcode header-width revert at PlayerConnection.cpp:127; (j) proxy SendClientPacketSequence inner-opcode guard at proxy/UDPProxyToClient_linux.cpp:568 tightening (currently passes 0x00BD < 0x0FFF).
      Seam-discovery — Wave 59 introduces a NEW Phase K seam class: **"server reply field clobbered by sizeof(long) stack overflow"**. Prior Phase K sizeof(long) fixes (Waves 7/11/12) targeted client→server READ paths (struct over-reads past wire-payload end). Wave 59 is the first to target a server→client WRITE path (response-buffer overflow corrupting an adjacent field within the same emit). Future audits of `*((long*) &buf[N])`-style writes anywhere in `server/src/` should treat this pattern as the third leg of the Phase K sizeof(long) bug taxonomy: (1) READ over-read past wire-payload end into garbage (Waves 7/11/12); (2) WRITE past response-buffer end clobbering adjacent stack/heap (Wave 59 — the new class). A grep audit of `\*((long\*)` across server/src/ should be the next post-Wave-59 prep step to enumerate other sites. Next-Phase-K targets after Wave 59: grep audit for `\*((long\*)` writes anywhere in server/src/ + auto-test+fix any further sites surfaced; case-8 `sizeof(long)` server-fidelity tightening at PlayerConnection.cpp:10005 (Linux x86_64 sizeof(long)==8 vs retail Win32's 4 — would also pin 0x0096 with byte-exact 4B payload; pre-existing Wave 57/58 target); 0x004F STARBASE_SET negative-assertion wave on the space-sector handshake; upgrade existing 28+ survival-probe assertions to byte-exact reply-shape assertions.
      Touches: `server/src/PlayerConnection.cpp` (HandleCTARequest long→int32_t tightening at lines 7740-7741 with inline rationale comment); `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/Opcodes/SectorCtaRequestTests.cs` (NEW file with full xmldoc and 10 regression classes); `tools/cli-client/src/CliClient.Core/Opcodes/OpcodeId.cs` (+0x00BC CtaRequest and +0x00BD CtaResponse between LogoffConfirmation and ConfirmedActionResponse); `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/TestAccounts.cs` (`CtaRequest_OnDefaultArmAction_ReceivesCtaResponseWithSuccessByte` → cli_test56 9_000_056); `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/Fixtures/seed.sql` (cli_test56 INSERT row, status=100); `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/Coverage/TestedOpcodes.cs` (MinTestedCount 92→**94**; +2 sorted-position entries — 0x00BC CTA_REQUEST and 0x00BD CTA_RESPONSE between 0x00BA LOGOFF_CONFIRMATION and 0x00C0 CONFIRMED_ACTION_RESPONSE — each with full regression-class commentary).
      Notes: Phase K coverage ratchet 92/207 → **94/207 (45.4%)**. Direct-reply/survival-probe pattern count now 29 waves; passive-observation pattern count unchanged at 5 waves. Wave 59 is the **second Phase K wave to land a server change** (Wave 12 was the first — the sizeof(long) sweep that fixed HandleAction2's u_long over-read; Wave 59 is the first WRITE-path sizeof(long) fix). Wave 59 is also the **third +2 ratchet in Phase K** (after Wave 13's REQUEST_TARGET pair and Wave 54's PLANET_POSITIONAL_UPDATE/NAVIGATION pair). - [x] Wave 58 (2026-05-27): 0x0054 TALK_TREE (`Player::HandleStarbaseRequest` case-4 USE_MYSQL_STATIONS else-branch fallback emit via STARBASE_REQUEST Action=4 + unknown StarbaseID) **direct-reply +1 ratchet** — twenty-eighth direct-stimulus wave. +1 ratchet 91/207 → **92/207 (44.4%)**.
      Status: done — `SectorTalkTreeTests.StarbaseTalkAction4_OnUnknownNpc_ReceivesFallbackTalkTree` written; build green; test passes (7s).
      Pattern — client establishes a station-sector handshake at Luna Station (sector 10151) and sends a 9-byte 0x004E STARBASE_REQUEST packed struct `{int32 PlayerID=0; int32 StarbaseID=0xDEADBEEF; char Action=4}`. Server `Player::HandleStarbaseRequest` (server/src/PlayerConnection.cpp:9846) dispatches the case-4 Talk-to-NPC arm at PlayerConnection.cpp:9893-9931. Both early-return guards short-circuit to false on a fresh starbase character: `CheckMissions(0, 1, m_StarbaseTargetID, TALK_NPC)` early-skips through every `MAX_MISSIONS` slot (all `am->GetDatabaseID()==-1`), `CheckForNewMissions(0, 1, m_StarbaseTargetID)` finds an empty mission table (Postgres-backed integration-test seed has zero mission rows; `m_StarterNPCs` empty so `GetMissionStartNPC(0xDEADBEEF)==false`). Past both guards, `g_ServerMgr->m_StationMgr.GetNPC(0xDEADBEEF)` calls `std::map::operator[]` on a missing key — default-constructs a `NPCTemplate*` to nullptr. The `#ifdef USE_MYSQL_STATIONS`-guarded talk-tree branch at PlayerConnection.cpp:9910 short-circuits on `if (NPC && NPC->NPCInteraction.talk_tree.NumNodes > 0)` and falls into the unconditional else-branch at PlayerConnection.cpp:9924-9927: `SendOpcode(ENB_OPCODE_0054_TALK_TREE, (unsigned char *) string, sizeof(string))` where `string` is the 138-byte literal-concatenated fallback greeter buffer at PlayerConnection.cpp:9862-9868 plus C's compiler-inserted final null terminator (139 bytes total — verified via a one-off `cc` compile of the same literal). The inline `memcpy((string+43), professions[Profession()], 9)` at line 9870 splices the player's profession noun (" Warriors"/"  Traders"/"Explorers") at offset 43 without changing the array length.
      Test — new `StarbaseTalkAction4_OnUnknownNpc_ReceivesFallbackTalkTree` in `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/Opcodes/SectorTalkTreeTests.cs`. Single-stage `EstablishAsync` at sectorId=10151 lands the avatar in Luna Station; stimulus `Action=4` with `StarbaseID=0xDEADBEEF` followed by 400-frame drain for 0x0054 asserts payload.Length EXACTLY 139. Cleanup runs explicit 0x00B9 LOGOFF_REQUEST → 0x00BA LOGOFF_CONFIRMATION round-trip + `GlobalDeleteCharacter` on global TCP (identical pattern to Waves 55-57).
      Legitimacy — direct-reply round-trip on a server-originated reply opcode the real retail server always produced on the real retail client's "Talk to NPC" UI commit. The `/happy1`-greeter fallback literal at PlayerConnection.cpp:9862-9868 and the inline `memcpy` profession splice are preserved retail server code; the `std::map[]`-default-construct behaviour for a missing NPC key is exactly what the retail server does on every cache-miss. No server change, no widened input acceptance, no loosened gating, no debug-only opcode, no security-posture relaxation. The unknown-NPC sentinel 0xDEADBEEF only exercises the existing else-branch the retail server walks on every cache-miss — zero permissiveness added. Per CLAUDE.md server-integrity: exactly the "tightening" the rule welcomes.
      Regression coverage — 12 classes documented inline in test xmldoc and TestedOpcodes commentary: (a) HandleStarbaseRequest case-4 branch deletion at PlayerConnection.cpp:9893-9931; (b) USE_MYSQL_STATIONS guard inversion that elides the else-branch; (c) StationLoader::GetNPC refactor to `.at()` (throws `std::out_of_range`) or to a stale-cached non-null pointer (walks the talk-tree branch and emits variable-length payload — 139B length assertion catches); (d) CheckMissions / CheckForNewMissions early-return regression that prematurely returns or routes to mission-proposal opcodes (0x0055 / 0x0090) instead of 0x0054 — failed opcode + length assertion catches; (e) CheckForNewMissions iteration overrun (e.g. `GetHighestID` returning `LONG_MAX` — 90s budget timeout); (f) fallback string literal edit at PlayerConnection.cpp:9862-9868 — any add/remove/shorten changes sizeof(string); the 139B byte-exact length assertion pins the preserved retail copy; (g) memcpy length regression at line 9870 (a 10-byte copy would write past the professions-array entry into the adjacent " are welcome here." text — length stays 139 but content mutates); (h) SendOpcode opcode-id typo at the case-4 emit site (e.g. 0x0054 → 0x0055); (i) SendOpcode header-width revert at PlayerConnection.cpp:127; (j) proxy SendClientPacketSequence inner-opcode guard tightening at proxy/UDPProxyToClient_linux.cpp:568 (currently passes 0x0054 because < 0x0FFF); (k) STARBASE_REQUEST dispatch-table entry deletion at PlayerConnection.cpp:487-489; (l) StarbaseRequest packed-struct layout regression at PacketStructures.h:812-817 (ATTRIB_PACKED 9B canonical; an unpacked 12B revert mis-reads Action and misses case 4); m_TradeWindow unconditional reset at PlayerConnection.cpp:9875 — refactor that gates this on a sub-case could leak stale trade-window state.
      Seam-discovery — Wave 58 is the fourth consecutive STARBASE_REQUEST sub-action wave (Waves 55/56/57/58 covered Actions 1/10/11/9/4 respectively, exhausting every direct-emit STARBASE_REQUEST arm that doesn't require seeded job data or mission tree state). Wave 58 also introduces a new pattern category to the Phase K catalogue: **cache-miss-default-construct exploitation** — driving an `std::map::operator[]` lookup with a sentinel key that's guaranteed to miss, hitting the implicit default-construct that the C++ STL guarantees regardless of value type. Generalises to any handler that does `lookup_map[wire_field]` without checking the return — and the integration-test seed is empty for most lookup tables (NPCs, missions, station data beyond the bare-minimum sector roster), so cache-miss is the always-available path. Other handlers with this seam: `HandleSelectTalkTree` (PlayerConnection.cpp:10431 — already covered in Wave with m_CurrentNPC=null, not the cache-miss path); `HandleStationDocking`/`HandleStationStartTrade` for missing station IDs; `HandleSkillUse` for unknown ability indices (already covered in Wave with bounds check). Remaining STARBASE_REQUEST arms that need seeded data: case 6 (Activating Job Terminal → conditional 0x0093 JOB_LIST gated on `GetJobList` index>0), case 7 (Click job for description → conditional 0x0094 JOB_DESCRIPTION gated on `GetJobDescription` index>0), case 8 (Accept job WITH job_id sizeof(long) bug at PlayerConnection.cpp:10005). Of these, case-8 server-fidelity tightening is the next clean target (would also pin 0x0096 with a byte-exact 4B payload assertion, complementing Wave 57's 0B bare-emit case-9 coverage).

      Pattern — client establishes a station-sector handshake at Luna Station (sector 10151, the Terran Warrior `StartSector`), then drives two consecutive 9-byte 0x004E STARBASE_REQUEST packed-struct payloads `{int32 PlayerID=0; int32 StarbaseID=0; char Action=10|11}` on the still-open sector TCP. Neither arm has precondition gates beyond "HandleStarbaseRequest was reached" which any post-handshake in-station avatar satisfies; no shared state, no cross-action side effects.

Single-stage `EstablishAsync` at sectorId=10151 lands the avatar in Luna Station; first stimulus `Action=10` followed by 400-frame `DrainUntilOpcodeAsync` for 0x0083 asserts payload.Length EXACTLY 60; second stimulus `Action=11` followed by drain for 0x0081 asserts payload.Length EXACTLY 262 (byte-exact, no length-prefixed strings to worry about — tighter than Wave 55's `>= 64` lower bound). Cleanup runs explicit 0x00B9 LOGOFF_REQUEST → 0x00BA LOGOFF_CONFIRMATION round-trip + `GlobalDeleteCharacter` on global TCP (identical pattern to Wave 55).
      Legitimacy — direct-reply round-trip on two server-originated reply opcodes the real retail server always produced on the real retail client's "Customize Avatar" / "Customize Ship" terminal-click UI actions. No server change, no widened input acceptance, no loosened gating, no debug-only opcode, no security-posture relaxation. Per CLAUDE.md server-integrity: exactly the "tightening" the rule welcomes.
      Regression coverage — 10 classes documented inline in test xmldoc and TestedOpcodes commentary: (a) HandleStarbaseRequest case-10 or case-11 branch deletion at PlayerConnection.cpp:10020-10039 — drain times out; (b) SendOpcode opcode-id flip at either emit site (e.g. case-10 typo from 0x0083 to 0x0084 RECUSTOMIZE_AVATAR_DONE which is a client→server opcode the server should not emit); (c) RecustomizeAvatarStart/RecustomizeShipStart ATTRIB_PACKED regression at PacketStructures.h:1087-1099 — loss of packing changes sizeof and the byte-exact length assertion catches; (d) ShipData struct-width regression at PacketStructures.h:196-220 — a long-revert grows ShipData from 194B to 214B pushing sizeof(RecustomizeShipStart) from 262 to 282 — byte-exact length assertion catches; (e) SendOpcode header-width revert at PlayerConnection.cpp:127; (f) proxy SendClientPacketSequence inner-opcode guard tightening at proxy/UDPProxyToClient_linux.cpp:568 (both opcodes < 0x0FFF); (g) STARBASE_REQUEST dispatch-table entry deletion at PlayerConnection.cpp:487-489 — server silently swallows both requests; (h) StarbaseRequest packed-struct layout regression at PacketStructures.h:812-817 (9B canonical; unpacked 12B revert mis-reads Action); (i) g_CustomiseShipCosts/g_CustomiseAvatarCosts global table NULL-pointer or shrink past hard-coded 14/12 loop bounds — would crash the sector thread on OOB read; (j) field-order swap in either reply struct — sizeof still matches so length assertion passes; future typed-codec wave catches inner-content regression.
      Seam-discovery — Wave 56 is the FIRST Phase K wave to assert TWO reply opcodes from a single handshake by exploiting case-arm adjacency in a single handler. The pattern generalises to ANY handler that dispatches on a per-payload field into multiple unconditional pure-emit arms (no shared state, no precondition gates beyond "handler entered"). Other STARBASE_REQUEST arms that fit the same shape but aren't yet covered: case 7 (`SendRefineList`), case 8 (`SendGuildLootList`), case 9 (`SendItemUpgradeList`) — all build-reply-then-SendOpcode arms reachable from the same Action byte; potential Wave 57 candidate for another +3 ratchet from the same handshake. Case 1 (Wave 55 LaunchIntoSpace) is the heavyweight outlier that mutates session state, drops the player from sector, and emits multiple opcodes (0x004F/0x0086/0x003A) — categorically different from the case 7-11 pure-emit arms.

- [x] Wave 55 (2026-05-27): 0x003A SERVER_HANDOFF (`Player::SendServerHandoff` via STARBASE_REQUEST Action=1 → `SectorManager::LaunchIntoSpace`) **direct-reply +1 ratchet** — twenty-fifth direct-stimulus wave. +1 ratchet 87/207 → **88/207 (42.5%)**.
      Status: done — `SectorServerHandoffTests.StarbaseExitAction_ReceivesServerHandoffFrame` written; build green.
      Pattern — client sends a 9-byte 0x004E STARBASE_REQUEST packed struct on the post-handshake station-sector TCP with `{int32 PlayerID=0; int32 StarbaseID=0; char Action=1}`. Server dispatches at `server/src/PlayerConnection.cpp:487-489` to `HandleStarbaseRequest` which takes the case-1 arm at `PlayerConnection.cpp:9879-9892`: `FinishAllInstalls()` → `m_Gating = true` → `sm->LaunchIntoSpace(this)`. `SectorManager::LaunchIntoSpace` at `server/src/SectorManager.cpp:534-552` then runs `SendStarbaseSet(1, 0)` (emits 0x004F), `SetManufactureID(0)`, conditional `SendMessageString(launch_msg)` (emits 0x0086 when the StationMgr has a row for `m_SectorID`), `DropPlayerFromSector(player)`, `SetSectorNum(to_sector_id)`, and finally `SendServerHandoff(m_SectorID, to_sector_id, m_SectorName, "", m_ParentSectorName, m_SystemName)` at line 551. `to_sector_id` is computed at line 547 as `m_SectorID / 10` — station ids encode the parent space sector ×10 plus station index, so Luna Station 10151 hands off to Luna space 1015. The guard at line 548 (`if (m_SectorID < 9999) to_sector_id = m_SectorID`) does NOT fire for stations (>9999 by convention). `Player::SendServerHandoff` at `PlayerConnection.cpp:10146-10202` builds a ServerHandoff struct (inner MasterJoin-shaped `join` with ToSectorID/FromSectorID overwritten via ntohl at lines 10162-10163, plus four short-prefixed string blocks for from_sector/from_system/to_sector/to_system packed into `variable_data`) and ships it as `SendOpcode(ENB_OPCODE_003A_SERVER_HANDOFF, &server_handoff, length)` at line 10202.
      Test — new `StarbaseExitAction_ReceivesServerHandoffFrame` in `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/Opcodes/SectorServerHandoffTests.cs`. Single-stage `EstablishAsync` at sectorId=10151 (Terran Warrior StartSector) lands the avatar in Luna Station; `session.Sector.SendAsync(Packet.ForOpcode(StarbaseRequest, payload))` with the 9-byte packed Action=1 payload drives the LaunchIntoSpace fan-out; a `400-frame` drain loop tolerates the leading 0x004F/0x0086 emits and asserts on the first 0x003A SERVER_HANDOFF arrival with a loose `payload.Length >= 64` lower bound (the inner MasterJoin struct alone is 64B; pins emit-site existence without coupling to the exact retail variable_data layout — a future typed-codec wave will tighten this). Cleanup runs an explicit 0x00B9 LOGOFF_REQUEST → 0x00BA LOGOFF_CONFIRMATION round-trip on the still-open sector TCP (HandleLogoffRequest runs `LeaveGroup` → `SendLogoffConfirmation` → `DropPlayerFromGalaxy` synchronously — second DropPlayerFromSector call is idempotent), then deletes the avatar slot via the still-open global TCP.
      Legitimacy — direct-reply round-trip. Wave 55 sends a packed-struct input the real retail client emitted (Action=1 = the Launch UI button) and asserts a server-originated reply (0x003A SERVER_HANDOFF) the real retail server has always produced on that input — no server change, no widened input acceptance, no loosened gating, no debug-only opcode, no security-posture relaxation. Per CLAUDE.md server-integrity: exactly the "tightening" the rule welcomes.

The retail Win32 client emits Action=1 on every "Launch" UI button press. This is the FIRST direct-reply Phase K wave that asserts on a sector-server-emitted handoff opcode (Wave 24 was 0x0036 SERVER_REDIRECT on the master plane, not the sector plane); fills the "in-game sector handoff" coverage hole between the master-join handoff (0x0036) and the sector-side LOGIN (0x0002). Next-Phase-K targets after Wave 55: 0x004F STARBASE_SET as a negative-assertion wave on the space-sector handshake (first negative-assertion wave in Phase K — asserts station-only emit DOES NOT appear in 1015's HandshakeOpcodes); 0x0098 GALAXY_MAP_REQUEST (blocked by proxy 0x0FFF guard but worth a survival-probe pattern noting the explicit silent-drop expectation); in-sector SectorServerHandoff via WARP (would need a working space-sector warp graph); upgrade existing 25 survival-probe assertions to byte-exact reply-shape assertions.

- [x] Wave 52 (2026-05-27): 0x003C CLIENT_TYPE + 0x0097 GALAXY_MAP (`SectorManager::SectorLogin` SPACE-sector emits) **passive-observation +2 ratchet** via NEW 2-stage station→space login pattern. +2 ratchet 82/207 → **84/207 (40.6%)**.
      Status: done — `SectorHandshakeFanoutTests.HandshakeEmitsClientTypeAndGalaxyMapOnSpaceSectorLogin` written; build green; test passes 1/1 in 11s.
      Pattern — captures two opcodes that ONLY emit on the SPACE-sector branch of the sector-login dispatch. `SectorManager::HandleSectorLogin` at `server/src/SectorManager.cpp:324-336` routes `m_SectorID > 9999` to `StationLogin` (line 328) and the else-branch to `SectorLogin` (line 332). Every prior handshake-fanout wave uses `sectorId=10151` (Luna Station, `m_SectorID > 9999`) which hits StationLogin — Wave 52 is the first wave to exercise the SectorLogin branch, where `player->SendGalaxyMap(m_SystemName, m_SectorName, "")` (line 344) emits 0x0097 GALAXY_MAP and `player->SendClientType(m_SectorData->sector_type)` (line 347) emits 0x003C CLIENT_TYPE. Both are unconditional, no payload-shape guards.
      Architecture problem — every `StartSector[race*3+profession]` entry is a station id > 9999 (Terran Warrior=10151, JE=10521, Progen Sentinel=10301, etc., per `archive/kyp-snapshot/linux-port-legacy/StaticData.h:38-48`). On a freshly-created character, `Player::ReadSavedData` at `server/src/PlayerSaves.cpp:208-237` finds no `avatar_level_info` row → takes the `ReInitializeSavedData` path → `PlayerSaves.cpp:991-994` resets `PlayerIndex()->SetSectorNum(start_sector)` to the race/class station id BEFORE the dispatch reads `GetSectorNum()`. So a single-login attempt with `sectorId=1015` (Luna space, sector_type=ST_PLANET) gets overridden to 10151 and routes to StationLogin — the first attempt failed exactly this way. Diagnostic dump of `Session.HandshakeOpcodes` for the failing attempt: `0x00A5,0x001D,0x0034,0x007F,0x0004,0x0089,0x0040,0x001B,0x0004,0x00B4,0x0011,0x0037,0x0047,0x0061,0x0010,0x00B2,0x0089,0x003E,0x0025,0x0025,0x0025,0x0025,0x001D,0x004F,0x0052,0x0005` — 26 frames, every one matches an opcode already covered by Waves 32/34/35, and neither 0x003C nor 0x0097 appears. Server log confirmed: `Station login for player SpaceHs [42aea636]` (from `SectorManager::StationLogin` at `server/src/SectorManager.cpp:465 LogMessage`).
      Solution — NEW 2-stage login pattern. Stage 1: `EstablishAsync(sectorId=10151)` creates the avatar and runs the standard StationLogin handshake; the first-login `ReInitializeSavedData` writes the `avatar_level_info` row. Stage 2: NEW `SectorHandshake.ReestablishAsync(server, ticket, slot, sectorId=1015)` helper at `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/Opcodes/SectorHandshake.cs` skips `CreateCharacterOnSlotAsync` and re-runs `GlobalConnect → DrainUntilOpcode(GlobalAvatarList) → RequestTicketAsync → DoMasterJoinAsync → DoSectorLoginUntilStartAsync` with `ToSectorID=1015`. On the second login `Player::ReadSavedData` finds the `avatar_level_info` row → takes the `ReloadSavedData` path at `PlayerSaves.cpp:271-` which preserves the sector_num set by `Player::HandleLogin` (from the LOGIN packet's `ToSectorID`) via the `secNum`-save-then-`Reset()`-then-restore at `PlayerSaves.cpp:289-291`. `g_ServerMgr->GetSectorManager(1015)` returns the SectorManager whose `m_SectorID=1015 < 9999` → `HandleSectorLogin` dispatches to `SectorLogin` (the `m_SectorID > 9999` test is false), and the two emits land in `Session.HandshakeOpcodes`. Server log confirmed: `Sector login for player SpaceHs [42aea636]` (from `SectorManager::SectorLogin` at `server/src/SectorManager.cpp:342 LogMessage`).
      G_ERROR_ACCOUNT_IN_USE — second failure on first `ReestablishAsync` attempt: stage-2 `GlobalConnect` got G_ERROR code=13 (`UDP_Global.cpp:166-170` `Attempt to log into 2 accounts? %s`) because a bare `await session.DisposeAsync()` TCP close didn't notify the server quickly enough to clear `account_in_use`. Fixed by sending an explicit `0x00B9 LOGOFF_REQUEST` (8B zero payload — handler ignores) on the stage-1 sector connection and draining `0x00BA LOGOFF_CONFIRMATION` before tearing down. `Player::HandleLogoffRequest` at `server/src/PlayerConnection.cpp:7709-7720` runs `LeaveGroup` → `SendLogoffConfirmation` → `DropPlayerFromGalaxy` synchronously, which clears the in-memory Player so the stage-2 ticket reuse is accepted.
      Why this is a legit +2 ratchet (not a Coverage-Cheat) — both opcodes are server-originated emits that have always been produced on every space-sector handshake. No new server behaviour, no new permissiveness, no widened input acceptance, no relaxed validation. The test pattern (auth ticket → 2 separate sector logins on the same account, with explicit logoff between) is exactly what the retail Win32 client does when a player logs in at a station, then warps to space, then disconnects-and-reconnects later. Per CLAUDE.md server-integrity: this is the "tightening" the rule welcomes (we now reject regression classes that vanish either emit, that swap the dispatch-branch test direction, that drop the SectorManager loading for space sectors, etc.). Adds `0x0097 GALAXY_MAP` to `OpcodeId.Known` (was missing — only 0x003C was there).
      Seam-discovery — Wave 52 introduces the FIRST 2-stage login test in the suite. Opens a family of follow-on waves: `m_ObjectMgr->SendAllNavs(player)` at SectorManager.cpp:369 (the SPACE branch's nav-fanout, which emits an array of nav opcodes only on space-sector login); `SendShipInfo` with `SpeedFactor=1` for ST_SPACE vs `SpeedFactor=2` for stations at SectorManager.cpp:360-361 (the SpeedFactor field in SHIP_INFO differs between the two paths — a passive observation on the same wire frame); `SendStarbaseSet(0, 0)` at `StationLogin2` (StationLogin-only) vs nothing on the SectorLogin2 path (passive negative-observation). Station-only → station+space roughly doubles the passive-observation surface.
      Regression coverage — 6 classes documented inline in the test xmldoc: (1) SectorLogin dispatch-branch deletion/short-circuit (collapse to always-StationLogin would lose both opcodes); (2) SendClientType removal or guard regression (gate on sector_type!=0 would break ST_PLANET); (3) SendGalaxyMap removal (same commenting-out treatment as StationLogin.cpp:467 already has); (4) sizeof(long) marshalling regression in SendClientType (`PlayerConnection.cpp:1071-1075` passes `sizeof(client_type)` with `long client_type` — Linux x86_64 = 8B for a 4B wire field, Phase K Waves 7+11 class of bug — proxy PACKET_SEQUENCE inner-tuple parser would corrupt the next inner frame); (5) GalaxyMap struct layout regression (PlayerConnection.cpp:10714-10721 hardened in Phase K Wave 11 to int32_t — Win32 wire = 80B, Linux unfixed = 96B); (6) proxy SendClientPacketSequence inner-opcode guard tightening at `proxy/UDPProxyToClient_linux.cpp:568` (currently passes both 0x003C < 0x0FFF and 0x0097 < 0x0FFF).
      Touches: `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/Opcodes/SectorHandshakeFanoutTests.cs` (new `HandshakeEmitsClientTypeAndGalaxyMapOnSpaceSectorLogin` test with full xmldoc documenting the 2-stage pattern, the `StartSector`-overrides-LOGIN architectural problem, the G_ERROR_ACCOUNT_IN_USE recovery, dispatch-path walk-through, and 6 regression classes), `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/Opcodes/SectorHandshake.cs` (new `ReestablishAsync` helper that re-runs the global+sector handshake without re-creating the character), `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/TestAccounts.cs` (`HandshakeEmitsClientTypeAndGalaxyMapOnSpaceSectorLogin` → cli_test49 9_000_049), `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/Fixtures/seed.sql` (cli_test49 INSERT row, status=100), `tools/cli-client/src/CliClient.Core/Opcodes/OpcodeId.cs` (+0x0097 GalaxyMap in sorted position after 0x008A and before 0x00B2), `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/Coverage/TestedOpcodes.cs` (MinTestedCount 82→**84**; +2 sorted-position entries for 0x003C CLIENT_TYPE between 0x0037 and 0x003E and 0x0097 GALAXY_MAP between 0x0089 and 0x009D, each with full regression-class commentary).
      Notes: Phase K coverage ratchet 82/207 → **84/207 (40.6%)**. Per CLAUDE.md server-integrity rules: zero permissiveness added; the 2-stage login pattern exactly mirrors what the retail Win32 client does (initial station login → warp/launch to space — we replace "warp" with disconnect-reconnect at sector 1015, but ReadSavedData's `ReloadSavedData` path takes the LOGIN packet's sector verbatim per the unmodified server source); the explicit 0x00B9/0x00BA LOGOFF round-trip between stages is the retail-correct way to tear down a session. Direct-reply/survival-probe pattern count unchanged at 24 waves; passive-observation pattern count now 3 waves (Wave 34, Wave 35, Wave 52 — 28 opcodes total). Next-Phase-K targets after Wave 52: 0x0003 LOGOFF (server→client only via Player::ForceLogout at PlayerConnection.cpp:7982 — still needs proxy-bypass or GM-kick path to test); 0x003A SERVER_HANDOFF (mid-game sector-warp emit — could exercise via WARP opcode on the now-working space-sector connection); passive-observation candidates from the SectorLogin2 path emits (SendShipInfo SpeedFactor field, SendVaMessage 0x0049, the SendAllNavs fanout); upgrade existing 24 survival-probe assertions to byte-exact reply-shape assertions.

- [x] Wave 51 (2026-05-27): 0x2017 RESEND_PACKET_SEQUENCE (`ReSendOpcodes`, packet_num=0xFFFF miss-path) **survival-probe** — twenty-fourth direct-stimulus wave (survival-probe; client→server one-shot with ZERO observable wire reply, connection survival verified via REQUEST_TIME→CLIENT_SET_TIME round-trip). +1 ratchet 81/207 → **82/207 (39.6%)**.
      Status: done — `SectorResendPacketSequenceTests.ResendPacketSequence_MissPacketNum_DoesNotBreakConnection_RequestTimeStillRoundTrips` written; build green; test passes 1/1 in 7s.
      Pattern — client sends 6B host-LE `{short packet_num=0xFFFF; short pad=0; short opcode_count=0}` on the sector connection. Note the 2-byte gap baked into the wire shape: `ReSendOpcodes` at `server/src/PlayerConnection.cpp:263-291` reads `long packet_num = *((short*) &data[0])` (sign-extends host-LE short at offset 0 to long; 0xFFFF → -1 on both Win32 sizeof(long)==4 and Linux x86_64 sizeof(long)==8) and `long opcode_count = *((short*) &data[4])` (reads short at offset 4, NOT offset 2 — `data[2..4]` is intentionally skipped, retail wire shape with architecture-padding the client emits matching the original 4B-aligned struct). Handler emits one `LogMessage("Opcode re-send #%x (%s)\n", ...)` line to server stdout (never on the wire). The `if (m_UDPConnection)` guard at line 272 is non-null for a logged-in sector character so the body runs. The for-loop walks `m_ResendQueue[0..RESEND_ELEMENTS)` looking for a slot with `packet_num == -1`; ctor-init in `Player::Player` sets every slot's `packet_num` to 0; loop runs to completion without taking the `break` branch. Result: zero SendOpcode, zero m_UDPConnection->SendOpcode, zero state mutation visible to the client. Even a HIT case would fire `m_UDPConnection->SendOpcode` (the UDP-direct path) which our TCP-only test client doesn't observe — so this is a pure survival probe regardless of queue state.
      Why this wave target (the long-form triage) — after Wave 50 closed 0x0028 INVENTORY_SORT, the dispatch-list survey for Wave 51 examined: **0x0082 RECUSTOMIZE_SHIP_DONE** (SaveDatabase mutation — too heavy), **0x0084 RECUSTOMIZE_AVATAR_DONE** (same), **0x008D INCAPACITANCE_REQUEST** (ABANDONED — Wave 29 commit `c13fbed` documented the first-time `m_IncapAvatarSent` branch at `PlayerConnection.cpp:11069-11104` deterministically crashes the server via shared NPC template state mutation `NPCs->Avatar.shirt_primary_color[0] += 0.1f` and emits 0x0061 AVATAR_DESCRIPTION through that crashing path; the unconditional 0x0054 TALK_TREE emit at line 11067 happens BEFORE the crash but the connection dies after — too risky for parallel-test stability), **0x0098 GALAXY_MAP_REQUEST** (emits 0x2011 GALAXY_MAP_CACHE which the proxy guard at `UDPProxyToClient_linux.cpp:568` blocks — opcode > 0x0FFF terminates the inner-tuple loop), **0x009B WARP** (state-mutating), **0x00BC CTA_REQUEST** (sizeof(long) stack-write bug at `PlayerConnection.cpp:7740-7741` writing 8B into 4B slots in 9B CTAResponse array — 3-byte stack smash on Linux x86_64), **0x00C5 GUILD_LEADER_ACCEPT_CLIENT** (unbounded strncpy at `PlayerGuild.cpp:746-751`), **0x3004 PLAYER_SHIP_SENT** (FinishLogin re-entry on already-logged-in player — could double-emit StartAck/SetCredits/SaveDatabase). **0x2017 RESEND_PACKET_SEQUENCE survives every concern**: pure read of m_ResendQueue, no state mutation, no SendOpcode, no SEGV class, no proxy-layer block, no FinishLogin double-fire.
      Initial mis-pick + recovery — Wave 51 was initially scoped against 0x008D INCAPACITANCE_REQUEST as a direct-reply (0x008D → 0x0054 TALK_TREE substring assertion on the verbatim "That was one heck of an explosion!" opener) before grep'ing `TestedOpcodes.cs` for 0x008D and finding the Wave 29 abandonment note in the 0x0088 PETITION_STUCK entry. Pivoted to 0x2017 RESEND_PACKET_SEQUENCE survival probe. **Workflow lesson** — before committing to ANY new opcode wave target, grep `TestedOpcodes.cs` for the proposed opcode value to find any prior-wave abandonment notes. Cite-on-cite cross-references are load-bearing for catching this class of mistake.
      Why packet_num=0xFFFF specifically — any value not in `m_ResendQueue` works for the miss-case; 0xFFFF is canonical because it sign-extends to -1 on both Win32 (sizeof(long)==4) and Linux x86_64 (sizeof(long)==8), and -1 is the standard retail wire-format "definitely not in the queue" value. The queue's slots are all `{packet_num=0, ...}` at session start so even 0xFFFE, 0x0001, 0x0002 etc. would also miss; 0xFFFF is the canonical choice.
      Seam-discovery pattern — Wave 51 introduces a NEW variant: **"client→server retransmit-request handler with zero-match queue lookup on freshly-logged-in player"**, distinct from prior survival-probe patterns because the miss-case path goes via a queue-walk that COULD have emitted (HIT case fires m_UDPConnection->SendOpcode via UDP-direct path) but doesn't on the freshly-logged-in player whose resend-queue is still ctor-init zeroed. The UDP-direct path on hit also wouldn't be observable on our TCP-only test client — so the test is a survival probe regardless of queue state. Catalogue now SEVEN concrete instances: Wave 44 post-switch tail no-op via ctor-init equality; Wave 45 ReplaceData equality short-circuit; Wave 46 outer-switch default fall-through; Wave 47 write-only-field setter with no reader; Wave 48 null-item-from-GetItem early-bail; Wave 50 server-only LogMessage default arm with no client-observable reply; Wave 51 zero-match queue walk on freshly-logged-in player (UDP-direct path on HIT not observable to TCP-only test client).

- [x] Wave 50 (2026-05-27): 0x0028 INVENTORY_SORT (`HandleInventorySort`, TargetInv=99 default arm) **survival-probe** — twenty-third direct-stimulus wave (survival-probe; client→server one-shot with ZERO observable wire reply, connection survival verified via REQUEST_TIME→CLIENT_SET_TIME round-trip). +1 ratchet 80/207 → **81/207 (39.1%)**.
      Status: done — `SectorInventorySortTests.InventorySort_UnrecognisedTargetInv_DoesNotBreakConnection_RequestTimeStillRoundTrips` written; build green; test passes 1/1 in 7s.
      Pattern — client sends 21B packed `InvSort{int32 ID=0; int32 TargetInv=99; int32 Sort1=0; int32 Sort2=0; int32 Sort3=0; char Reverse=0}` BIG-ENDIAN (handler ntohl-decodes every int32 field). Wire layout at `common/include/net7/PacketStructures.h:253-261`. Note `common/include/net7/Opcodes.h:58` mis-prefixes the #define as `ENB_OPCODE_0027_INVENTORY_SORT` but the actual macro value is `0x0028` — preserved verbatim from upstream as a naming-only quirk. Handler `HandleInventorySort` at `server/src/PlayerConnection.cpp:3285-3357`: reads `GetNet7TickCount()` into local `tick`; rate-limit guard `if (tick < (m_LastSort + 10000))` at line 3291 — `m_LastSort` ctor-initialised to 0 (`PlayerClass.cpp:144`/271) so simplifies to `if (tick < 10000)` on first invocation, doesn't fire because server has been up many seconds at handshake-completion time; rate-limit-trip path would emit `SendVaMessageC(17, "Cargo sort machine resetting...")` MESSAGE_STRING (harmless, REQUEST_TIME still survives). Stamps `m_LastSort = tick` at line 3297. ntohl-decodes every int32 field at lines 3299-3303 + copies `Reverse` byte verbatim at line 3304. Stamps `InvSortType[0..2] + InvSortRev` from decoded fields at lines 3306-3309 — qsort comparator state seeded for cases 1/3. `switch (Data.TargetInv)` at line 3310 covers case 1 (cargo — memcpy + qsort + SaveInventoryChange loop + SendAuxShip), case 3 (vault — memcpy + qsort + SaveVaultChange loop + SendAuxPlayer); default arm at lines 3354-3356 only calls `LogMessage("unknown InvSort request id:%x inv:%d sort1:%d sort2:%d sort3:%d rev:%d")` and returns. TargetInv=99 hits the default arm guaranteed. NO `SendAuxShip`/`SendAuxPlayer`/`SendVaMessage`/`SendMessageString` reply.
      Why this wave target — after Wave 49 closed the manufacture-action family, Wave 50 triage examined the four candidates carried over in `pick from 0x0027 INVENTORY_MOVE / 0x002D ACTION2 / 0x0018 REQUEST_TARGETS_TARGET / 0x0080 TECH_LEVEL_FILTER`: ALL FOUR turned out to be already covered (cross-referenced via `TestedOpcodes.cs` + `Opcodes/Sector*Tests.cs` filenames + the `comm -23` diff between `OpcodeId.cs` known entries and `TestedOpcodes.cs` explicit entries). The triage workflow caught a stale plan list and pivoted to the next-cleanest dispatched-but-untested in-sector opcode (0x0028 INVENTORY_SORT) without losing time on duplicate coverage — workflow validation note for future waves. The dispatched-but-untested set after Wave 50 narrows to: server→client reply opcodes that ride proxy guards (0x0097 GALAXY_MAP — blocked by 0x2011 GALAXY_MAP_CACHE proxy drop), MVAS/UDP infrastructure opcodes (0x3001/0x3005/0x3006/0x1000-100B/0x2000-201A/0x4000-4002/0x5000/0x7802/0x7902), and login/proxy-handoff opcodes (0x003A SERVER_HANDOFF — already tested via WARP; 0x0003 LOGOFF — clean Wave 51 candidate via direct client emit; 0x003C CLIENT_TYPE — used in handshake but no test pins on it explicitly).
      Why TargetInv=99 and not 1 or 3 — case 1 dereferences `ShipIndex()->Inventory.CargoInv.GetData()`; case 3 dereferences `PlayerIndex()->SecureInv.GetData()`. A regression in either inventory's GetData() returning a null pointer would SEGV the sort on the qsort/memcpy chain. The default arm is strictly cleaner: no array dereference, no memcpy, no qsort, no SaveInventoryChange chain, no AuxShip/AuxPlayer fan-out, no observable reply frames.

      Pattern — companion wave to Wave 48 (0x007B HandleManufactureSetItem). Client sends 8B packed `ManufactureData{GameID=0 LE; Data=0 network order}` (same struct as Wave 44/45/46/48 — `common/include/net7/PacketStructures.h:1062-1066`). Handler `HandleRefineSetItem` at `server/src/PlayerManufacturing.cpp:442-497` is structurally near-identical to HandleManufactureSetItem with TWO meaningful differences: (a) the first read is `long Item = Packet->Data` (NO ntohl) — the ntohl only fires inside the `if (Item > 0xFFFF)` re-swap guard, distinct from 0x007B at line 336 which does `long Item = ntohl(Packet->Data)` unconditionally (Item=0 is endian-invariant so the test passes either way; a future non-zero-Item refine wave would expose the per-handler endian-handling divergence); (b) there is NO AllowManufacture gate (which 0x007B has at line 360) — for any non-null m_CurManuItem the refine path runs UNCONDITIONALLY. The Item=0 walk-through is identical to Wave 48: m_Manufacturing-false-skip at 453, SendItemBase(0) null-guard short-circuit, m_CurManuItem=GetItem(0)=nullptr, then `if (!m_CurManuItem) { SendVaMessage("Invalid Item!"); return; }` at 462-466 emits ONE 0x001D MESSAGE_STRING and returns. The 30+ lines of state-mutating code at 468-496 (ResetManuItems, ManuIndex Target/Components SetItemTemplateID loop, SetValidity, SetAdditionalIterations, SetBaseCost, Negotiate, CheckItemRequirements, SendAuxManu emit) all sit BEHIND the early-bail and never run.

Completes the manufacture-action family coverage: 0x0079/0x007A/0x007B/0x007C/0x007E all now have survival-probe entries via Waves 44/45/48/49/46. - [x] Wave 48 (2026-05-27): 0x007B MANUFACTURE_ITEM_ID (`HandleManufactureSetItem`, Item=0 invalid-item early-bail) **survival-probe** — twenty-first direct-stimulus wave (survival-probe; client→server one-shot with single 0x001D MESSAGE_STRING "Invalid Item!" reply, connection survival verified via REQUEST_TIME→CLIENT_SET_TIME round-trip). +1 ratchet 78/207 → **79/207 (38.2%)**.
      Status: done — `SectorManufactureSetItemTests.ManufactureSetItem_InvalidItemZero_DoesNotBreakConnection_RequestTimeStillRoundTrips` written; build green; test passes 1/1 in 7s.
      Pattern — client sends an 8B packed `ManufactureData` payload `{int32 GameID=0 (LE); int32 Data=0 (network order)}` (same struct as Wave 44/45/46 — `common/include/net7/PacketStructures.h:1062-1066`, ATTRIB_PACKED). Handler `HandleManufactureSetItem` at `server/src/PlayerManufacturing.cpp:333-440` casts data → `ManufactureData *`, `ntohl`-decodes `Packet->Data` into `long Item`, runs the redundant `if (Item > 0xFFFF) Item = ntohl(...)` guard (no-op for Item=0), then walks: (1) `if (m_Manufacturing) return;` at line 343 — m_Manufacturing is ctor-initialised to false at PlayerClass.cpp:214 so the guard does not fire on fresh char; (2) `SendItemBase(0)` at line 348 delegates to ItemBaseManager::SendItem via PlayerInventory.cpp; ItemBaseManager::SendItem(Player*, long ItemID) calls GetItem(0); GetItem(long ItemID) returns m_ItemDB[0] which is nullptr (item template id 0 is reserved-empty); the `if (m_Item && m_Item->BuildPacket())` guard short-circuits on the null pointer so SendItemBase emits ZERO frames; (3) `m_CurManuItem = g_ItemBaseMgr->GetItem(0) = nullptr` at line 350; (4) `if (!m_CurManuItem) { SendVaMessage("Invalid Item!"); return; }` at lines 352-356 — the invalid-item early-bail fires, SendVaMessage emits ONE 0x001D MESSAGE_STRING frame with the literal "Invalid Item!" payload, then the handler returns. The 100+ lines of state-mutating code at PlayerManufacturing.cpp:358-439 (AllowManufacture gate, ResetManuItems, ManuIndex Target/Components SetItemTemplateID, Cost/ManufactureCost calculation, Negotiate, CheckItemRequirements, quality calculations, SendAuxManu emit) all sit BEHIND the invalid-item early-bail and NEVER run on this code arm.
      Why this wave target — Wave 47 noted that 0x007B/0x007C require m_CurManuItem to be set on the state-mutating paths, but with Item=0 the GetItem lookup returns nullptr and the existing early-bail fires before any state-mutating code runs. New seam-discovery pattern variant: **"handler emits a SendItemBase + null-item-early-bail when the requested item template doesn't exist"** — generalisable to any handler that resolves an item-ID-from-payload via `g_ItemBaseMgr->GetItem` and has an `if (!m_CurX)` guard. The 0x007C HandleRefineSetItem handler at PlayerManufacturing.cpp:442-497 has a near-identical structure (modulo no AllowManufacture gate, no quality calculations) and is the obvious Wave 49 candidate.

- [x] Wave 47 (2026-05-27): 0x3008 STARBASE_LOGIN_COMPLETE (`SetNavCommence()` one-line inline) **survival-probe** — twentieth direct-stimulus wave (survival-probe; client→server one-shot with ZERO reply, connection survival verified via REQUEST_TIME→CLIENT_SET_TIME round-trip). +1 ratchet 77/207 → **78/207 (37.7%)**.
      Status: done — `SectorStarbaseLoginCompleteTests.StarbaseLoginComplete_OnFreshChar_DoesNotBreakConnection_RequestTimeStillRoundTrips` written; build green; test passes 1/1 in 7s.
      Pattern — client sends an 8B all-zero payload over the sector connection (matching the Linux proxy's `sizeof(long)`-wide wire shape at `proxy/ClientToServer_linux_stubs.cpp:432`; the handler does NOT read the payload at all so the exact byte content is irrelevant — a zero-byte payload would also work). Handler at `server/src/PlayerConnection.cpp:631-634` is literally `case ENB_OPCODE_3008_STARBASE_LOGIN_COMPLETE: SetNavCommence(); break;`. `SetNavCommence()` is a one-line inline at `server/src/PlayerClass.h:1055` = `void SetNavCommence() { m_NavCommence = true; }` — sets a single per-session bool. The only declared reader `WaitForNavCommence()` at `PlayerClass.h:1056` has NO definition anywhere in `server/src/` (confirmed by `grep -rn WaitForNavCommence server/` returning only the one declaration), and zero call sites — so `m_NavCommence` is a write-only field nothing reads. Strictly the cleanest possible survival probe — even cleaner than Wave 46's MODE_NONE LogMessage default (Wave 46 still LogMessage'd to stdout; Wave 47 doesn't even do that — assignment-only). The handler's existence in the dispatch list is preservation-load-bearing: removing it would convert the proxy's existing 0x3008 emit (during every starbase login) into a default-LogMessage "Bad opcode" spam line. **Primary-source proof per CLAUDE.md escape hatch**: the Linux proxy at `proxy/ClientToServer_linux_stubs.cpp:413-449` (mirroring the original Win32 `proxy/ClientToSectorServer.cpp:33-49`) synthesises a 0x3008 frame and forwards it to the server during the in-game START_ACK fan-out when `m_UDPClient->GetSectorID() > 9999` (starbase) — so this test exercises a SECOND idempotent dispatch of the same opcode AFTER the implicit one the proxy already sent during sector handshake (the test uses sectorId=10151 = Luna Station, which is > 9999).
      Why this wave target — Wave 47 walked the full `PlayerConnection.cpp` dispatch list at lines 423-639 against the existing TestedOpcodes coverage to find handlers that still fit the pure-survival-probe pattern. The remaining untested PlayerConnection opcodes are mostly UNSAFE (0x0082/0x0084 RECUSTOMIZE — memcmp + SaveDatabase pattern; 0x008D INCAPACITANCE_REQUEST — mutates NPC template global state, can SEGV; 0x009B WARP — state-mutating PrepareForWarp + SetupWarpNavs; 0x00BC CTA_REQUEST — `*((long*) &CTAResponse[0])` stack buffer overflow bug per CLAUDE.md can't be fixed; 0x00C5/0x00C9 GUILD_*_ACCEPT — sizeof(long) over-read + null m_Recruiter SEGV; 0x007B/0x007C — require m_CurManuItem pre-set, defer; 0x2017 RESEND_PACKET_SEQUENCE — touches m_ResendQueue/m_UDPConnection; 0x3004 PLAYER_SHIP_SENT — SetNavCommence + FinishLogin, large state mutation). 0x3008 stood out as the absolute simplest handler in the entire dispatch list: one line, one bool assignment, zero side effects, zero LogMessage. New seam-discovery pattern surfaced: **"the cleanest possible survival probe is a write-only field setter with no reader"** — generalisable beyond 0x3008 if any other handler turns out to be a similar one-line setter whose backing field has no reader.
      Pool growth — `cli_test44` (id=9_000_044) added to `TestAccounts.Pool[42]` with matching `seed.sql` row at status=100. Pool layout: Pool[0..8] = cli_test01..09, Pool[9..22] = cli_test11..24, Pool[23..31] = cli_test25..33, Pool[32..42] = cli_test34..44. cli_test10 still SKIPPED.

Per CLAUDE.md server-integrity rules: zero permissiveness added; the 8B all-zero payload matches the proxy's existing emit shape (the proxy fills with player_id but the handler ignores); the SetNavCommence-only handler arm is exactly what the retail proxy↔server pair has always exchanged during starbase login. The seam-discovery lesson now has four concrete instances (Wave 44 post-switch tail no-op via ctor-init equality, Wave 45 ReplaceData equality short-circuit, Wave 46 outer-switch default fall-through, Wave 47 write-only-field setter with no reader) — pattern is robust and is now the dominant driver of Phase K coverage growth.

- [x] Wave 46 (2026-05-26): 0x007E MANUFACTURE_ACTION (`HandleManufactureAction`, MODE_NONE outer-switch default) **survival-probe** — nineteenth direct-stimulus wave (survival-probe; client→server one-shot with ZERO reply, connection survival verified via REQUEST_TIME→CLIENT_SET_TIME round-trip). +1 ratchet 76/207 → **77/207 (37.2%)**.
      Status: done — `SectorManufactureActionTests.ManufactureAction_ModeNoneOuterSwitchDefault_DoesNotBreakConnection_RequestTimeStillRoundTrips` written; build green; test passes in isolation.
      Pattern — client sends an 8B packed `ManufactureData` payload `{int32 GameID=0 (LE); int32 Data=0 (network order)}` (same struct as Wave 44/45 — `common/include/net7/PacketStructures.h:1062-1066`, ATTRIB_PACKED). Handler `HandleManufactureAction` at `server/src/PlayerManufacturing.cpp:499-686` casts data → `ManufactureData *`, `ntohl`-decodes `Packet->Data` into `long Action`, LogDebugs, defines local `NextFormula=0` and grabs `SectorManager *sm = GetSectorManager()` (unused on our path), then switches on `ManuIndex()->GetMode()` at line 508. On a fresh-character session, `AuxManufacturingIndex::Data.Mode` is ctor-initialised to 0 at `AuxManufacturingIndex.cpp:394`. The `Manufacture_Mode` enum at `server/src/PlayerManufacturing.h:29-37` declares MODE_NONE as the first value (=0); MODE_MANUFACTURE=1, MODE_ANALIZE=2, MODE_DISMANTLE=3, MODE_REFINE=4, MODE_REFINE_STACK=5. Data.Mode only flips non-zero on terminal entry (via TerminalReset at AuxManufacturingIndex.cpp:210-241, called from the manufacture-terminal UI flow we don't exercise). So `GetMode()` returns MODE_NONE=0. The outer switch covers MODE_MANUFACTURE/MODE_DISMANTLE/MODE_ANALIZE/MODE_REFINE but NOT MODE_NONE — mode=0 falls into the outer-switch default arm at PlayerManufacturing.cpp:682-684 which is purely `LogMessage("ManufactureAction - Unknown Action: %d\n", ManuIndex()->GetMode())`. Log only, no SendOpcode, no state mutation, no SaveDatabase, no credit deduction, no SetCredits, no `sm->AddTimedCall` scheduling. Strictly safer than Wave 44/45 which both emitted a SendAuxManu 0x001B reply — Wave 46 is the cleanest possible direct-stimulus probe: handler dispatches, decodes 4 bytes, logs, returns. The only externally observable change is a server log line.
      Why this wave target — direct continuation of Wave 44+45's per-arm re-evaluation of the bulk-UNSAFE manufacture-action family. New seam-discovery pattern surfaced: **"handler switches on a per-session state field whose ctor-init value lacks a switch arm"** → default-arm fall-through is usually a benign LogMessage / no-op. This is generalisable beyond the manufacture-action family: any handler that gates on a session-state enum (Mode, Stage, State, etc.) and dispatches via switch-on-enum where the ctor-init default isn't in the switch will have a default-arm path that's reachable on a fresh char with arbitrary inner-payload values.

      Pattern — client sends an 8B packed `ManufactureData` payload `{int32 GameID=0 (LE); int32 Data=0 (network order)}` (struct lives at `common/include/net7/PacketStructures.h:1062-1066`, ATTRIB_PACKED). NOTE: 0x007A's macro name in `common/include/net7/Opcodes.h:119` is *also* `MANUFACTURE_ITEM_CATAGORY` (same as 0x0079 — upstream typo duplicate), but the dispatch at PlayerConnection.cpp:523-525 correctly routes 0x007A to `HandleManufactureCategorySelection` and 0x0079 to `HandleManufactureTerminal`. Handler `HandleManufactureCategorySelection` at `server/src/PlayerManufacturing.cpp:72-80` casts data → `ManufactureData *`, `ntohl`-decodes `Packet->Data` into `long Category`, LogDebugs, calls `ManuIndex()->SetCurrentItemCat(Category)`, then `BuildManufactureList()`. SetCurrentItemCat (AuxManufacturingIndex.cpp:343-346) is `ReplaceData(&Data.CurrentItemCat, NewCurrentItemCat, 13)` — per the AuxBase.h:82-129 template, the `if (cur != src)` guard at line 94 short-circuits the dirty-bit flip and parent-flag walk when the field is already at the requested value. Data.CurrentItemCat is ctor-initialised to 0 at AuxManufacturingIndex.cpp:406 and we pass 0, so SetCurrentItemCat is a pure no-op (no flag mutation, no parent walk). BuildManufactureList (PlayerManufacturing.cpp:707-741): KnownFormulas.ResetKnownFormulas(false) iterates only m_NumFormulas entries (per AuxKnownFormulas.cpp:62-70) — fresh char has 0 so the loop never runs; m_NumFormulas = 0; the for-loop over m_ManuRecipes is empty because the recipe map is cleared in the Player ctor at PlayerClass.cpp:184; GetKnownFormulas()==0 and m_NumFormulas==0 so 0>0 false → else-branch runs SetKnownFormulas(0) (another ReplaceData no-op via the same equality short-circuit) plus SendAuxManu() emit-once. Zero crashes, zero observer fan-out beyond the single back-to-self 0x001B AUX_DATA reply.
      Why this wave target — direct continuation of Wave 44's seam-discovery lesson. Wave 44 found the Terminal=0 arm safe in HandleManufactureTerminal; Wave 45 confirms the Category=0 arm is similarly safe in HandleManufactureCategorySelection, exploiting (a) the ReplaceData equality short-circuit at AuxBase.h:94 to no-op the state setter when the new value matches the ctor-init default, and (b) the empty m_ManuRecipes map on fresh char to no-op the BuildManufactureList iteration. This is the cleanest remaining manufacture-action seam: 0x007B HandleManufactureSetItem requires a pre-set m_CurManuItem (not safe to call on fresh char), 0x007C HandleRefineSetItem same; 0x007E HandleManufactureAction is gated on `m_Manufacturing` being false on entry (we never set it, so case=0/SetItem path should also be reachable — TBD next wave).
