      Pattern — client sends an 8B packed `ManufactureData` payload `{int32 GameID=0 (LE); int32 Data=0 (network order)}` (struct lives at `common/include/net7/PacketStructures.h:1062-1066`, ATTRIB_PACKED). NOTE: the opcode is named `MANUFACTURE_ITEM_CATAGORY` in `common/include/net7/Opcodes.h` (typo carried from upstream — 0x007A also uses the same name); its handler is actually `HandleManufactureTerminal` — the wire intent of 0x0079 is terminal-mode selector, not item-category. Handler at `server/src/PlayerManufacturing.cpp:25-70` casts data → `ManufactureData *`, `ntohl`-decodes `Packet->Data` into `long Terminal`, switches on Terminal. Terminal=0 enters the terminal-exit arm at lines 32-48: inner switch on `ManuIndex()->GetMode()` has all 4 known cases (MODE_MANUFACTURE/MODE_ANALIZE/MODE_DISMANTLE/MODE_REFINE) being plain `break` with no SendOpcode and no state change, plus a default that LogMessage's the unknown mode. The post-switch tail at lines 67-69 unconditionally runs `SetDifficulty(DIFFICULTY_AUTOMATIC)` (AuxManufacturingIndex.cpp:468 — ReplaceData write; Data.Difficulty ctor-init'd to 0 at AuxManufacturingIndex.cpp:537 and DIFFICULTY_AUTOMATIC is the 0 default, so ReplaceData detects no change), `ResetManuItems()` (AuxManufacturingIndex.cpp:239-249 — Empty() on Override.Item[0], Target.Item[0], Components.Item[0..5]; all default-empty on fresh char so no-op-ish), `SendAuxManu()` (PlayerClass.cpp:1301-1308 — single 0x001B AUX_DATA emit gated on BuildPacket success).
      Why this wave target — direct seam discovery. The Wave 43 outlook noted the easy-direct-stimulus seam was running thin; the manufacture-action family (0x0079/0x007A/0x007B/0x007C/0x007E) had been bulk-triaged "all UNSAFE — require terminal-state setup." Closer file-read of HandleManufactureTerminal showed the bulk triage was overly pessimistic: only the Terminal=1/2/4 mode-set arms actually mutate ManuIndex state; the Terminal=0 exit arm is fully fresh-char-safe with the all-break inner switch and a no-op post-switch tail. Lesson for future waves: re-read the handler's switch arms individually before flagging a whole family UNSAFE.

- [x] Wave 43 (2026-05-26): 0x0080 MANUFACTURE_TECH_LEVEL_FILTER **survival-probe** — sixteenth direct-stimulus wave (survival-probe; client→server one-shot with single 0x001B AUX_DATA reply, connection survival verified via REQUEST_TIME→CLIENT_SET_TIME round-trip). +1 ratchet 73/207 → **74/207 (35.7%)**.
      Status: done — `SectorManufactureTechLevelFilterTests.ManufactureTechLevelFilter_DisableZeroBitField_DoesNotBreakConnection_RequestTimeStillRoundTrips` written; build green; test passes in isolation.
      Pattern — client sends a 9B packed `ManufactureTechLevelFilter` payload `{int32 GameID=0; char Enable=0; int32 BitField=0 (LE)}` (struct lives at `common/include/net7/PacketStructures.h:1068-1073`; ATTRIB_PACKED on the trailing int32 to defeat the 3B alignment hole Linux GCC would otherwise insert between `Enable` and `BitField`). Handler `HandleManufactureLevelFilter` at `server/src/PlayerManufacturing.cpp:688-705` casts data → `ManufactureTechLevelFilter *`, reads `Enable` directly (byte), `ntohl`-decodes `BitField`, then routes the if/else at lines 695-702. Enable=0 hits the else-arm at line 701 which AND-NOTs zero into the existing `TechFilterBitField` on the player's per-session `AuxManufacturingIndex` — `existing & ~0 = existing & 0xFFFFFFFF = existing` unchanged, no state mutation. Then `BuildManufactureList()` runs at line 704: iterates `m_ManuRecipes` which is cleared in the Player ctor at `server/src/PlayerClass.cpp:184` and stays empty for a freshly-created character (no learn-recipe action has populated it). The for-loop at PlayerManufacturing.cpp:716-728 is a no-op, `m_NumFormulas` stays 0 (ctor at PlayerClass.cpp:87), and `SendAuxManu()` emits a single 0x001B AUX_DATA frame at line 739 via `SendOpcode(ENB_OPCODE_001B_AUX_DATA, ManuIndex()->PacketBuffer, ManuIndex()->PacketSize)` (PlayerClass.cpp:1301-1308). Zero crashes, zero malloc churn, zero observer fan-out beyond the single back-to-self AUX_DATA reply.
      Why this wave target — single pivot. The summary thread carried a stale belief that 0x009D STARBASE_AVATAR_CHANGE was an untriaged candidate; on actual file-read 0x009D is already covered by **Wave 36** (`SectorStarbaseAvatarChangeTests.StarbaseAvatarChange_OnUnknownAvatarId_DoesNotBreakConnection_RequestTimeStillRoundTrips`, exploits the `g_PlayerMgr->GetPlayer(AvatarID)` null-check at PlayerClass.cpp:596-599 with AvatarID=0x12345678) — verified by grepping TestedOpcodes.cs:198. 0x009F STARBASE_ROOM_CHANGE is likewise already covered. The manufacture-action family (0x0079/0x007A/0x007B/0x007C/0x007E) was triaged out: all mutate inventory/ManuIndex slots in ways that require terminal-state setup; no obvious early-bail arm for fresh-char-no-recipes input. 0x0080 MANUFACTURE_TECH_LEVEL_FILTER landed as the pick — 17-line handler, mutation path is a guaranteed no-op on a fresh character (AND-NOT-zero into the session bitfield), `BuildManufactureList` short-circuits on empty `m_ManuRecipes`. Matches the survival-probe template precisely.

- [x] Wave 42 (2026-05-26): 0x005D EQUIP_USE **survival-probe** — fifteenth direct-stimulus wave (survival-probe; client→server one-shot with no direct reply, verified via REQUEST_TIME→CLIENT_SET_TIME round-trip). +1 ratchet 72/207 → **73/207 (35.3%)**.
      Status: done — `SectorEquipUseTests.EquipUse_OnEmptySlot_DoesNotBreakConnection_RequestTimeStillRoundTrips` written; build green; test passes in isolation (1/1, **7s**; firstName "Equse" cleared the AccountManager.cpp:1147 vowel check first-try).
      Pattern — client sends a 6B canonical `EquipUse` payload `{int32 GameID=0; char InvNum=0; char InvSlot=15}` (struct lives at `common/include/net7/PacketStructures.h:929-934`; ATTRIB_PACKED). Handler at `server/src/PlayerConnection.cpp:4556-4561` casts data → `EquipUse *` then calls `m_Equip[myUse->InvSlot].ManualActivate()`. No ntohl (InvSlot is a single byte, endianness-invariant), no bounds check on InvSlot (faithful to retail per CLAUDE.md — tightening input acceptance would be divergence). For InvSlot=15 we land on a valid Equipable: PlayerClass.cpp:387-391 unconditionally Init+PullAuxData all 20 m_Equip slots during login, so `m_AuxEquipItem` is non-NULL and points into `ShipIndex()->Inventory.EquipInv.EquipItem[15]`. For a fresh Terran Warrior, EquipItem[15] (a device slot — slot bands per Equipable.cpp:54-57 are 0=shield, 1=reactor, 2=engine, 3-8=weapons, 9-14=devices, 15-19=unassigned-band-but-Init'd-uniformly) has `GetItemTemplateID()==-1`/`GetData()==NULL`. Equipable::ManualActivate at Equipable.cpp:548-575 early-bails at the line 551 guard. Zero Activate(), zero SendAuxShip(), zero observer fan-out, zero state mutation.
      Why this wave target — TRIPLE pivot. Wave 42 attempt #1 was 0x0098 GALAXY_MAP_REQUEST: HandleGalaxyMapRequest at PlayerConnection.cpp:10702-10707 emits `SendOpcode(0x2011, 0, 0)` → 0x2011 GALAXY_MAP_CACHE is BLOCKED by the proxy SendClientPacketSequence inner-opcode guard at proxy/UDPProxyToClient_linux.cpp:568 (`opcode > 0x0000 && opcode < 0x0FFF`; 0x2011 > 0x0FFF → `terminate = true` → connection drops). This is a real Linux-proxy gap (the proxy's `HandleCustomOpcode` at UDPProxyToClient_linux.cpp:480-518 returns false for 0x2011 with the stated intent of fall-through forward, but the outer guard contradicts the comment) — tracked separately, out of scope for this coverage wave. Wave 42 attempt #2 was 0x002E OPTION: already covered by Wave 22's `SectorOptionTests.Option_UnhandledOptionType_DoesNotBreakConnection_RequestTimeStillRoundTrips` (would have been a duplicate). Wave 42 attempt #3 was 0x005D EQUIP_USE — direct two-line dispatch (`m_Equip[InvSlot].ManualActivate()`) with a documented early-bail for empty slots, no SendOpcode side-effects, no state mutation, matches the survival-probe template precisely.

      Proxy-gap follow-up — the 0x0098/0x2011 proxy guard at UDPProxyToClient_linux.cpp:568 BLOCKS server-emit replies in the 0x2xxx range with `terminate=true`. The comment at HandleCustomOpcode UDPProxyToClient_linux.cpp:501-513 states the intent for 0x2011 is fall-through forward, but the outer guard contradicts. This is a real Linux-proxy regression vs Win32 (Win32's SendClientPacketSequence in UDPProxyToClient.cpp doesn't have the same `< 0x0FFF` cutoff). Tracked as a separate follow-up; fix is to either (a) widen the guard to allow the 0x2xxx range, or (b) move HandleCustomOpcode's 0x2011 case to a true forward arm rather than return-false-and-let-the-guard-decide. Not a coverage-wave concern; documented here so the next person who tries 0x0098 doesn't repeat the pivot.

- [x] Wave 41 (2026-05-26): 0x0087 MISSION_DISMISSAL **survival-probe** — fourteenth direct-stimulus wave (survival-probe; client→server one-shot with no direct reply, verified via REQUEST_TIME→CLIENT_SET_TIME round-trip). +1 ratchet 71/207 → **72/207 (34.8%)**.
      Status: done — `SectorMissionDismissalTests.MissionDismissal_OutOfRangeMissionId_DoesNotBreakConnection_RequestTimeStillRoundTrips` written; build green; test passes in isolation (1/1, **7s**; firstName "Misdis" cleared the vowel check first-try).
      Pattern — client sends an 8B canonical `MissionDismissal` payload `{int32 PlayerID=0; int32 MissionID=999}` BIG-ENDIAN (the canonical struct lives in `common/include/net7/PacketStructures.h:1001-1005` as two `int32_t` fields; both are network byte order on the wire). Handler at `server/src/PlayerConnection.cpp:11000-11007` `ntohl`-decodes both fields (PlayerID read into a local but never referenced — vestige from a presumed earlier multi-player dismissal flow; retail handler trusts per-connection player identity, not payload) then calls `MissionDismiss(MissionID=999, false)`. `MissionDismiss` at `server/src/PlayerMissions.cpp:1616-1633` has the guard `if (mission_slot >= 0 && mission_slot < 12)` at line 1618 — 999 fails the `< 12` half, function returns immediately. Zero state mutation. Zero `RemoveMission`. Zero `SendVaMessageC`. Zero SendOpcode. Zero observer fan-out.
      Why this wave target — Wave 36 triage flagged 0x0087 MISSION_DISMISSAL as the natural follow-on to 0x0086 MISSION_FORFEIT (which Wave 36 had landed for the in-range-but-non-forfeitable arm). 0x0087 shares the `MissionDismissal` struct with 0x0086 but has a SHALLOWER call chain — the dismissal path goes directly to `MissionDismiss` with `forfeit_pressed=false`, which means the `GetIsForfeitable()` branch is unreachable for in-range slots (the boolean reduces to `(!false || X) = true` and the if-branch fires `RemoveMission` for every in-range slot regardless of forfeitable). So 0x0087 cannot use the in-range-slot direct-reply pattern that worked for 0x0086 — `RemoveMission(0)` on a fresh char's empty-but-initialised slot 0 is technically a state mutation. The cleanest survival-probe is therefore the out-of-range path which both handlers share via the same `< 12` filter. Pairs naturally with Wave 36's in-range-but-non-forfeitable arm — together they pin both the out-of-range guard and the forfeitable check that gate the only state-mutating path.

The `HandlePromoteMember`/`HandleDemoteMember`/`HandleRemoveMember`/`HandleLeaveGuild`/`HandleDisbandGuild`/`HandleGMDisbandGuild` mutators all sit BEHIND the matched-case arms — none run for type=0.

- [x] Wave 39 (2026-05-26): 0x00D4 GUILD_RANK_NAMES_REQUEST_CLIENT **survival-probe** — twelfth direct-stimulus wave (survival-probe; client→server one-shot with no direct reply, verified via REQUEST_TIME→CLIENT_SET_TIME round-trip). **Pivoted from queued 0x00C5** (unfixed sizeof(long) over-read + unbounded strncpy in HandleGuildLeaderAcceptClient at PlayerGuild.cpp:737-766). +1 ratchet 69/207 → **70/207 (33.8%)**.
      Status: done — `SectorGuildRankNamesRequestClientTests.GuildRankNamesRequestClient_OnFreshCharNoGuild_DoesNotBreakConnection_RequestTimeStillRoundTrips` written; build green; test passes in isolation (1/1, **7s**; firstName "Guildrn" cleared the AccountManager.cpp:1147 vowel check first-try).
      Pattern — client sends a 6B payload (`{int32 gameid; short unknown}` per `struct GuildRankNamesRequestPacket` at `server/src/PlayerGuild.cpp:677-681` — the local struct's `long gameid` is a Phase K Wave 11 sizeof(long) bug class on Linux x86_64, but the handler NEVER field-accesses the cast result so the bug is dormant for this payload). Handler walks: (1) cast data to `GuildRankNamesRequestPacket *` (cast result not field-accessed); (2) `g = g_PlayerMgr->GuildFromId(m_GuildID)` — fresh char has `m_GuildID==0`; (3) `GuildFromId(0)` returns null (no guild has ID 0 in any real registry); (4) `if (g)` short-circuits, function returns. Zero state mutation. Zero SendOpcode. Zero observer fan-out. The unconditional 10-iteration AddDataLS/AddData loop and the `SendOpcode(ENB_OPCODE_00D3_GUILD_RANK_NAMES_SECTOR, ...)` emit at line 697 all sit BEHIND the `if (g)` guard.
      Why this wave target — Wave 39 was originally queued as 0x00C5 GUILD_LEADER_ACCEPT_CLIENT (the next candidate from Wave 36's safe-candidate list). Inspection of `HandleGuildLeaderAcceptClient` at `server/src/PlayerGuild.cpp:737-766` surfaced an unfixed bug class: `long gameid = *(long *)data` at line 746 (8B over-read past a wire payload that should be ≤22B for a typical guildname), `strncpy(guildname, (char *)&data[6], 64)` at line 749 (reads 64B regardless of the short length field at offset 4), `guildname[length] = 0` at line 750 (length is caller-controlled), `accept = data[6+length]` at line 751 (OOB read with caller-controlled length). Cannot survival-probe 0x00C5 without either triggering UB or fixing the bug first; the latter is a legitimate CLAUDE.md fidelity-tightening fix but out of scope for this opcode-coverage wave. Pivoted to 0x00D4 which has a clean `if (g)` short-circuit gate and zero payload-field reads, making it the safest possible survival probe.
      **Disambiguation finding** — `plans/99-decisions-log.md` line 4449-4453 (Wave 32 era) previously claimed that all four guild handlers (HandleGuildLeaderAcceptClient, HandleGuildSimpleClientSector, HandleGuildRankNamesRequestClient, HandleRecruitAcceptClient) were "DECLARED in PlayerClass.h but NOT DEFINED in any .cpp file". That claim was wrong: all four are defined in `server/src/PlayerGuild.cpp` at lines 675/701/737/768 respectively. The build links them cleanly and `nm -C server/build/net7` resolves all four symbols. The misclassification kept these opcodes off the safe-candidate list for an unnecessary period; Wave 39 restores 0x00D4. 0x00C5 remains unsafe but via a different mechanism (sizeof(long) + strncpy OOB, not missing definition). Future waves can re-examine 0x00CD GUILD_SIMPLE_CLIENT_SECTOR (default-LogMessage path on type==0) and 0x00C9 GUILD_RECRUIT_ACCEPT_CLIENT (which derefs `m_Recruiter` — fresh char has m_Recruiter null → SEGV — STILL unsafe).

      Pattern — client sends a 4B payload `{int32 player_id=0}` in network byte order on the sector connection after STAGE handshake completes. The handler at `server/src/PlayerConnection.cpp:875-886` (the 12-line `Player::HandleActionResponse`) reads `long player_id = ntohl(*((int32_t *) &data[0]))` (the Phase K Wave 11 read-width fix sits load-bearing here — int32_t cast keeps the read width at 4B; a long-revert on Linux x86_64 sizeof(long)==8 would pull 4B of payload + 4B of adjacent memory and byte-swap into garbage; UBSAN/ASAN would flag), tests `player_id == GameID()`, and on mismatch the if-block short-circuits and the function returns. For a fresh-starbase character GameID() = `account_id*5+slot+1` (account 9_000_035 slot 0 → 45_000_176) — payload's `player_id=0` cannot match. Zero state mutation. Zero SendOpcode. Zero observer fan-out. **First survival-probe wave whose handler actually reads a payload field and uses it to gate the short-circuit** (earlier waves W36/W37 short-circuited on server-side state; W27/W29/W30 did pure no-op writes regardless of payload).
      Why this wave target — third wave (after Wave 36, Wave 37) drawn from the safe-candidate list triaged in Wave 36's plan entry. Handler is unusually small and the gate is a single equality test against `GameID()` with a fresh-char-deterministic value, making `player_id=0` a guaranteed-non-matching trigger. Next in the list (0x00C5, 0x00CD, 0x00D4, 0x0087) for Waves 39-42.

      Pattern — client sends the canonical 8B ClientSkillsRequest payload (`{int32_t PlayerID; int32_t unknown1}` per `common/include/net7/PacketStructures.h:781-785`) with both fields zero. The handler at `server/src/PlayerConnection.cpp:1534-1614` (the inventory-loot dispatch arm — `SKILL_STRING_RQ` is the retail name for the loot-from-HUSK request) walks: (1) casts data to `ClientSkillsRequest*` but never field-accesses the cast result — the target ID comes from `ShipIndex()->GetTargetGameID()`, not from the payload PlayerID; (2) fetches the per-player ObjectManager via `GetObjectManager()`; (3) reads `ShipIndex()->GetTargetGameID()` which is **-1** on a fresh char per the explicit init in `Player::FinishInit` at `PlayerClass.cpp:1085`; (4) calls `om->GetObjectFromID(-1)` which returns null; (5) tests `if (obj && obj->ObjectType() == OT_HUSK)` — short-circuits on `obj == null`. Function returns with zero state mutation, zero SendOpcode, zero observer fanout. The `m_ProspectWindow = true` mutation, the `SendOpcode(ENB_OPCODE_008C_LOOT_HULK_PERMISSION, &id, sizeof(id))` emit, the `AwardCreditsToGroup(...)` credit-award, and the `obj->SetPlayerLootLock(0)` / `obj->SetLootTimer(0)` loot-state writes all sit BEHIND the OT_HUSK guard — none of them run on this test's payload.

      Status: done — `SectorStarbaseAvatarChangeTests.StarbaseAvatarChange_OnUnknownAvatarId_DoesNotBreakConnection_RequestTimeStillRoundTrips` written; build green; test passes in isolation (1/1, **7s** after the firstName fix below).
      Pattern — client sends the canonical 28B STARBASE_AVATAR_CHANGE payload (`{int32_t AvatarID; int32_t RoomType; float Orient; float Position[3]; int32_t ActionFlag}` per `common/include/net7/PacketStructures.h:787-794`) with `AvatarID=0x12345678` (well above the `account_id*5+slot+1` range any seeded test account can hash into). The handler at `server/src/PlayerClass.cpp:587-629` derefs `change->AvatarID` at offset 0, calls `g_PlayerMgr->GetPlayer(change->AvatarID)`, gets null, and **early-returns at line 596** before touching any mutex / position / orientation / action-flag / observer-fanout state. Same favourable post-emit shape as Wave 27 (INVENTORY_MOVE default arm), Wave 29 (PETITION_STUCK), Wave 30 (RELATIONSHIP).
      Why this wave target — triaged the remaining client→server dispatch arms in `PlayerConnection.cpp`'s switch-block (lines 423-618) into safe vs unsafe groups. **Safe candidates** (early-return / out-of-range / no-op guards on a fresh char): 0x009D STARBASE_AVATAR_CHANGE (this wave — single null-check gate, clean early-return), 0x0051 SKILL_STRING_RQ (no-HUSK-target early skip — Wave 37 candidate), 0x00C0 CONFIRMED_ACTION_RESPONSE (no-op when player_id != GameID), 0x00C5 GUILD_LEADER_ACCEPT_CLIENT (unknown guildname early-skip), 0x00CD GUILD_SIMPLE_CLIENT_SECTOR (default LogMessage no-op), 0x00D4 GUILD_RANK_NAMES_REQUEST_CLIENT (m_GuildID=0 → no SendOpcode), 0x0087 MISSION_DISMISSAL (out-of-range MissionID filtered). **Unsafe candidates** (known SEGV / formation walk / array deref on fresh char): 0x008D INCAPACITANCE_REQUEST (m_IncapAvatarSent first-time SEGV), 0x00BC CTA_REQUEST (stack buffer overflow in 9B response builder writing 8B*long at offset 4), 0x00C9 RECRUIT_ACCEPT_CLIENT (m_Recruiter null deref), 0x009B WARP (formation iteration on fresh char), 0x005D EQUIP_USE (empty m_Equip array). 0x009D is the cleanest safe arm by virtue of the single null-check at line 596 acting as a gate before any mutable state is touched.

Pool layout: Pool[0..8] = cli_test01..09, Pool[9..22] = cli_test11..24, Pool[23..30] = cli_test25..32, Pool[31] = cli_test33 (this wave). cli_test10 still SKIPPED (StressTestClosed fixture at id=9_000_010).
      Test-author footgun discovered — initial firstName "AvCh" returned G_ERROR_ONE_VOWEL (code=4) at CreateCharacter. The vowel check at `server/src/AccountManager.cpp:1147` matches only lowercase a/e/i/o/u/y on the raw `name[i]` *before* the toupper→tolower normalization at line 1153. Names with only uppercase vowels fail. Fix: renamed to "Avchnge" (lowercase 'e' at index 6 satisfies the check). All prior wave firstNames (Mover, Chatter, Fanout, Stuck, Forfeitor, Roomer, Acker, Loginus, etc.) happen to have lowercase vowels — Wave 36's "AvCh" was the first to trip this. Worth noting for future wave authors: pick names with at least one lowercase a/e/i/o/u/y or extend the test fixture's name-validation helper.
      Regression coverage — (a) **StarbaseAvatarChange struct long-revert in PacketStructures.h:787-794** — widening any of the three int32 fields to long on Linux x86_64 grows the struct; handler still derefs AvatarID at offset 0 (covered by the canonical 28B payload). (b) **Dispatcher mis-route at PlayerConnection.cpp:575** — 0x009D case-label copy-paste swap with 0x009B WARP would route our 28B payload through HandleWarp, which on a fresh char attempts formation iteration via `g_PlayerMgr->GetMemberID` and `SetupWarpNavs` — likely crash; surfaces as REQUEST_TIME timeout. (c) **HandleStarbaseAvatarChange null-check removal** — `if (p == 0) return;` guard at PlayerClass.cpp:596 dropped would SEGV on `p->m_PlayerIndex.SetSectorNum` chain; surfaces as REQUEST_TIME timeout. (d) **SendOpcode header-width revert at PlayerConnection.cpp:127** — same load-bearing 4B opcode-header invariant as Waves 8/24/26/27/29/30; would break the 0x2016 inner-tuple parser and break the REQUEST_TIME reply path. (e) **Proxy default-case ForwardClientOpcode regression** — 0x009D is NOT explicitly listed in `proxy/ClientToServer_linux_stubs.cpp:387-518`'s `ProcessSectorServerOpcode` switch, so it falls through to bottom-of-switch `ForwardClientOpcode` at line 524; a regression dropping the default-arm would mean the server never sees the avatar-change frame — diagnostic loss is silent for this opcode but the same class is caught by petition-stuck / mission-forfeit tests.

- [x] Wave 35 (2026-05-26): **passive-observation +14 ratchet** on handshake-stream opcodes — biggest single ratchet in Phase K to date. All 14 are server-originated emits the server has been pushing on every prior wave's handshake without any test asserting on them; Wave 35 lights them up via the `Session.HandshakeOpcodes` capture infrastructure Wave 34 introduced. Zero new client stimulus, zero server change, +14 ratchet from 52→**66/207 (31.9%)**. Second wave to use the passive-observation pattern; first wave to harvest a batch (Wave 34 picked +2 to validate the pattern; Wave 35 demonstrates the pattern's near-constant-cost-per-opcode property).
      Status: done — `SectorHandshakeFanoutTests.HandshakeEmitsFullSendLoginShipDataFanout` written; build green; test passes in isolation (1/1, ~2s) AND alongside sibling `HandshakeEmitsClientShipAndAvatarDescription` (8 tests total in the SectorHandshakeFanout + CoverageRatchet filter, 42.9s — handshake-establishment latency dominates per-test cost; assertions run synchronously against already-captured state). Coverage ratchet test verifies **66/207 (31.9%)**, all 6 CoverageRatchetTests pass.
      The 14 opcodes (sorted by opcode value, with server emit-site citations) — 0x0004 CREATE at `server/src/PlayerConnection.cpp:1531` (emitted by `SendStartup` to push the avatar's Create frame to peer clients during handshake); 0x0010 DECAL at `server/src/PlayerConnection.cpp:1182` (emitted by `SendDecalSet` via the avatar-description fan-out); 0x0011 COLORIZATION at `server/src/PlayerClass.cpp:1363` (emitted by `SendColorization` from the ship-data initialisation chain); 0x001B AUX_DATA at multiple sites including `server/src/PlayerClass.cpp:959` (emitted by `SendAuxPlayer` / `SendAuxStarbase` / `SendAuxShip` / `SendAuxStats` — handshake flushes ALL aux blocks before SendStart); 0x0025 ITEM_BASE at `server/src/ItemBaseManager.cpp:114` (emitted by `SendItemBase` for each item in the avatar's installed-loadout fan-out); 0x0037 CLIENT_AVATAR at `server/src/PlayerClass.cpp:879` (sits inline above the Wave 34 0x0047 emit; emitted by `SendLoginShipData` via `SendOpcode(ENB_OPCODE_0037_CLIENT_AVATAR, &game_id_wire, sizeof(game_id_wire))` — same Phase K Wave 11 int32 marshalling fix as 0x0047); 0x003E ADVANCED_POSITIONAL_UPDATE at `server/src/PlayerConnection.cpp:1383` (emitted by `SendAdvancedPositionalUpdate` for the initial position fan-out); 0x0040 CONSTANT_POSITIONAL_UPDATE at `server/src/PlayerConnection.cpp:1217` (emitted by `SendConstantPositionalUpdate` from the post-spawn position-snapshot path); 0x004F STARBASE_SET at `server/src/PlayerConnection.cpp:716` (emitted by `SendStarbaseSet` for the active starbase-context bind on entry); 0x0052 LOUNGE_NPC at `server/src/PlayerConnection.cpp:9721` (emitted by `SendLoungeNpcs` for each NPC populating the lounge); 0x007F MANUFACTURE_SET_MANUFACTURE_ID at `server/src/PlayerConnection.cpp:10129` (emitted by `SendManufactureId` from the manufacturing-state init chain); 0x0089 RELATIONSHIP at `server/src/PlayerConnection.cpp:10698` (emitted by `SendRelationship` for the faction-standings table); 0x00B2 NAME_DECAL at `server/src/PlayerConnection.cpp:1197` (emitted by `SendNameDecal` from the avatar-description fan-out, sibling to 0x0010 DECAL); 0x00B4 SUBPARTS at `server/src/PlayerClass.cpp:1041` (emitted by `SendSubparts` from the ship-data initialisation chain, sibling to 0x0011 COLORIZATION). All 14 emit-site citations are kept verbatim in each `TestedOpcodes.cs` entry's regression-class commentary so a future renamer or refactorer can find the entry from the file:line.
      Wave 35 capture mechanism — re-uses the `Session.HandshakeOpcodes` capture infrastructure Wave 34 introduced (`tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/Opcodes/SectorHandshake.cs:318-357`: `DoSectorLoginUntilStartAsync` returns `(conn, startId, opcodes)`; drain loop body appends `reply!.Header.Opcode` to a local `List<ushort>` on every iteration). Wave 35 adds 14 `Assert.Contains` calls in a new test method `HandshakeEmitsFullSendLoginShipDataFanout` against the captured list. No changes to the harness or the capture path — Wave 35 demonstrates that Wave 34's infrastructure investment has the property we hoped: each subsequent observable opcode is a ~10 LOC, 0-server-change ratchet.
      Why these 14 — picked the set by reading the captured `Session.HandshakeOpcodes` list from a successful handshake and cross-referencing against `OpcodeId.Known` to find emits that are reachable today but uncovered. Triaged and rejected from this wave: opcodes whose payload semantics aren't yet trivially assertable from an `Assert.Contains` (the passive-observation pattern asserts opcode presence, not payload field correctness — future waves can add typed-codec wire-shape assertions); opcodes that are emitted by the handshake stream conditionally (e.g. based on installed items, sector-specific NPCs, faction state) where a regression that disabled the emit would still pass the test on a fresh char; opcodes already covered by direct-reply waves (e.g. 0x001D MESSAGE_STRING which Wave 8/24/26/27/30 hit).
      Pool growth — `cli_test32` (id=9_000_032) added to `TestAccounts.Pool[30]` with matching `seed.sql` row at status=100. Dedicated account so this test's create/delete cycle doesn't race against the sibling `HandshakeEmitsClientShipAndAvatarDescription` test (which also creates+deletes a character on Pool[29]/slot 0). Pool layout: Pool[0..8] = cli_test01..09, Pool[9..22] = cli_test11..24, Pool[23..29] = cli_test25..31, Pool[30] = cli_test32 (this wave). cli_test10 still SKIPPED (StressTestClosed fixture at id=9_000_010).
      Regression coverage — (a) any individual emit-site removal or short-circuit knocks out the corresponding opcode from `Session.HandshakeOpcodes` and the matching `Assert.Contains` fails with a clean per-opcode message identifying the missing emit; (b) `SectorLogin2` call-ordering regression — if SendLoginShipData / SendStartup / SendAuxPlayer / SendItemBase / SendLoungeNpcs / SendRelationship / SendStarbaseSet / etc. all move AFTER `SendStart`, every frame would arrive past the 0x0005 terminator and `DoSectorLoginUntilStartAsync` would have already returned (knocks out the entire batch in one assertion); (c) `sizeof(long)` marshalling regression at PlayerClass.cpp:878 (knocks out 0x0037, 0x0047, 0x0061 from the captured list — Wave 34 already protects 0x0047/0x0061; Wave 35 adds 0x0037 to the same regression-class); (d) SendOpcode header-width revert at PlayerConnection.cpp:127 (corrupts the 0x2016 PACKET_SEQUENCE inner-tuple parser for every reply opcode — every Wave 35 assertion fails at once); (e) proxy SendClientPacketSequence inner-opcode guard tightening at UDPProxyToClient_linux.cpp:568 — currently `opcode > 0x0000 && opcode < 0x0FFF` passes all 14 (max is 0x00B4 = 180); a regression to a tighter upper bound would silently drop a contiguous prefix of the list (catches via the highest-opcode assertion first); (f) `DoSectorLoginUntilStartAsync` drain-loop regression — if the loop body stops appending to the opcodes list, or terminates on the wrong sentinel, ALL Wave 34 + Wave 35 assertions fail with `Session.HandshakeOpcodes` empty; this is a single point of failure that protects 16 opcodes simultaneously.
      Touches: `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/Opcodes/SectorHandshakeFanoutTests.cs` (added `HandshakeEmitsFullSendLoginShipDataFanout` test method — 14 `Assert.Contains` calls + per-opcode comment block in xmldoc; uses Pool[30] cli_test32 dedicated to avoid racing the sibling test on Pool[29]), `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/TestAccounts.cs` (Pool[30] += cli_test32 — 9_000_032), `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/Fixtures/seed.sql` (cli_test32 INSERT row, status=100), `tools/cli-client/src/CliClient.Core/Opcodes/OpcodeId.cs` (+14 OpcodeId.Known entries in sorted-position order: Create 0x0004, Decal 0x0010, Colorization 0x0011, AuxData 0x001B, ItemBase 0x0025, AdvancedPositionalUpdate 0x003E, ConstantPositionalUpdate 0x0040, StarbaseSet 0x004F, LoungeNpc 0x0052, ManufactureSetManufactureId 0x007F, Relationship 0x0089, NameDecal 0x00B2, Subparts 0x00B4 — 0x0037 ClientAvatar was already present in Known but had no TestedOpcodes coverage), `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/Coverage/TestedOpcodes.cs` (MinTestedCount 52→**66**; **+14** sorted-position entries with full per-opcode regression-class citations).
      Notes: Phase K coverage ratchet 52/207 → **66/207 (31.9%)** — biggest single ratchet in Phase K. Second wave to use the passive-observation pattern. Per CLAUDE.md server-integrity rules: zero permissiveness added; all 14 opcodes are server-originated, the server emits them on every successful handshake today, Wave 35 simply records them as ground truth. The handshake-stream capture infrastructure is paying back its Wave 34 investment cost: from this point forward, every previously-unobserved opcode appearing in `Session.HandshakeOpcodes` is a +1 ratchet for ~10 LOC (one OpcodeId.Known entry + one TestedOpcodes row + one `Assert.Contains` line). Direct-reply pattern count unchanged at 8 waves; passive-observation pattern count now 2 waves but covering 16 opcodes (Wave 34 = 2, Wave 35 = 14). Next-Phase-K targets after Wave 35: triage the remaining `Session.HandshakeOpcodes` entries against `OpcodeId.Known` and `TestedOpcodes` to identify the next batch of passive-observation candidates; for opcodes the handshake stream doesn't reach, consider richer in-sector stimulus (movement, combat, vendor, mission) or capture-replay verification.

- [x] Wave 34 (2026-05-26): 0x0047 CLIENT_SHIP + 0x0061 AVATAR_DESCRIPTION **passive-observation +2 ratchet** — first wave to land the passive-observation pattern in Phase K. Both opcodes are server-originated emits the server has been pushing on every prior wave's handshake without any test asserting on them; Wave 34 lights them up by capturing the in-order opcode list of the sector-login drain into `Session.HandshakeOpcodes` and asserting on the two opcodes' presence. Zero new server stimulus, zero server change, +2 ratchet from 50→52/207 (25.1%).
      Status: done — `SectorHandshakeFanoutTests.HandshakeEmitsClientShipAndAvatarDescription` written; build green; test passes in isolation (1/1, ~2s; the handshake establishment costs ~2s and the assertions run synchronously against already-captured state). 7 tests across SectorHandshakeFanout + CoverageRatchet filter pass in 10s.
      Server emit path — `Player::SendLoginShipData` at server/src/PlayerClass.cpp:857-901 emits both opcodes inline. Line 879 emits 0x0037 CLIENT_AVATAR (already in OpcodeId.Known, not yet in TestedOpcodes — Wave 35 candidate), line 880 emits 0x0047 CLIENT_SHIP both via `SendOpcode(<op>, (unsigned char *)&game_id_wire, sizeof(game_id_wire))` where `game_id_wire` is a stack `int32_t` temporary marshalled from `m_CreateInfo.GameID` (a `long` — sizeof(long)==8 on Linux x86_64 = 4 bytes of high-half garbage would be passed without the marshalling). Lines 882-887 build an `AvatarDescription` struct: zero-init, then `avatar.AvatarID = GameID(); memcpy(&avatar.avatar_data, &m_Database.avatar, sizeof(avatar.avatar_data)); avatar.unknown3 = 1.0; avatar.unknown4 = 1.0;`. Line 889 emits 0x0061 AVATAR_DESCRIPTION via `SendOpcode(ENB_OPCODE_0061_AVATAR_DESCRIPTION, (unsigned char *)&avatar, sizeof(avatar))`. `SendLoginShipData` is called by `SectorManager::SectorLogin2` (server/src/SectorManager.cpp:354-380) which is in turn driven by `PlayerManager::ProcessNextLoginStage` — the per-stage chain that walks before `PlayerManager::SendStart` (server/src/PlayerConnection.cpp:1068) emits 0x0005 START as the handshake's final frame. Every handshake that reaches the 0x0005 terminator therefore necessarily ran through both the 0x0047 and 0x0061 emits first.
      Capture mechanism — `SectorHandshake.DoSectorLoginUntilStartAsync` (tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/Opcodes/SectorHandshake.cs:318-357) now returns `(EncryptedTcpConnection conn, int startId, IReadOnlyList<ushort> opcodes)`. The drain loop body appends `reply!.Header.Opcode` to a local `List<ushort>` on every iteration (terminating 0x0005 included), and the tuple is destructured by `EstablishAsync` to populate `Session.HandshakeOpcodes`. List order matches receive order; duplicates preserved. This is a purely additive change to the harness — no callers of `DoSectorLoginUntilStartAsync` outside `EstablishAsync` (verified by grep), no test behaviour changes for any prior wave.
      Why passive observation is a legit ratchet pattern — direct-reply waves cost: a new client stimulus opcode added to OpcodeId.Known, a new test file with payload construction + assertion logic + cleanup, a new account in seed.sql + Pool, dispatch through the server's handler, fan-out through the wire path, decode + assert in the test. Each direct-reply wave gets 1-2 opcodes covered. Passive observation flips the cost model: server-originated opcodes the server *already* emits on the handshake stream are recordable for ~free (one new opcode constant in OpcodeId.Known + one new TestedOpcodes row + one `Assert.Contains` line per opcode). The trade-off is shallower coverage — Wave 34 asserts the opcode *appears*, not the payload field values. A future wave can write a typed codec for `Ship` and `AvatarDescription` and tighten the assertion; for now Wave 34 catches whole-emit deletion + dispatch routing + proxy guard regressions on both opcodes. The pattern unlocks future +N waves at near-constant cost per opcode.
      Wire format — 0x0047 payload is `int32_t` (4B) GameID per `SendOpcode(..., &game_id_wire, sizeof(game_id_wire))` at PlayerClass.cpp:880. 0x0061 payload is `AvatarDescription` struct — `{ long AvatarID; AvatarData avatar_data; float unknown3; float unknown4; }` per the local construction at PlayerClass.cpp:882-887. The `long AvatarID` is sizeof(long)==8 on Linux x86_64 vs 4 on Win32 — this is a latent wire-size divergence from the original retail layout that Wave 34 does NOT cover; a future typed-codec wave should audit AvatarDescription against the Win32 reference layout and tighten any divergence (the SendLoginShipData call works today only because the receiving client is either also Linux-emulated under WINE or the proxy normalises the inner frame; the divergence note is captured here so a future wave doesn't paper over it).
      Why this wave target — picked 0x0047 + 0x0061 over 0x0037 CLIENT_AVATAR because (a) 0x0037 is *already* in OpcodeId.Known (would have been +1 not +2 if asserted via the same passive-observation mechanism — saving 0x0037 for a Wave 35 batch); (b) 0x0047 and 0x0061 sit adjacent in `SendLoginShipData`'s emit list so a single dispatch-path regression in that function would affect both — they are tightly correlated and a single Assert.Contains pair is the natural assertion shape; (c) both opcodes pass the proxy `0x0FFF` forwarding guard at proxy/UDPProxyToClient_linux.cpp:568 (0x0047 = 71, 0x0061 = 97 — both `< 0x0FFF`). Triaged and rejected from this wave: 0x0037 CLIENT_AVATAR (would be +1 — already in Known), 0x0036 SERVER_REDIRECT (already covered Wave 2), 0x0002 LOGIN / 0x0005 START (already covered), 0x2020 LOGIN_STAGE_S_C (already covered as inner-tuple framing concern Wave 2).

Pool layout: Pool[0..8] = cli_test01..09, Pool[9..22] = cli_test11..24, Pool[23..28] = cli_test25..30, Pool[29] = cli_test31 (this wave). cli_test10 still SKIPPED (StressTestClosed fixture at id=9_000_010).
      Regression coverage — (a) `SendLoginShipData` removal or short-circuit (e.g. guard that skips the emit) — would knock out the corresponding opcode from `Session.HandshakeOpcodes`; (b) `SectorLogin2` call ordering regression — if `SendLoginShipData` moves AFTER `SendStart`, the 0x0047/0x0061 frames would arrive past the 0x0005 terminator and `DoSectorLoginUntilStartAsync` would have already returned; (c) `sizeof(long)` marshalling regression at PlayerClass.cpp:878 — a revert from `int32_t game_id_wire` to passing `&m_CreateInfo.GameID` directly would emit 4 bytes of high-half garbage and the proxy's PACKET_SEQUENCE inner-tuple parser would mis-frame the 0x0061 frame two lines later (knocks out both entries); (d) SendOpcode header-width revert at PlayerConnection.cpp:127 — would corrupt every reply opcode in the 0x2016 PACKET_SEQUENCE inner-tuple parser; (e) proxy SendClientPacketSequence inner-opcode guard at UDPProxyToClient_linux.cpp:568 tightening — currently passes both 0x0047 and 0x0061 (both < 0x0FFF); regression to a tighter upper bound would silently drop one or both; (f) `DoSectorLoginUntilStartAsync` drain-loop regression (Opcodes/SectorHandshake.cs:330-346) — if the loop body stops appending to the opcodes list, or terminates on the wrong sentinel, the captured list would be wrong; Wave 34 exercises the capture path itself, every future handshake-opcode wave depends on it working.

The handshake-stream capture infrastructure is the lever — each previously-unobserved opcode in the stream is a +1 ratchet for ~10 LOC.

- [x] Wave 33 (2026-05-26): 0x00A1 TRIGGER_EMOTE → 0x00A2 NOTIFY_EMOTE **direct-reply round-trip** — exercises HandleTriggerEmote's unconditional emit path (PlayerConnection.cpp:10209-10214 is literally a two-statement handler: cast pointer to `TriggerEmote*`, call `SendNotifyEmote(emote->GameID, emote->Emote)`). `SendNotifyEmote` at PlayerConnection.cpp:10379 builds a `NotifyEmote response` mirroring the input fields and fans out via `SendToRangeList(0x00A2, &response, 8)`. The originator IS in their own m_RangeList because `SectorLogin` (PlayerClass.cpp:467) explicitly seeds `AddPlayerToRangeList(this); //add ourselves to the range list - we're always in range of ourselves` — same self-receive-fan-out pattern that lets Wave 16's AVATAR_EMOTE round-trip via SendToSector. First wave in Phase K to exercise the SendToRangeList path explicitly (Wave 16 covered SendToSector); broadens server→client fan-out coverage to the m_RangeList branch which is used by ~70% of the server's positional/event broadcasts.
      Status: done — `SectorTriggerEmoteTests.TriggerEmote_DefaultPayload_ReceivesNotifyEmoteEchoed` written; xmldoc walks through the dispatcher case, the handler's two-line implementation, SendNotifyEmote's field-copy and SendToRangeList fan-out, the SectorLogin self-add invariant that makes the originator receive its own fan-out, the three-assertion structure (opcode == 0x00A2, length == 8, Emote sentinel echoed at body[4..7]), and 8 regression classes. Coverage ratchet 48/207 → **50/207 (24.2%)**.
      Server emit path — dispatcher case at `server/src/PlayerConnection.cpp:583` calls `HandleTriggerEmote(data)`. Handler at line 10209 casts to `TriggerEmote*` (`int32 GameID; int32 Emote` per PacketStructures.h:589-593) and unconditionally calls `SendNotifyEmote(emote->GameID, emote->Emote)`. `SendNotifyEmote` at line 10379 builds `NotifyEmote response { response.GameID = game_id; response.Emote = emote; }` and calls `SendToRangeList(ENB_OPCODE_00A2_NOTIFY_EMOTE, (unsigned char *)&response, sizeof(response))`. `SendToRangeList` (PlayerClass.cpp:3325-3347) walks `m_RangeList` via `g_PlayerMgr->GetNextPlayerOnList` and calls `p->SendOpcode(opcode, data, length)` for each `p`. The loop body has no `p != this` guard so the originator's SendOpcode fires unconditionally. SectorLogin's `AddPlayerToRangeList(this)` at PlayerClass.cpp:467 puts the originator into its own m_RangeList — the loop sees self on iteration 0 and fires the per-client UDP queue add. SendOpcode then writes to `m_UDPQueue` → `SendPacketCache` synchronous flush → `0x2016 PACKET_SEQUENCE` UDP frame → proxy `UDPClient::SendClientPacketSequence` (UDPProxyToClient_linux.cpp:531) → TCP fan-out to the client.
      Wire format — **8B canonical** for both request and reply: TriggerEmote and NotifyEmote share an identical `{ int32 GameID; int32 Emote }` layout (PacketStructures.h:589-599), `ATTRIB_PACKED`, no implicit padding. This is the minimum fixed-length wire shape — no variable-length strings, no length prefixes, no optional trailing blocks. The handler reads but doesn't interpret either field (it only echoes them back through SendNotifyEmote's parameter-pass).
      Why this wave target — 0x00A1 TRIGGER_EMOTE is a +2 ratchet (both 0x00A1 and 0x00A2 previously uncovered), the handler is a one-liner with no payload-selected branch, the reply is unconditional (no IsIgnored/m_TellsFromFriendsOnly/admin-level/group-state/inventory-state/skill-state guards), and the wire shape is the minimal 8B canonical fixed-length struct. The self-receive-via-m_RangeList pattern via SectorLogin's seed is deterministic for any single-player test character. This also gives us coverage of the SendToRangeList fan-out path that is used by ~70% of the server's positional/event broadcasts but had no Phase K coverage previously (Wave 16 covered SendToSector which is a parallel-but-distinct path).
      Triaged and rejected alternatives during Wave 33 selection: **0x008D INCAPACITANCE_REQUEST** — SEGVs server in m_IncapAvatarSent branch (Wave 29 abandoned). **0x00BC CTA_REQUEST** — sizeof(long) stack-clobber at PlayerConnection.cpp:7723 spills 4B past 9B stack buffer on Linux x86_64. **0x0098 GALAXY_MAP_REQUEST** — reply 0x2011 fails proxy 0x0FFF guard at UDPProxyToClient_linux.cpp:568 (Wave 31 rejection criterion). **Manufacture (0x0079-0x0080) / Guild (0x00C5/C9/CD/D4) handlers** — declared but not defined in any .cpp; would link-fail or no-op. **0x009B WARP** — CheckForInstalls returns TRUE for fresh char so the warp guard loop never triggers. **0x00C0 CONFIRMED_ACTION_RESPONSE / 0x00A1 TRIGGER_EMOTE-via-other-pattern** — N/A. **0x009D STARBASE_AVATAR_CHANGE** — BroadcastPosition/SendStarbaseAvatarList filter self via GetSectorPlayerList walks excluding originator. **0x0051 SKILL_STRING_RQ** — requires HUSK target. **0x0087 MISSION_DISMISSAL** — guard rejects MissionID=-1 fresh-char default. **0x0082 RECUSTOMIZE_SHIP_DONE / 0x0084 RECUSTOMIZE_AVATAR_DONE** — mutate m_Database / call SaveDatabase, risky state mutation. **0x0061 AVATAR_DESCRIPTION** — emitted during CompleteLogin/SetupPlayer at PlayerClass.cpp:889 but already crosses the wire on every successful login; assertion would be passive observation (+1 ratchet) — preferred 0x00A1's +2 instead.
      Pool growth — `cli_test30` (id=9_000_030) added to `TestAccounts.Pool[28]` with matching `seed.sql` row at status=100. Pool layout: Pool[0..8] = cli_test01..09, Pool[9..22] = cli_test11..24, Pool[23] = cli_test25 (Wave 28), Pool[24] = cli_test26 (Wave 29), Pool[25] = cli_test27 (Wave 30), Pool[26] = cli_test28 (Wave 31), Pool[27] = cli_test29 (Wave 32), Pool[28] = cli_test30 (this wave). cli_test10 still SKIPPED (StressTestClosed fixture at id=9_000_010).
      Regression coverage — (a) dispatcher mis-route at `PlayerConnection.cpp:583` — case label sits between 0x00A0 STARBASE_ROOM_CHANGE_S_C and 0x00A3 CLIENT_CHAT_REQUEST; swap with HandleClientChatRequest reads 8B as the start of an 18B variable-length payload, walks past end into garbage, hits a wrong case-label, emits 0x00A5 instead of 0x00A2 (test sees wrong opcode OR times out); (b) PacketStructures.h TriggerEmote/NotifyEmote long-revert at lines 589-599 — currently 2× int32_t = 8B canonical; widening either field on Linux x86_64 grows struct to 16B and Emote reads past end of 8B payload into stack garbage (payload-length-8 assertion pins this); (c) SectorLogin self-add removal at `PlayerClass.cpp:467` — if `AddPlayerToRangeList(this)` is removed or guarded incorrectly, m_RangeList is empty for solo player and SendToRangeList loop never executes (test times out); (d) SendNotifyEmote field-copy regression at PlayerConnection.cpp:10381-10383 — if `response.Emote = emote` were hardcoded to a literal or read from `this->Emote` instead of the parameter, the sentinel echo would fail; (e) proxy default-case ForwardClientOpcode dropping 0x00A1 (0x00A1 NOT explicitly cased in proxy/ClientToServer_linux_stubs.cpp; relies on bottom-of-switch forward); (f) proxy SendClientPacketSequence guard tightening at `UDPProxyToClient_linux.cpp:568` — currently `opcode > 0x0000 && opcode < 0x0FFF` passes 0x00A2; a regression to e.g. `< 0x0080` would silently drop the reply; (g) SendOpcode header-width revert at PlayerConnection.cpp:127 — would corrupt the 0x2016 inner-tuple parser for every reply opcode; (h) SendToRangeList originator-exclusion regression — if a future refactor adds a `p != this` guard mirroring SendToVisibilityList's pattern at line 3134, the originator-receive path would break for solo players (test times out).
      Touches: `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/Opcodes/SectorTriggerEmoteTests.cs` (new — uses Pool[28] cli_test30 dedicated; 90s budget; 400-frame drain cap; xmldoc walks through the dispatcher case, two-line handler, SendNotifyEmote field-copy, SendToRangeList fan-out, SectorLogin self-add invariant, three-assertion structure, and 8 regression classes), `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/TestAccounts.cs` (Pool[28] += cli_test30 — 9_000_030), `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/Fixtures/seed.sql` (cli_test30 INSERT row, status=100), `tools/cli-client/src/CliClient.Core/Opcodes/OpcodeId.cs` (+0x00A1 TriggerEmote and +0x00A2 NotifyEmote in sorted position between 0x0088 PetitionStuck and 0x00A3 ClientChatRequest), `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/Coverage/TestedOpcodes.cs` (MinTestedCount 48→**50**; **+2** sorted-position entries for 0x00A1 TRIGGER_EMOTE and 0x00A2 NOTIFY_EMOTE inserted between 0x009F STARBASE_ROOM_CHANGE and 0x00A3 CLIENT_CHAT_REQUEST).

Per CLAUDE.md server-integrity rules: zero permissiveness added; TRIGGER_EMOTE is exactly what retail Win32 client emits when user triggers an emote in space or starbase (two int32_t LE fields, GameID + Emote code); the SendToRangeList self-receive fan-out is verbatim retail server behaviour (retail client has no client-side prediction so the originator always received its own emote-broadcast via this round-trip). Direct-reply pattern count after Wave 33: 8/33 waves (Waves 8 CLIENT_CHAT, 24 ITEM_STATE, 26 SKILL_ABILITY, 27 INVENTORY_MOVE, 28 SELECT_TALK_TREE, 30 MISSION_FORFEIT, 31 LOGOFF_REQUEST, 32 CLIENT_CHAT_REQUEST, 33 TRIGGER_EMOTE).       Server emit path — dispatcher case at `server/src/PlayerConnection.cpp:587` calls `HandleClientChatRequest(data)`. Handler at line 1648 casts to `ClientChatRequest*` and walks the variable-length string layout (lines 1659-1692): reads `string_length1` (=0 here), advances past the short, optionally memcpys length1 bytes (skipped — length1=0), reads `_string_length2` (=0), then `_string_length3` (=0), then the trailing `_data_size` int32 (=0). With all-zero lengths every `memcpy_s` is skipped and the walker fast-paths through. The switch on `request->type` at line 1695 hits case `CCR_FRIEND_STATUS_ONLY` (=28, PacketStructures.h:663) at line 1709, sets `m_StatusToFriendsOnly = true`, calls `SendClientChatEvent(CHEV_FRIEND_STATUS_ONLY, this)`, breaks. The long if-else chain at lines 1731-1803 is skipped because type=28 doesn't match any of CCE_SPEAK_LOCALLY/CCE_SPEAK_ON/CCE_ENTER_CHANNEL/CCE_EXIT_CHANNEL/CCE_INSERT_CHANNEL/CCR_LIST_CHANNELS/CCR_LIST_ALL_CHANNELS/CCR_SECTOR_LOGIN. `SendClientChatEvent` at PlayerConnection.cpp:10318-10369 for `Source==this`: IsIgnored(self) at line 10328 returns false (m_NumIgnore==0 short-circuits PlayerMisc.cpp:1445-1455), m_TellsFromFriendsOnly+PRIVATE_MESSAGE guard at 10336 doesn't apply (Type!=CHEV_PRIVATE_MESSAGE), GetPostFix at 10341 falls into AdminLevel<HELPER default branch at PlayerConnection.cpp:10294 which snprintfs `"%s", Name()` — no postfix, just the avatar firstName as LastName. Body assembled via AddData(Type=25)/AddData(0)/AddDataLS(LastName)×2/AddDataLS(NULL)×3/AddData((short)0)/AddData(0) at lines 10351-10360, then `SendOpcode(ENB_OPCODE_00A5_CLIENT_CHAT_EVENT, Packet, Index)` at line 10368 — per-client UDP queue add + SendPacketCache synchronous flush.
      Wire format — **18B canonical** with all three string-length shorts =0 and the trailing data-size int32 =0: `ClientChatRequest { int32 PlayerID; int32 type; short string_length1; char string1[1]; short _string_length2; char _string2[1]; short _string_length3; char _string3[1]; int32 _data_size; char _unknown_data[1]; } ATTRIB_PACKED` per `common/include/net7/PacketStructures.h:669-681`. The header struct is variable-length (`char stringN[1]` is "1-or-more bytes" per the upstream comment); collapsed to the empty-strings degenerate shape, the canonical wire is 4 (PlayerID) + 4 (type) + 2 (length1) + 2 (length2) + 2 (length3) + 4 (data_size) = 18 bytes. Handler reads PlayerID then immediately ignores it (the switch is on `request->type` only); writes `m_StatusToFriendsOnly` regardless of any other field state.
      Why this wave target — 0x00A3 CLIENT_CHAT_REQUEST is a +2 ratchet (both 0x00A3 and 0x00A5 previously uncovered), the CCR_FRIEND_STATUS_ONLY branch is deterministic on a fresh-starbase char (no spawned threads, no MESSAGE_STRING dependency on admin level, no channel/friend-list state assumption), and the handler is a clean switch-on-type so the wire payload directly selects the path. CCR_FRIEND_STATUS_ONLY (type=28) was chosen over CCR_LIST_CHANNELS (type=25) — that branch emits 0x001D MESSAGE_STRING via SendVaMessage("Request channel list") which is already covered (Waves 8/24/26/27/30 hit it). The type=28 path emits the previously-uncovered 0x00A5 CLIENT_CHAT_EVENT giving +2 ratchet instead of +1.
      Triaged and rejected alternatives during Wave 32 selection: **0x008D INCAPACITANCE_REQUEST** — initially promising (unconditional 0x0054 TALK_TREE emit) but Wave-29-era plan notes documented that the handler mutates static NPC template state and can SEGV via the avatar branch; per CLAUDE.md cannot patch server to make tests pass. **0x0079/0x007A/0x007E MANUFACTURE opcodes** — need SendAuxManu emit verification on fresh char (no manufacturing state); deferred. **0x00C0/0x00C5/0x00C9** — dispatcher case lookup needed; deferred. **Guild opcodes (HandleGuildLeaderAcceptClient/etc)** — DECLARED in PlayerClass.h but NOT DEFINED in any .cpp; would either link-fail or be no-ops; rejected.

Pool layout: Pool[0..8] = cli_test01..09, Pool[9..22] = cli_test11..24, Pool[23] = cli_test25 (Wave 28), Pool[24] = cli_test26 (Wave 29), Pool[25] = cli_test27 (Wave 30), Pool[26] = cli_test28 (Wave 31), Pool[27] = cli_test29 (this wave). cli_test10 still SKIPPED (StressTestClosed fixture at id=9_000_010).
      Regression coverage — (a) dispatcher mis-route at `PlayerConnection.cpp:587` — case label sits between 0x00A1 TRIGGER_EMOTE and 0x00B9 LOGOFF_REQUEST; swap with HandleTriggerEmote routes our 18B into SendNotifyEmote which fans out 0x00A2 NOTIFY_EMOTE to range-list peers only (test times out); swap with HandleLogoffRequest tears down sector before 0x00A5 emits (test sees surprise 0x00BA + connection drop); (b) ClientChatRequest layout regression at `PacketStructures.h:669-681` — long→int32 revert on PlayerID or type would shift type to offset 8 where our short(0) sits, switch reads type==0 → default → no reply → timeout; (c) variable-length string walker regression at `PlayerConnection.cpp:1659-1692` — short→int32 revert on string_length fields desyncs the walker; (d) CCR_FRIEND_STATUS_ONLY case-label drift — constant must match PacketStructures.h:663=28; (e) SendClientChatEvent guards firing on CHEV_FRIEND_STATUS_ONLY — IsIgnored(self) returning true → early return at line 10334, or m_TellsFromFriendsOnly+PRIVATE_MESSAGE guard widening → emits 0x00A4 CLIENT_CHAT_ERROR instead; (f) AddData<long> width revert at `PacketMethods.h:38-42` (Phase K Wave 11 fix class) — Type would land as int32 but occupy 8 bytes, body[0..3] anchor still passes but body[4..7] would overlap the (0) spacer slot; (g) proxy default-case ForwardClientOpcode dropping 0x00A3 (0x00A3 NOT explicitly cased in proxy/ClientToServer_linux_stubs.cpp; relies on bottom-of-switch forward); (h) proxy SendClientPacketSequence guard tightening at `UDPProxyToClient_linux.cpp:568` — currently `opcode > 0x0000 && opcode < 0x0FFF` passes 0x00A5; a regression to e.g. `< 0x0080` would silently drop the reply.

- [x] Wave 31 (2026-05-25): 0x00B9 LOGOFF_REQUEST → 0x00BA LOGOFF_CONFIRMATION **direct-reply round-trip** — exercises HandleLogoffRequest's unconditional emit path: the handler runs LeaveGroup → SendLogoffConfirmation → DropPlayerFromGalaxy in strict order, and `SendLogoffConfirmation` unconditionally emits a zero-body 0x00BA LOGOFF_CONFIRMATION via per-client SendOpcode + SendPacketCache (the synchronous flush ensures the reply lands on the wire BEFORE the post-emit DropPlayerFromGalaxy teardown runs). First direct-reply wave with payload-field-ignored handler (the LogoffRequest* cast is commented out at server/src/PlayerConnection.cpp:7711) — vacuously satisfies Wave 30's "read past every CALL + identify payload-selected branch" methodology refinement because there IS no payload-selected branch.
      Status: done — `SectorLogoffRequestTests.LogoffRequest_AllZeroPayload_ReceivesLogoffConfirmationWithEmptyBody` passes in isolation (1/1, **7s**). Coverage ratchet test verifies **46/207 (22.2%)**, all 6 CoverageRatchetTests pass. Build clean (0 warnings, 0 errors).
      Server emit path — `Player::HandleLogoffRequest` at `server/src/PlayerConnection.cpp:7709-7720` runs three statements in strict order: (1) `g_ServerMgr->m_PlayerMgr.LeaveGroup(GroupID(), GameID())` — for a fresh char with `GroupID() == -1`, `PlayerManager::LeaveGroup` at `server/src/GroupManager.cpp:549` calls `GetGroupFromID(-1)` which returns NULL via the early `if (GroupID == -1) return NULL;` guard at `GroupManager.cpp:39` (same guard Wave 22 OPTION test relies on). The handler then falls into the null-group branch which calls `SendEmptyGroupAux(PlayerID)` — for a fresh char with `GroupID == -1` the `SendVaMessage("You have left the group")` is gated off, the set-to-default operations on `GroupInfo` are idempotent (already-cleared state), and the trailing `SendAuxPlayer()` emits a 0x0061 AUX_PLAYER only if `HasDiff()` is true (drain loop tolerates the interleaved frame either way). (2) `SendLogoffConfirmation()` at `PlayerConnection.cpp:7746-7751` calls `SendOpcode(0x00BA, 0, 0)` + `SendPacketCache()` for the unconditional zero-body reply. SendPacketCache at PlayerConnection.cpp:235-246 calls `m_UDPConnection->SendResponse` synchronously — the 0x00BA goes out over the UDP wire BEFORE the next statement runs. (3) `g_ServerMgr->m_PlayerMgr.DropPlayerFromGalaxy(this)` — the post-emit teardown sequence (DropPlayerFromSector → SetRemove → SaveLogout → ReleasePlayerNode → SetActive(false) → UnSetIndex → SetLoginStage(-1) per `PlayerManager.cpp:119-140`) is asynchronous-to-emit, so the test reliably sees the reply before any teardown side-effect could race. SaveLogout (`PlayerSaves.cpp:1638`) queues a SAVE_CODE_LOGOUT message but the DB row persists, so the global-plane `DeleteCreatedCharacterAsync` cleanup at the test's tail still works (the GLOBAL connection is a separate UDP channel on UDP 3810, independent of the now-dropped sector player).
      Wire format — **8B canonical** with both fields ignored: `LogoffRequest { int32 PlayerID; int32 LogOutType }` per `common/include/net7/PacketStructures.h:583`. Handler's `LogoffRequest * request = (LogoffRequest *) data;` cast at `PlayerConnection.cpp:7711` is COMMENTED OUT — the handler never reads either field, so byte-order is moot. All-zero bytes are the safest payload.
      Why this wave target — 0x00B9 LOGOFF_REQUEST is the cleanest remaining direct-reply candidate: the handler emits ONE deterministic frame (0x00BA, length 0) on EVERY call, with no payload-driven branching to qualify. Triaged and rejected alternatives: **0x009B WARP** — `HandleWarp` at PlayerConnection.cpp:1876 calls `CheckForInstalls` which returns TRUE for a fresh char (no items installed) so the direct-reply branch is not taken; **0x0098 GALAXY_MAP_REQUEST** — reply opcode 0x2011 sits OUTSIDE the proxy's `opcode > 0x0000 && opcode < 0x0FFF` inner-tuple forwarding guard at `proxy/UDPProxyToClient_linux.cpp:568`, so the reply would be silently dropped; **0x00BC CTA_REQUEST** — `HandleCTARequest` at PlayerConnection.cpp:7723 has a Wave-12-class `*((long*) &CTAResponse[N])` stack-clobber bug (writes 8B into a 9B array, spills 4 bytes past the buffer on Linux x86_64) which could SEGV on return; **0x005D EQUIP_USE** — survival probe only, less interesting than LOGOFF's direct-reply assertion. **First direct-reply wave whose reply opcode sits in the 0x00BA-class proxy-forwarded band** (0x0086 MISSION_FORFEIT and 0x0088 PETITION_STUCK were 0x0086/0x0088 client→server only; here we cover the 0x00B9/0x00BA pair end-to-end).
      Why a positive reply assertion rather than survival probe — 0x00BA LOGOFF_CONFIRMATION is unconditional (every call emits exactly one 0x00BA frame regardless of state). A survival probe via REQUEST_TIME wouldn't work here because DropPlayerFromGalaxy runs after the emit and tears down the sector Player — subsequent REQUEST_TIME would arrive at a Player node that's been released back to the pool, and the CLIENT_SET_TIME reply path goes through the now-inactive player. The direct-reply assertion is strictly stronger: we pin the exact reply opcode (0x00BA) and the exact body length (0).
      Why all-zero payload — handler never reads either field (commented-out cast). All-zero bytes: no field-order assumptions, no byte-order assumptions, no parser side-effects.
      Pool growth — `cli_test28` (id=9_000_028) added to `TestAccounts.Pool[26]` with matching `seed.sql` row at status=100. Pool layout: Pool[0..8] = cli_test01..09, Pool[9..22] = cli_test11..24, Pool[23] = cli_test25 (Wave 28), Pool[24] = cli_test26 (Wave 29), Pool[25] = cli_test27 (Wave 30), Pool[26] = cli_test28 (this wave). cli_test10 still SKIPPED (StressTestClosed fixture at id=9_000_010).
      Regression coverage — (a) dispatcher mis-route at `PlayerConnection.cpp:591` — case label sits between 0x0088 PETITION_STUCK and 0x00BC CTA_REQUEST; a copy-paste swap with HandleCTARequest would route 8B into that handler's `*((long*) &CTAResponse[N])` writes (4 bytes past the 9B stack buffer on Linux x86_64) and SEGV on return → connection drops → no 0x00BA → test times out; (b) proxy 0x00B9 fall-through regression at `proxy/ClientToServer_linux_stubs.cpp:498-507` — the case sets g_LoggedIn=true then breaks, falling through to bottom-of-switch ForwardClientOpcode; a regression that returned early would mean the server never receives 0x00B9 → no 0x00BA → test times out; (c) SendLogoffConfirmation length-arg flip at `PlayerConnection.cpp:7749` (a regression from `0` to `sizeof(LogoffRequest)` or similar would emit a non-empty body — test pins payload length to exactly 0); (d) SendOpcode header-width revert at `PlayerConnection.cpp:127` (Phase K sizeof(int32_t) fix; revert would shift every reply opcode in the 0x2016 PACKET_SEQUENCE inner-tuple parser by 4 bytes — 0x00BA would be unparseable, test times out); (e) proxy SendClientPacketSequence guard tightening at `UDPProxyToClient_linux.cpp:568` (`opcode > 0x0000 && opcode < 0x0FFF` currently passes 0x00BA; a regression to e.g. `< 0x00BA` would silently drop the reply); (f) pre-emit LeaveGroup crash if `GetGroupFromID(-1)` early-return guard at `GroupManager.cpp:39` regresses (would walk m_GroupList from a stale head and crash before the 0x00BA emit); (g) DropPlayerFromGalaxy running synchronously and tearing down before the UDP queue flushes — currently safe because SendPacketCache synchronously calls m_UDPConnection->SendResponse before DropPlayerFromGalaxy runs; a refactor that made the UDP write asynchronous would silently break this.
      Touches: `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/Opcodes/SectorLogoffRequestTests.cs` (new — uses Pool[26] cli_test28 dedicated; 90s budget; 400-frame drain cap; xmldoc walks through HandleLogoffRequest's three-statement sequence, the pre-emit LeaveGroup safety chain, the SendPacketCache synchronous-flush ordering vs DropPlayerFromGalaxy teardown, and 7 regression classes including the dispatcher-mis-route trap into HandleCTARequest's sizeof(long) stack-clobber bug), `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/TestAccounts.cs` (Pool[26] += cli_test28 — 9_000_028), `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/Fixtures/seed.sql` (cli_test28 INSERT row, status=100), `tools/cli-client/src/CliClient.Core/Opcodes/OpcodeId.cs` (+0x00B9 LogoffRequest and +0x00BA LogoffConfirmation in sorted position between 0x0088 PetitionStuck and 0x006D GlobalConnect — opcode value order, not declaration order), `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/Coverage/TestedOpcodes.cs` (MinTestedCount 44→**46**; **+2** sorted-position entries for 0x00B9 LOGOFF_REQUEST and 0x00BA LOGOFF_CONFIRMATION inserted between 0x009F STARBASE_ROOM_CHANGE and 0x2020 LOGIN_STAGE_S_C).

- [x] Wave 30 (2026-05-25): 0x0086 MISSION_FORFEIT **direct-reply round-trip** — exercises HandleMissionForfeit's empty-mission-slot path which falls into MissionDismiss's else-branch and emits the "This mission is non forfeitable." reply as a 0x001D MESSAGE_STRING. Same defensive-error-string direct-reply shape as Waves 24 (ITEM_STATE UNRECOGNISED), 26 (SKILL_ABILITY not-yet-working), and 27 (INVENTORY_MOVE UNRECOGNISED).
      Status: done — `SectorMissionForfeitTests.MissionForfeit_EmptySlotZero_ReceivesNonForfeitableErrorString` passes in isolation (1/1, **7s**). Coverage ratchet test verifies **44/207 (21.3%)**, all 6 CoverageRatchetTests pass. Build clean (0 warnings, 0 errors).
      Server emit path — Player::HandleMissionForfeit at `server/src/PlayerConnection.cpp:11009` ntohl-decodes both fields then calls `MissionDismiss(MissionID, true)` if `MissionID < 12`. Player::MissionDismiss at `server/src/PlayerMissions.cpp:1616` accesses `m_PlayerIndex.Missions.Mission[mission_slot]` and checks `GetIsForfeitable()`. For a fresh starbase character `AuxMission::Init` (server/src/AuxClasses/AuxMission.h:85) zero-initialised `Data->IsForfeitable=0`, so with `forfeit_pressed=true` the if-condition `(!true || GetIsForfeitable())` reduces to `(false || false) = false` and the else-branch at PlayerMissions.cpp:1630 fires `SendVaMessageC(17, "This mission is non forfeitable.")`. SendVaMessageC → SendMessageString → SendOpcode(0x001D) per-client UDP queue (same path Wave 27's UNRECOGNISED INVENTORY MOVE rode). No SendToSector fan-out, no login-stage race.
      Wave-29 candidate rejection of 0x0086 was over-cautious — Wave 29's plans entry rejected MISSION_FORFEIT with "cautious — fresh-char mission array deref" because the if-branch calls RemoveMission(0) which could SEGV on a zero-initialised mission slot. But the cautious rejection missed that with `forfeit_pressed=true` the else-branch (SendVaMessageC) is taken instead — the array is accessed only via `&Missions.Mission[0]` (address-of into a 12-element packed struct, always valid) and `GetIsForfeitable()` (a trivial accessor returning `Data->IsForfeitable` which AuxMission::Init zeroed). RemoveMission is NOT called on the empty-slot path; the false-branch reply is. **Methodology refinement** — re-evaluating Wave 29's rejection list with the Wave 29 "read past first SendOpcode" methodology applied at finer grain: read past every CALL too, not just past every SendOpcode. Each call's post-conditions matter.
      Wire format — **8B big-endian**: `MissionDismissal { int32 PlayerID; int32 MissionID }` from `common/include/net7/PacketStructures.h:1001`. Handler ntohl-decodes both fields at `PlayerConnection.cpp:11012-11013` — wire is NETWORK byte order (matches Wave 27's per-handler grep for `ntohl(`). Sending all-zeros means byte-order is fixed-point on this exact payload, but a future variant with MissionID=1 (BE) would catch a missing ntohl by reading 0x01000000 host-LE → outside [0..12) → silent rejection.
      Why MissionID=0 and not a higher in-range value — slot 0 is the canonical "first mission slot" and the AuxMissions array (12 slots, `AuxClasses/AuxMissions.h:74`) was zero-initialised when Player constructed. Any slot in [0..12) on a fresh character would work, but slot 0 is the easiest to reason about (it's the slot the retail Win32 client also visits first when the user opens the mission tracker — empty UI rows aren't sent so the first non-empty slot stays 0).
      Why MISSION_FORFEIT not MISSION_DISMISSAL — sibling 0x0087 MISSION_DISMISSAL calls `MissionDismiss(MissionID, false)`. With `forfeit_pressed=false` the if-condition `(!false || GetIsForfeitable())` reduces to `(true || false) = true` and fires `RemoveMission(mission_slot)` instead of the SendVaMessageC reply. RemoveMission on an empty slot has wider side-effect surface (may touch `m_AssignedMissions` / emit mission-update auxes / trigger AuxPlayer refresh downstream); MISSION_FORFEIT's else-branch is the strictly cleaner direct-reply candidate.
      Pool growth — `cli_test27` (id=9_000_027) added to `TestAccounts.Pool[25]` with matching `seed.sql` row at status=100. Pool layout: Pool[0..8] = cli_test01..09, Pool[9..22] = cli_test11..24, Pool[23] = cli_test25 (Wave 28), Pool[24] = cli_test26 (Wave 29), Pool[25] = cli_test27 (this wave). cli_test10 still SKIPPED (StressTestClosed fixture at id=9_000_010).
      Regression coverage — (a) PacketStructures.h:1001 MissionDismissal long-revert (currently 2× int32_t = 8B canonical; long widening grows the struct and MissionID reads past wire end into stack garbage, likely outside [0..12) → silent rejection → test times out); (b) dispatcher mis-route at `PlayerConnection.cpp:551` — case label sits between 0x0084 RECUSTOMIZE_AVATAR_DONE and 0x0087 MISSION_DISMISSAL; a copy-paste swap with HandleMissionDismissal would call MissionDismiss(0, false) and hit RemoveMission instead of SendVaMessageC; (c) proxy default-case ForwardClientOpcode dropping 0x0086 (NOT in proxy/ClientToServer_linux_stubs.cpp:387-518 — falls through to bottom-of-switch); (d) HandleMissionForfeit `[0..12)` guard removal at `PlayerConnection.cpp:11015`; (e) AuxMission::Init `IsForfeitable=0` default flip at `AuxMission.h:85` — if the initial-state default flips to true, the if-branch fires RemoveMission instead and the test times out; (f) SendVaMessageC/SendMessageString format-string regression (test pins on substring "non forfeitable" — tolerates punctuation tweaks; full literal change surfaces here); (g) 0x001D MESSAGE_STRING fan-out path width regression at `PlayerConnection.cpp:127`.
      Touches: `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/Opcodes/SectorMissionForfeitTests.cs` (new — uses Pool[25] cli_test27 dedicated; 90s budget; 400-frame drain cap; xmldoc walks through MissionDismiss's nested if/else, justifies MissionID=0 + `forfeit_pressed=true` selection, and 7 regression classes including the dispatcher-mis-route swap with sibling HandleMissionDismissal), `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/TestAccounts.cs` (Pool[25] += cli_test27 — 9_000_027), `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/Fixtures/seed.sql` (cli_test27 INSERT row, status=100), `tools/cli-client/src/CliClient.Core/Opcodes/OpcodeId.cs` (+0x0086 MissionForfeit in sorted position between 0x005F AvatarEmoteResponse and 0x0088 PetitionStuck), `tests/integration/CliClient.IntegrationTests/Coverage/TestedOpcodes.cs` (MinTestedCount 43→**44**; sorted-position entry for 0x0086 MISSION_FORFEIT inserted between 0x0075 GLOBAL_ERROR and 0x0088 PETITION_STUCK).
      Notes: Phase K coverage ratchet 43/207 → **44/207 (21.3%)**. Per CLAUDE.md server-integrity rules: zero permissiveness added; MISSION_FORFEIT with MissionID=0 from a fresh-starbase character is exactly what the retail Win32 client emits when user clicks the Forfeit button on the first row of the mission tracker; the "This mission is non forfeitable." reply is the verbatim retail server response when IsForfeitable is false. Next-Phase-K targets: re-evaluating Wave 29's rejection list more carefully under the "read past every call" refinement: 0x009B WARP (HandleWarp may have a guard that early-returns on a docked starbase character — worth verifying), 0x005D EQUIP_USE (only crashes on m_Equip[InvSlot=valid] — sending InvSlot out-of-range could hit an early-return), 0x0079/0x007A MANUFACTURE opcodes (need to check the SendAuxManu path for fresh-char early-returns). The methodology is "pick one, trace dispatcher, find safe branch, read every callee to confirm no post-emit crash, write test."
